Risk MEDIUM (wire-format + server handler + libsystem_kernel/mldr in lockstep; checkin is on the hot fork/thread path). RED->GREEN + A/B + e2e. Build from darling-build, integrate via west. Nothing pushed. Independent of perf #3 (recv-spin) and compounds with it. See dar-perf3-recv-adaptive-spin.

title: perf #6: PROFILE — real build wall-clock breakdown (server idle, 57% RPC-wait, 34% reaping)
description: DONE (the measurement). Profiled a real fork/exec-heavy Darling build (make -j8, 1500 small -O2 compiles) sampling guest thread wchan + server state every 0.3s (601 samples). KEY FINDING that reframes the whole perf epic: guest spends <1% of time RUNNING; the rest is WAITING, and the darlingserver worker is busy 0% of samples (server is NOT the bottleneck -> server multithread perf#2/#2d are NOT in the hot path for this workload). Breakdown of where guest threads block (601 samples): __skb_wait_for_more_packets 57% (waiting on an RPC reply from the server), ZOMBIE 21% + do_wait 13% + sigsuspend 5% = ~39% on child-reaping/wait (children exited but parent hasn't reaped them; make/sh blocked in wait()/SIGCHLD), ep_poll 2%, RUNNING <1%. CONCLUSION: the big wins are (A) generalize perf#3's adaptive recv-spin from checkin-only to ALL hot RPCs (attacks the 57%), and (B) speed up the SIGCHLD-delivery + wait4 reaping path (attacks the ~39%). Together ~91% of real wall-clock. perf#1/#2b/#3 only shaved micro-latency off individual checkins, which is why the real-build wall-clock barely moved. Harness ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/profile-build.sh (wchan histogram + server busy-fraction). Earlier perf#1-3 numbers stand as latency wins but do not move end-to-end build time materially.
close_reason: Completed: real build wall-clock profile was captured and conclusions recorded; follow-up work is tracked in later perf beads.

title: perf #7: generalize adaptive recv-spin to ALL hot RPCs (attacks 57% __skb_wait)
description: From the perf#6 profile (dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.13): 57% of guest wall-clock is spent in __skb_wait_for_more_packets -- guest threads SLEEPING while waiting for an RPC reply from the server. perf#3 (mldr-recv-adaptive-spin) already does a bounded non-blocking recvmsg spin before sleeping, BUT it was scoped to the checkin reply path only (dserver_rpc_hooks_receive_message gated by DARLING_PERF3_RECVSPIN). This task: confirm the spin is actually applied to ALL synchronous RPC replies (every guest syscall that round-trips to darlingserver), not just checkin -- and if not, generalize it. Likely already partially covered since the hook is shared; needs verification + measurement of which RPC numbers dominate the __skb_wait time (instrument: bucket __skb_wait by the pending call number). Low-medium risk (mldr client-side, env-gated, bounded spin -> never busy-waits unboundedly). Symmetric to perf#1 (creator-side wait) and perf#3 (checkin recv). Expected: large fraction of the 57%. RED->GREEN: ctx-switches/round-trip gate like perf#3's test. Depends on perf#6 finding.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: perf#9 done (.16): per-call breakdown of a real build shows top 6 RPCs = 62% of traffic, all sub-16us server-side per-thread/process self-traps (mach_reply_port, task_self_trap, kprintf, pthread_canceled, host_self_trap, thread_self_trap). recv-spin (perf#3) is ALREADY generic and already covers them. So perf#7 is NOT 'add spin to more call sites'. Refocus: (A) low-risk = confirm/tune the 512-poll spin budget covers these calls' reply-latency under load (A/B on ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/percall-build.sh). (B) bigger structural win = CACHE the STABLE self-trap results guest-side (task/host/thread self port are process/thread-stable) so they never RPC at all -> eliminates ~28% of all RPCs outright. Do NOT spin fork_wait_for_child (correct blocking).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DONE (fix committed+packaged, NOT pushed). The prior comment was WRONG: perf#3's recv-spin only ever landed in mldr's copy of dserver-rpc-defs.h. The dylib's copy (used by every guest build proc: clang/make/sh for mach_msg_overwrite 19% + mach_reply_port 8.7% + mach_port_deallocate 8.4% + self-traps) did a plain BLOCKING recvmsg with NO spin -> the whole 28%+ was untouched.

FIX: ported the bounded LINUX_MSG_DONTWAIT spin-then-block into the dylib hook (libc-free: raw syscall + ia32_pause; budget=compile-time DARLING_GUEST_RECVSPIN default 512; CMake cache var for A/B, =0 disables). Hook compiles into BOTH dylib AND dyld -> both rebuilt+deployed. RED->GREEN tests/dylib_recv_adaptive_spin_test.sh committed WITH the fix. Branch perf/generalize-recv-spin-guest @1169ebe (xnu, base 0984b9b). Packaged xnu/generalize-recv-spin-guest.patch, west patch verify=70 GREEN.

Correctness: clean boot + 200/200 fork-heavy build GREEN with spin-ON dylib e5cd6b12+dyld 025aa772.

WALL-CLOCK A/B INCONCLUSIVE + blocked by a SEPARATE env bug found while validating: the prefix darlingserver had regressed to perf-lineage 716f958d which LACKS the dar-6x4 checkin-suspend crash fix 5e5d688 -> it SEGFAULTS under fork storms (dmesg: repeated 'darlingserver segfault in libc.so.6'; log: 'Uncaught exception ... checkin: Attempt to suspend thread other than current thread'), wedging the build at a partial .o count. My recv-spin is guest-side and CANNOT crash the server.

NEW SUB-FINDING (needs its own bead): the perf-server lineage (NO_REPLY/metrics) and the checkin-crash-fix 5e5d688 were never merged into ONE server -> no single server has both protocol+crashfix. The integration server 831a09d5 has the crashfix but NOT NO_REPLY, so it desyncs against the perf-lineage dylib/mldr (server log: 'Client-pushed reply overwriting existing saved reply'). Needs a server integration pass before fork-heavy e2e perf can be measured.

HARNESS: old percall-build.sh/perf7-wall-ab.sh misreported server crashes as build TIMEOUT (inferred completion from a guest .done file + darling shell exit, both unreliable). New ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/build-correctness.sh detects completion HOST-SIDE by polling the on-disk .o count, separates boot from build, and adds a SERVER-ALIVE ground-truth signal (pgrep darlingserver + stat-socket reachability) so a server segfault is reported as SERVER_CRASHED, not a false TIMEOUT.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: e2e wall-clock A/B re-run on the dar-l8k-FIXED lineage (server a13ec2ae, dylib ON=e5cd6b12 / OFF=c706f699, dyld ON=025aa772 / OFF=79814e5e). Harness recvspin-ab-robust.sh = correct dual-component deploy (dylib x3 + dyld x2 per arm) + build-correctness.sh-style detection (host-measured compile-phase wall from first .o to .o==NFILES; .done no longer trusted for timing -- kills the false-TIMEOUT noise that made the prior A/B inconclusive). Workload 500 files, -j8, 3 interleaved reps. RESULT: OFF {3.0,4.0}s guest_make, ON {3.0,3.0,3.0}s; both arms service ~38.4k RPCs. recv-spin ON vs OFF is a WASH at wall-clock (within the 1s poll granularity) -- now EXPLAINED not just noisy: on an uncontended host the 500-file build finishes in ~3s and recv-spin's per-RPC microsecond savings don't surface in a 3s wall. Spin is a CPU-vs-latency trade that only pays under saturation. (One OFF rep was a degenerate boot: 97/500 .o, 9241 RPCs, session barely executed -- discarded, not a crash, not a recv-spin effect.) Recv-spin stays correctness-validated (200/200) + wall-neutral => ships safe-but-not-proven-faster. The headline: 0/6 SERVER_CRASHED across fresh fork-storm boots -- dar-l8k UAF fix confirmed e2e (was ~1-in-3-6 pre-fix).

title: perf #8: speed up child reaping path — SIGCHLD delivery + wait4 (attacks ~39% zombie/do_wait)
description: From the perf#6 profile (dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.13): ~39% of guest wall-clock is child-reaping latency -- ZOMBIE 21% (children exited but not yet reaped) + do_wait 13% (parent blocked in wait()) + sigsuspend 5% (waiting for SIGCHLD). In a make -jN build the parent (make/sh) forks a compiler, then sleeps until the child exits AND the SIGCHLD is delivered AND wait4 returns. The big zombie backlog (2776/601 samples) shows a real lag between child exit and parent reap. This is the SAME subsystem as the dar-gwn.1.8 lost-reaping bug, but here it's LATENCY not loss. Investigate: (1) how fast does darlingserver deliver SIGCHLD to the parent microthread after a child exits, (2) is the wait4 RPC round-trip itself slow / does it sleep when the child is already a zombie, (3) can wait4 fast-path return immediately for an already-exited child without a full RPC sleep (adaptive spin / cached exit status). Medium-high risk (signal + wait semantics must stay macOS-faithful). Expected: large fraction of the ~39%. RED->GREEN: a fork+exit+wait storm measuring reap latency per child. Depends on perf#6 finding.
close_reason: No addressable target: reaping/wait4 is already optimal (~5us/child, native Linux __NR_wait4, no RPC; already-zombie wait4 returns in 23-53us). The perf#6 '39% reaping' was a wchan-sampling illusion -- the parent is idle-waiting while children are slow to CHECK IN (~2.85ms/fork). Real lever re-scoped to fork-checkin; see new bead. Full diagnosis in bead comment + memory dar-perf8-reaping-not-bottleneck.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DIAGNOSIS (Opus 2026-06-28, on the dar-l8k-fixed lineage, uncontended host): perf#8's premise is EMPIRICALLY FALSE -- the reaping/wait4 path is NOT a bottleneck and there is no fast-path to add.

CODE: guest wait4 (libsystem_kernel .../bsd/impl/process/wait4.c:36) is a DIRECT Linux __NR_wait4 -- NO darlingserver RPC. SIGCHLD is explicitly EXCLUDED from sigexc routing (sigexc.c:219 'continue' on LINUX_SIGCHLD) and installed natively via __NR_rt_sigaction (sigaction.c:52-69). Guest fork (fork.c:27/29) is native clone|SIGCHLD producing a real Linux child. So fork->exit->SIGCHLD->wait4 is 100% native Linux; darlingserver is not in the reap loop. (process.cpp:449 waitForChildAfterFork tracks fork-CHECKIN, not child exit/reap.)

MEASURED (guest microbenchmarks, ~/work reap-probe.c + fork-decomp.c, 200 children x2 runs):
- already-zombie wait4: 23-53 us (instant; the bead's hypothesis (3) 'wait4 sleeps on a full RPC for an already-exited child' is DISPROVEN).
- reap-all 200 children: ~1000-1185 us total, ~5 us/child (p50 4.5-5.7, p99 23, max 42). Reaping is NEGLIGIBLE.
- fork() return latency: p50 ~2.85 MILLISECONDS/fork, min 2.5ms, max 4ms -- with children that do _exit(0) immediately, so ~99.6% of the fork-cycle is the FORK MECHANISM (native clone + the child's synchronous dserver_rpc_checkin(true) at fork.c:61 + the parent's waitForChildAfterFork block), NOT reaping.

CONCLUSION: the '39% reaping' in the perf#6 profile is a wchan-SAMPLING ILLUSION -- the parent (make/sh) shows up in do_wait/ZOMBIE/sigsuspend because it is CORRECTLY idle-waiting for children, but the children are slow to CHECK IN AND RUN (~2.85ms/fork), not slow to be REAPED. wait4 already returns instantly for zombies; there is no wait4/SIGCHLD fast-path with a real target.

RE-SCOPE: the addressable lever is the ~2.85ms per-fork CHECKIN cost (child dserver_rpc_checkin + parent fork_wait_for_child, perf#9 measured fork_wait_for_child=257us so the bulk is the child-side checkin+socket/space refresh running serially before the parent unblocks). That is perf#1's territory (adaptive wait, [[dar-perf1-thread-create-adaptive-wait]]) generalized to the FORK path, NOT a reaping fix. Recommend: close perf#8 as 'no target -- reaping already optimal' and refocus on fork-checkin cost. Evidence binaries/probes in ~/work/dar-6x4-repro-style guest home.

title: perf #9: decompose the 57% __skb_wait by RPC number (which calls dominate)
description: Sharpen the perf#6 finding before committing to perf#7/#8 effort: instrument mldr (or the server) to attribute __skb_wait time to the PENDING RPC call number, so we know whether the 57% RPC-wait is dominated by a few hot calls (e.g. vchroot path ops, mmap, stat, read) or spread thin. Cheap diagnostic (per-call counter + time-in-recv histogram keyed by call number). Prevents optimizing the wrong RPC. Output feeds perf#7 scope. Low risk (instrumentation only). Depends on perf#6.
close_reason: Diagnostic delivered: per-call RPC breakdown built (RED->GREEN test), live-validated on a real build, finding documented and feeds perf#7 refocus.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DONE 2026-06-28 (Opus), NOT pushed. Server-side per-call-number breakdown (cleaner than instrumenting mldr recv): Metrics gets lock-free per-callnum count+log2-latency table (metrics.hpp), thread.cpp records next to rpc_latency, snapshotJSON emits sparse per_call object. RED->GREEN test per_call_metrics_test.cpp committed WITH impl. Branch perf/per-call-rpc-metrics @4c528d9 (base=dar-6x4 fix 5e5d688). Built in darling-build (install-prefix=darling-prefix; NOTE darling-dev/darling/build tree has WRONG /usr/local prefix -> server fails 'Cannot mount overlay'), deployed md5 ef36c9c1, live-validated.

FINDING (real make -j8, 500 files, 46777 RPCs, ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/PERF9-FINDING.txt): server service time tiny everywhere (p50 4-16us) -> confirms perf#6 the 57% recvmsg-wait is WAKEUP latency not server work. Top 6 calls=62% of all RPCs, ALL sub-16us: mach_reply_port 13.2%, task_self_trap 13.2%, kprintf 10.9%, pthread_canceled 9.9%, host_self_trap 8.8%, thread_self_trap 6.6%. Per-thread/process traps returning STABLE values. fork_wait_for_child only legit-slow (p50 256us max 1.1ms = correct parent-blocks-on-child, do NOT spin).

perf#7 REFOCUS: recv-spin already generic+covers these. Bigger win=CACHE/ELIMINATE the stable self-traps guest-side so they never RPC, not just spin faster.

title: perf #10: batch the ~15 serial per-process startup RPCs into one round-trip
description: perf#9 showed ~40% of build RPCs are once-per-process startup calls (checkin, set_executable_path, set_dyld_info, get_tracer, mldr_path, started_suspended, uidgid, set_thread_handles, vchroot_path...) each a SEPARATE synchronous round-trip on the process-launch critical path -> directly feeds the 57% recvmsg-wait (perf#6). Coalesce the deterministic startup sequence into ONE batch RPC (or pipeline them) so a process pays 1 sleep instead of ~15. Med-high risk: new RPC protocol + ordering/dependency analysis. RED->GREEN + live A/B on percall-build.sh. Depends on perf#9.
close_reason: NO_REPLY fire-and-forget mechanism implemented + RED->GREEN test + applied to 2 safe setters; full matched set boots and builds correctly. set_thread_handles left synchronous (sensitive).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: REFINED approach after reading the code: the ~15 startup RPCs are NOT a contiguous sequence -- they fire lazily from independent subsystems (mldr: checkin/vchroot_path/set_dyld_info/set_executable_path; sigexc: started_suspended/get_tracer; pthread: set_thread_handles; execve: mldr_path; getuid: uidgid). Fusing all into one batch = huge cross-subsystem refactor + ordering risk. BIGGER + SAFER lever found: many are pure SETTERS with EMPTY reply (set_dyld_info, set_executable_path, set_thread_handles, set_tracer, stop_after_exec) -- the guest does a full synchronous round-trip (send + ~200us recvmsg sleep) only to read a status code it does nothing with. Make them FIRE-AND-FORGET (new NO_REPLY rpc flag): guest sends + returns immediately (skip recv block); server processCall still runs but _sendReply becomes a no-op (so no stray datagram desyncs the next sync reply). Per-thread datagrams stay ordered server-side, so a NO_REPLY setter is always processed before any later sync RPC that depends on it. Eliminates ~1 recvmsg sleep per setter call (~4-5K/build) with no batching/refactor + no deallocate-contract hazard. First cut = the 3 safe setters (set_dyld_info, set_executable_path, set_thread_handles); keep checkin + exec-critical calls synchronous. RED->GREEN + live A/B.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DONE 2026-06-28 (Opus), NOT pushed. Implemented NO_REPLY rpc-generator flag (fire-and-forget): client wrapper sends + returns 0 immediately (skips recv block); server _sendReply is a no-op (no stray datagram). Applied to set_dyld_info + set_executable_path (mldr-side pure metadata). set_thread_handles TRIED but REVERTED (early pthread init too sensitive -> guest hung). RED->GREEN test no_reply_rpc_test.sh committed with the mechanism. Branch perf/no-reply-fire-and-forget-rpcs @96298b1 (base perf#9 4c528d9).

CRITICAL GOTCHA (cost ~1h, now documented in commit): mldr STATICALLY links the generated rpc wrappers (mldr_dserver_rpc/rpc.c). A NO_REPLY change requires rebuilding ALL THREE consumers together -- mldr, libsystem_kernel.dylib, darlingserver -- else a stale mldr keeps waiting for a reply the new server no longer sends -> guest launchd hangs in recvmsg, shellspawn never starts ('Error connecting to shellspawn'). Proven by /proc/<launchd>/stack = __skb_wait_for_more_packets. Also: deploy dylib to ALL 3 copies (DPREFIX upper + darling-prefix/usr + darling-prefix/libexec/darling/usr).

VALIDATED: full matched set (server 716f958d, mldr bb642bf8, dylib c958a92f) boots clean + real make -j8 build 500/500. set_dyld_info/set_executable_path (1027 each) no longer block the guest on a reply (~2054 recvmsg sleeps/build eliminated). Server still services them (metric counts server-side processing; guest-sleep elimination is invisible to server counters). Wall-clock A/B inconclusive (boot-noise dominated, as perf#6 predicted for a ~few-% RPC change). Mechanism is reusable for future safe setters.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RETROSPECTIVE PROOF 2026-06-29 (Opus, via perf#15/.27): the original 'batch all ~15 startup RPCs into one round-trip' title is now MEASURED to be IMPOSSIBLE, which vindicates the pivot to NO_REPLY done here. perf#15 added a per-RPC batchability trace (gap_before = CPU between prior reply and this send). For the libSystem-init startup RPC chain: EVERY adjacent pair has 20-445us of CPU between them, 0 back-to-back, 0% batchable -- the startup RPCs form a SEQUENTIAL data-dependent Mach-bootstrap chain (task_self -> reply_port -> host_self -> msg_overwrite consumes both, ...). So a single batch RPC could never have worked; NO_REPLY (which removes the reply-wait WITHOUT reordering) was the correct and only safe coalescing. This bead stays CLOSED/correct. See dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.27 for the full measurement.

title: perf #7: cache immutable task_self_trap guest-side (done)
description: Cache the immutable task self port guest-side so task_self_trap stops doing an RPC on every call (reset on fork via mach_init_doit). Only task_self_trap is safe (host/thread self return caller-deallocated send rights). DONE: task_self_trap 6162->4109 (-33%, -4.4% total RPCs). RED->GREEN cached_self_ports_test.c. Branch perf/cache-task-self-trap @b3e857a (xnu). NOT pushed. NOTE distinct from .14 (recv-spin generalize) -- this is guest-side CACHING, the perf#9 refocus.
close_reason: Completed: implementation/measurement is recorded as DONE in the bead; remaining rollout/publication is handled by patch-profile workflow and successor perf tasks.

title: perf #9-decompose: GUEST per-callnum recvmsg-sleep accountant (done)
description: Guest-side per-callnum reply-wait accountant (the guest counterpart of .16's server-side metrics). Generator brackets each recv with rdtsc per-callnum timer (#ifdef DARLING_RPC_SLEEP_ACCOUNT, default OFF/byte-identical), dumps at sys_exit. DONE: decomposed the 57% reply-wait -- mach_msg_overwrite 19%, kprintf 14.5%, fork_wait_for_child 12.6%, self-traps 17%. 3 gotchas: clock_gettime wedges guest (rdtsc), __thread aborts (stack local), no destructor + single-table .c. RED->GREEN rpc_sleep_account_test.sh. Branches perf/guest-per-callnum-sleep-account: darlingserver @ee46485, xnu @2b28548. NOT pushed.
close_reason: Completed: implementation/measurement is recorded as DONE in the bead; remaining rollout/publication is handled by patch-profile workflow and successor perf tasks.

title: perf #2: parallelize darlingserver checkin/RPC handling (remove single-event-loop serialization)
description: THE REAL BOTTLENECK, biggest payoff + biggest risk. darlingserver processes ALL client RPCs (incl every fork/exec/thread checkin) on ONE event loop (Server::start in server.cpp) draining a single listener SOCK_DGRAM via receiveMany -> callFromMessage -> _workQueue. Under N concurrent guest processes hammering checkins, this serializes them so per-create latency scales with TOTAL system concurrency. Investigate concurrent checkin processing: worker pool draining WorkQueue in parallel, or sharded listener sockets, keeping dtape/registry locking correct (threadRegistry/processRegistry are shared_mutex-guarded; Thread/Process via shared_ptr). Audit registerIfAbsent races, _replyAddress routing, and the dtape microthread model assumption of single-threaded kernel emulation. May be partly/wholly infeasible without large dtape rework — SCOPE FIRST (spike: can checkin specifically be fast-pathed off the main loop?). Measure per-create latency vs concurrent-process count, before/after. Build from darling-build. Risk HIGH (core emulated-kernel concurrency). Likely depends on perf #1. Nothing pushed.
close_reason: Server dispatch bottleneck removed by perf #2b inline fast-path (+22% throughput, ~8x p99, ships safe at default ST). Originally-feared MT rewrite proven unnecessary by profiling. Sub-ideas #2c/#2d/#2e tracked separately.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
SCOPING SPIKE DONE 2026-06-28 (Opus), NOT pushed.

KEY FINDING: darlingserver's WorkQueue (workers.hpp) is ALREADY a hardware_concurrency()-sized thread pool; processCall runs on it. It is forced to exactly 1 worker only by build flag DSERVER_SINGLE_THREADED=ON (CMakeLists.txt:59), which upstream commit 2b0ae0e flipped ON as a TEMP workaround for "bugs specific to multi-threaded darlingserver" (never revisited). So perf #2 = re-enable MT + fix races, NOT a from-scratch rewrite.

Concurrency model less dangerous than feared: current_thread() is a HOOK -> C++ thread-local (server.cpp:85), so each native worker correctly resolves its own microthread (NO GS-segment race). dtape locks are real (native libsimple_lock-backed, owner-tracked, locks.c).

EMPIRICAL A/B (8-proc tcstorm multistorm, 22.6s window; built MT server md5 0d64e45a vs ST baseline c9184cd6, deployed to local prefix, 2 runs each):
- MT is STABLE: clean boot + 2 storms, NO crash / panic / deadlock; pool scaled to workers_total=10.
- BUT throughput REGRESSED ~32%: ST ~3830 checkin/s (3858, 3803) vs MT ~2594 (2548, 2640). checkin p50 16us -> 64us. Queue DID drain (wq_depth 5->0, blocked 6->1).

ROOT CAUSE: ~all RPC work happens inside dtape critical sections (waitq / scheduler / registry / IPC big-locks), so N workers serialize on those anyway, plus pay native-lock cache-line bouncing + context-switch + the lock-without-active-thread SPIN path (locks.c:59 = task #53). Mid-storm workers_busy ~0 => workers spend their time BLOCKED on dtape locks, not doing parallel work. This is exactly why upstream left it single-threaded.

VERDICT: naive flag-flip is a NET LOSS; do NOT ship it. Real perf #2 (true parallelism) requires narrowing the dtape critical sections (finer-grained kernel locks, or fast-pathing checkin off the big lock) = the HIGH-risk large dtape rework the bead itself warned of. The cheap win is NOT here.

RECOMMEND: keep ST; redirect perf effort to perf #3 (lighten per-checkin RPC cost: socket reuse / fast-path), which attacks the same latency without the concurrency rework.

State restored: build reverted to DSERVER_SINGLE_THREADED=ON; ST baseline c9184cd6 redeployed to the prefix; MT binary 0d64e45a kept at ~/work/darling-build for future dtape work. Artifacts: ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/perf1-multi-{baseline-st,mt,mt2,baseline-st2}-*. Detail in memory dar-perf2-server-multithread-scope.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RESOLVED via the cheap+safe route, not the originally-feared rewrite. The scope spike + perf #2a profiling reframed this: the server bottleneck was NOT single-event-loop serialization of kernel work, it was the main-loop<->worker condvar handoff tax (~70% CPU). perf #2b (dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.8) eliminates it by running cheap RPCs inline on the loop: +22% throughput over ST baseline, ~8x better p99, and it ships SAFE at the default DSERVER_SINGLE_THREADED=ON (no MT flip needed). Remaining sub-ideas: #2c parallel-dispatch (P3, parse is only 0.66% so low value), #2d narrow-dtape-locks (DEFERRED -- no lock contention exists), #2e shard-server (backlog). Closing this umbrella as the bottleneck is removed; reopen only if a future workload re-surfaces server-side serialization.

title: perf #11: gate hot-path kprintf debug traces (done)
description: perf#9-decompose found kprintf=14.5% of build reply-wait = 3 unconditional debug traces (execve 'execve expand' every exec, filio 'dtype for fd', sigexc 'darling_sigexc_self()' every proc-start), each a SYNC RPC round-trip. FIX: hotpath_kdebug() no-op macro (simple.h, -DDARLING_HOTPATH_KPRINTF_DEBUG) for execve/filio; sigexc via existing kern_printf/DEBUG_SIGEXC gate. GOTCHA: traces compiled into BOTH dylib AND dyld (dyld links VARIANT_DYLD sigexc.c) -- rebuild+deploy dyld too. DONE: server kprintf log 144->0, breakdown drops kprintf, build 100/100. RED->GREEN hotpath_kprintf_gated_test.sh. Branch perf/gate-hotpath-kprintf-debug @0984b9b (xnu). NOT pushed.
close_reason: Completed: implementation/measurement is recorded as DONE in the bead; remaining rollout/publication is handled by patch-profile workflow and successor perf tasks.

title: perf #12: cut the ~2.85ms per-fork checkin cost (the real build-speed lever)
description: Re-scoped from perf#8 (closed: reaping is already optimal). MEASURED on the dar-l8k-fixed lineage: guest fork() returns in ~2.85ms (p50, children _exit(0) immediately so ~99.6% is the fork mechanism itself), vs reaping ~5us/child. This per-fork cost is what makes make -jN builds look hung -- the parent (make/sh) idle-waits in do_wait while each child takes ~2.85ms to check in and start (mis-attributed as '39% reaping' by the perf#6 wchan sampler). The cost lives in fork.c:61 dserver_rpc_checkin(true,...) + guard_table/socket/lifetime-pipe refresh, running serially in the child before the parent's waitForChildAfterFork/fork_wait_for_child (~257us, perf#9) unblocks. GOAL: decompose the ~2.85ms (child-side checkin RPC vs socket refresh vs address-space/dtape setup), find the dominant component, design a fix (likely perf#1-style adaptive wait generalized to fork, or reducing serial RPC round-trips in the child checkin). RED->GREEN: a fork+exit storm measuring fork()-return latency (fork-decomp.c probe already built). Med risk (fork/checkin semantics must stay faithful). Expected: large fraction of build wall-clock since fork dominates fork-heavy builds.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DECOMPOSITION (Opus 2026-06-28, dar-l8k-fixed lineage, uncontended host; probes fork-decomp.c + fork-split.c in guest home, 150-200 children x2):

- fork() return latency (parent unblocks): p50 ~2.8ms, mean ~2.9ms, consistent across runs.
- child-first-user-instr minus parent-fork-call: p50 ~3.4-3.7ms (child reaches user main slightly after parent unblocks -- expected, child finishes checkin+mach_init before returning to caller).
- RECV-SPIN OFF vs ON makes NO difference to fork latency (OFF p50 2.80ms / 2.82ms; ON ~same). => the 2.85ms is NOT recv reply-wait that spinning can hide.
- Per-call server metrics (perf#9, [[dar-perf9-percall-rpc-breakdown]]) already showed checkin SERVICE time is tiny => the 2.85ms is NOT server compute either.

So the per-fork cost is STRUCTURAL handshake choreography, distributed across: native clone -> child guard_table_postfork + __dserver_per_thread_socket_refresh (fresh socket()+connect() to the server per fork, via elfcalls->mldr) + __dserver_process_lifetime_pipe_refresh + dserver_rpc_checkin(true) (server schedules a microthread, dtape_task_create + dtape_thread_create -- the same notifyCheckin path fixed in dar-l8k) -> reply -> parent fork_wait_for_child unblocks (~257us, perf#9) + child mach_init_doit (task_self_trap + mach_reply_port, 2 RPCs, self-port cache reset on fork per perf#7). No single hot component -> no micro-opt (recv-spin/cache/NO_REPLY) addresses it; the floor is the serial 'new child must set up a fresh server connection + new dtape task/thread + 2 self-trap RPCs before it can run, while the parent blocks'.

CANDIDATE DIRECTIONS (all med-high risk, need design): (1) OVERLAP parent fork_wait with child checkin instead of strict serial (already partly so); (2) reduce child startup RPCs -- fold mach_init_doit's task_self_trap+mach_reply_port into the checkin reply (server already knows the new task's self port -> return it in the checkin reply, saving 2 round-trips); (3) avoid the per-fork socket()+connect() by pre-creating or inheriting a usable RPC channel; (4) perf#1-style: make the parent's wait a futex on a checked-in flag rather than any spin/yield (already done for thread-create). NEXT: instrument the child checkin timeline server-side (timestamp at notifyCheckin entry/exit + reply-sent) on a debug build to attribute the 2.85ms across socket-connect vs dtape-create vs scheduling, THEN pick the highest-yield direction. Probes saved in guest home.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
RPC-FLOOR measurement (rpc-cost.c, 500 warm calls): task_self_trap = 0.1us (CACHED, perf#7 working -- not hitting server), mach_reply_port round-trip = 32us. So a WARM RPC round-trip floor is ~32us. The fork child's mach_init_doit pays ~1 real RPC (reply_port ~32us; task_self cached) + the checkin itself = WELL UNDER 100us of round-trips total.

=> ~2.7ms of the 2.85ms/fork is NOT RPC round-trip latency. It is the fork-SPECIFIC structural cost: clone of the full mldr address space + child socket refresh/reconnect + the SERVER scheduling a brand-new microthread for the checking-in child and creating its dtape TASK + THREAD (dtape_task_create/dtape_thread_create in notifyCheckin -- ~3200B thread struct, the same path as dar-l8k). A warm reply_port (32us) does none of that; the 89x gap to 2.85ms IS the new-task/new-thread bring-up + scheduling.

REVISED RECOMMENDATION: candidate (2) 'fold the 2 self-traps into the checkin reply' would save <100us -- NOT WORTH IT. The real lever is the per-fork NEW-MICROTHREAD SCHEDULING + DTAPE TASK/THREAD CREATION on the server. Directions worth pursuing: (A) make dtape task/thread creation for a fork-checkin cheaper / partly lazy; (B) overlap child address-space bring-up with the server-side task creation; (C) confirm via server-side timestamps (notifyCheckin entry -> dtape_task_create done -> dtape_thread_create done -> reply sent) exactly how the ~2.7ms splits between server-side creation and child-side clone/socket. Recommend (C) FIRST on a debug/instrumented server build before any fix -- this is now firmly in 'measure the server creation timeline' territory, NOT a guest micro-opt. Probe rpc-cost.c saved in guest home.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
SERVER-SIDE TIMELINE INSTRUMENTATION (Opus 2026-06-28). Added -DDSERVER_FORK_TIMELINE (CMake option DSERVER_FORK_TIMELINE, #ifdef DARLING_FORK_TIMELINE; OFF by default, byte-identical when off): the Thread ctor stashes per-phase timestamps in a thread_local (ctor-enter / tid-resolved / dtape-create-done), the Checkin::processCall handler emits a 'forktimeline' always-logged line with the deltas + reply time. Built server 3baaee34 (non-ASan, normal size), deployed, ran the fork-decomp probe (200 forks x2). 415 fork-checkin timelines captured.

HARD RESULT -- the server is NOT the bottleneck:
  tid_resolve (procfs NSpid scan): p50=23us p90=40us max=111us. ptrace SLOW path NEVER taken (0/415) -- procfs fast path always resolves the TID.
  dtape_create (dtape_thread_create + 4 semaphores): ~3-12us.
  notify+handler (notifyCheckin incl parent notify): ~6-87us.
  ctor_to_reply_TOTAL (server-side everything from Thread ctor entry to just before reply send): p50=53us p90=94us p99=184us max=370us.

So the ENTIRE server-side handling of a fork checkin is p50 53us / p99 184us, NEVER >0.37ms. But the guest sees fork() take ~2770us (p50). => ~2600us (~94-98%) of the per-fork cost is OUTSIDE darlingserver's checkin handling. The lever is NOT dtape task/thread creation (my prior hypothesis: 'the 89x gap IS new-task/thread bring-up' is now DISPROVEN -- dtape_create is ~6us). The 2.6ms is CHILD-SIDE / transport: the freshly-cloned child's mldr postfork reinit (__mldr_postfork_child + guard_table_postfork_child) + per-fork socket()+connect() refresh + the scheduling/wakeup latency for the cloned child to run and for its checkin message to reach the server + the reply to get back and wake the parent's fork_wait_for_child. NEXT: instrument the GUEST fork.c child path (clone-return -> postfork -> socket refresh -> checkin send -> checkin return) to split the 2.6ms across mldr-postfork vs socket-connect vs RPC-transport. Instrumented server kept in darling-build; prod a13ec2ae backed up to ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/darlingserver.prod-a13ec2ae.bak.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
GUEST CHILD-SIDE TIMELINE (Opus 2026-06-28) -- the per-fork cost is now FULLY PINNED. Added -DDARLING_FORK_CHILD_TIMELINE (emulation CMake option, #ifdef-gated, OFF=byte-identical): rdtsc phase markers in sys_fork's child path (fork.c), emitted via one __simple_kprintf AFTER checkin (so the emit RPC does not perturb measured phases). Built dylib 98373b8e + dyld ea9da917, deployed (x3/x2) with the timeline server 3baaee34, ran 40 forks with DSERVER_LOG_LEVEL=debug (kprintf category needs >=info). 51 child timelines. TSC ~268 ticks/us.

CHILD-SIDE BREAKDOWN (mean, calibrated to us against fork()-return p50~3245us):
  __mldr_postfork_child:            63us   1.9%
  guard_table_postfork_child:      115us   3.5%
  __dserver_per_thread_socket_refresh (socket+connect): 170us  5.2%
  __dserver_process_lifetime_pipe_refresh: 0.7us 0.0%
  misc (guard_table_add):           30us   0.9%
  dserver_rpc_checkin ROUND-TRIP: 2902us  88.5%   <<<<
  TOTAL:                          3280us

The checkin RPC round-trip is 88.5% of the per-fork cost. Cross-referencing the server-side timeline (ctor_to_reply p50=53us): of the ~2902us checkin_rtt, the server does only ~53us of actual work -> ~2849us (~87% of the WHOLE fork) is TRANSPORT + SCHEDULING/WAKEUP latency: the freshly-cloned child being scheduled to run, its checkin message reaching the server, and (dominant) the round-trip WAKEUP latency to receive the reply. NOT server compute, NOT child setup (postfork+socket = only ~11%).

=> THE LEVER IS THE CHECKIN ROUND-TRIP WAKEUP LATENCY. This is the same class perf#7 recv-spin targets, yet the perf#7 A/B showed no fork wall-clock change -- so either the fork-child checkin recv does NOT go through the recv-spin hook, or a freshly-cloned child's first recvmsg has a structural ~2.8ms wakeup floor (scheduler placing the new task on a CPU + cross-process futex/socket wakeup). NEXT: (1) verify whether dserver_rpc_checkin's recv in the fork child uses the bounded recv-spin hook (it should, same dylib) and why spin doesn't cut the 2.8ms -- is the child not even SCHEDULED to spin until ~2.8ms after clone? (2) measure clone-return -> child-first-scheduled latency in isolation. If the 2.8ms is Linux scheduler latency placing the cloned child, that is a host scheduling / CPU-affinity / sched-wakeup issue, not an RPC-protocol one. All instrumentation gated+uncommitted; prod a13ec2ae + recvspin-ON dylib e5cd6b12/dyld 025aa772 backed up for restore.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DISCRIMINATOR: recv-spin budget A/B (Opus 2026-06-28). Built a dylib+dyld with DARLING_GUEST_RECVSPIN=20000000 (hispin, dylib 3e9d96df) to test whether the 2.85ms checkin_rtt is BLOCKING-RECV reply-wakeup latency (which a huge busy-spin would catch -> fork drops toward the server's 53us) or CHILD SCHEDULING/transport (child not even running to spin -> unchanged/worse). RESULT: hispin fork()-return p50 ~4100us, mean ~3830-4200us -- WORSE than the ~2800us baseline, NOT better. CONCLUSIVE: the 2.85ms is NOT recv reply-wakeup. Busy-spinning burns the CPU the freshly-cloned child needs to be scheduled in the first place (and steals cores from the server/siblings), so it makes things worse. This is exactly why the perf#7 recv-spin A/B was wall-clock-neutral on fork.

FINAL ROOT CAUSE for perf#12: ~87% of the per-fork cost is the freshly-cloned child's LINUX SCHEDULING LATENCY to first-run + complete its first cross-process checkin round-trip -- NOT server compute (53us), NOT child setup (postfork+socket ~11%/~370us), NOT RPC reply-wakeup (spin makes it worse). The newly cloned mldr child takes ~2.8ms to get scheduled onto a CPU and drive its checkin to completion. This is a HOST-SCHEDULER-shaped problem (new-task placement / wakeup / cross-process futex), not an RPC-protocol micro-opt. Candidate levers now: (A) reduce the NUMBER of forks the build does (out of scope -- that's the workload); (B) make the cloned child cheaper to schedule (smaller address space to COW? warm a pool of pre-checked-in helper processes? posix_spawn fast-path that skips the fork+checkin and creates the task server-side directly?); (C) investigate why a cloned child needs ~2.8ms to first-run -- CPU affinity, scheduler features, or mldr's postfork blocking on something. HIGH risk / architectural. Recommend pausing perf#12 implementation here with the root cause pinned, and bringing options A/B/C to the user. All instrumentation gated+uncommitted.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RED GATE established (red-tdd, per user request 'сделать RED тест ... обернуть в GREEN фиксом'). ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/fork-latency-gate.c + run-fork-latency-gate.sh: measures p50 fork() latency under a serial fork storm (children _exit(0)), asserts <=1000us. PROVEN RED on current prod (server a13ec2ae / dylib e5cd6b12): 'fork() latency us (N=200 serial): min=2504 p50=2943 p90=3265 max=3881 | threshold=1000 -> FAIL (RED) rc=1'. Threshold 1000us = ~3x below the pathological ~2900us intake-queue latency, ~2x above a healthy fork (server handles checkin in 53us; warm RPC floor 32us; child first post-checkin RPC 61us) -> CI/host-noise headroom. A perf#12 fix (server checkin-intake responsiveness = perf#2-intake, OR pool .22, OR posix_spawn fast-path .23) must flip it GREEN and COMMIT the gate in the same branch (ideally migrated to west test env:darling once that path exists). FULL next-agent handoff written to ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/PERF12-HANDOFF.md (root cause + proof + all instrumented binaries + file map + env/build/deploy + constraints). Memory: dar-perf12-fork-checkin-cost. Investigation bead .24 CLOSED. Candidates .22/.23 OPEN+unblocked. Prod restored a13ec2ae/e5cd6b12/025aa772; all instrumentation gated+UNCOMMITTED.

title: perf #12-A: pre-warmed pool of checked-in helper processes (fork fast-path)
description: CANDIDATE solution for perf#12 (the ~2.85ms/fork = cloned child's Linux scheduling latency to first-run + first checkin round-trip; see bead dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.21 for the full two-sided instrumentation proof). IDEA: maintain a pool of pre-forked, already-checked-in helper processes so the build hands work to a warm process instead of paying the ~2.8ms cold fork+schedule+checkin each time. Amortizes the dominant cost. BIGGEST potential win, BIGGEST change to the fork/exec model, HIGH risk (correctness of fork semantics, COW state, pid identity, signal/wait behavior must stay macOS-faithful). BLOCKED ON the perf#12 scheduling investigation (bead TBD) which must first confirm WHERE the 2.8ms goes (scheduler placement vs mldr postfork blocking) -- if a cheap scheduler knob fixes it, a pool may be unnecessary. Do NOT start before that investigation reports.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: JUSTIFIED by perf#15/.27 measurement 2026-06-29 (Opus): per-process startup wall ~2.7-3.0ms is MEASURED 64% RPC (~1.7-1.9ms), and those startup RPCs are a 0%-batchable sequential Mach-bootstrap chain (every adjacent pair has 20-445us CPU between -> data-dependency barriers). Cheap fixes (cache/NO_REPLY=A, batch=C) are ruled out BY MEASUREMENT. This bead is now on the critical path as one of the only remaining startup levers. Use the .29 per-phase RPC accountant (DARLING_RPC_PHASE_COUNT) as the RED->GREEN A/B gate. Still weeks-scale/high-risk per perf#12 -- user decision pending.

title: perf #12-C: posix_spawn server-side fast-path (skip fork+checkin for exec case)
description: CANDIDATE solution for perf#12 (the ~2.85ms/fork = cloned child's Linux scheduling latency to first-run + first checkin round-trip; proof on bead dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.21). IDEA: for the common make/clang pattern of fork() immediately followed by exec(), build the new task SERVER-SIDE directly and skip the cold fork+checkin handshake entirely (a posix_spawn-style fast-path). Helps spawn-heavy builds (the dominant real workload -- make spawns clang per file), though not raw fork()-without-exec. HIGH risk (must preserve exec semantics, fd inheritance, signal disposition, vfork-like atomicity). BLOCKED ON the perf#12 scheduling investigation (bead TBD): confirm the 2.8ms is the cold-child bring-up the fast-path would avoid, and that the server-side task-create path (~6us per the timeline) scales. Do NOT start before that investigation reports.
close_reason: M2 decision point (2026-06-29, Opus): server-side posix_spawn cannot move the gate. spawn-of-a-real-macho ~9ms is ~70% irreducible dyld/libSystem STARTUP; even zeroing the entire clone+checkin chain leaves ~6ms = 4x over the 1500us gate. The whole .23 premise was built on a wrong cost model (assumed checkin dominates spawn). M1 ns-placement gate retained. Re-scoped to startup-cost: perf#13 framing (.25) + perf#14 dyld-shared-cache (.26) + perf#15 libSystem-chatter (.27) + perf#16 compiler-server (.28). Full evidence in the .23 M2 comment + memory dar-perf12-fork-checkin-cost.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
2026-06-28 (Opus) investigation done + surgical attempt FAILED + feasibility found.

(1) ROOT CAUSE (proven, .21 + memory dar-perf12-fork-checkin-cost): ~2.8-3ms/spawn = SERIALIZED CROSS-PROCESS SCHEDULING CHAIN of per-process clone+register+exec (clone->child-sched, child-RPC->server-wake ~480us, reply->child-wake, exit->parent-wake). Server ~58us/checkin, never backlogs (loop-intake instr). NOT fixable server-side or by spin (hispin WORSE).

(2) SURGICAL 'collapse double round-trip' IMPLEMENTED+TESTED -> FAILED. Guest-only: spawn-child skips fork-checkin (sys_fork_for_spawn), parent skips fork_wait_for_child (libSystem_posix_spawn_parent drops _mach_fork_parent), child registers lazily via task_self_trap, parent syncs via posix_spawn error pipe. CORRECTNESS PASSED (spawn runs, exit status 7 propagates, exec-fail rc ok, death-detect via pidfd monitor + mldr re-sends lifetime pipe). BUT spawn p50=8711us vs ~3000us prod = 3x SLOWER, gate still RED. Removing fork-checkin does NOT remove a scheduling hop; the fork-checkin was the EFFICIENT registration. Source REVERTED pristine. RED gate spawn-latency-gate.c + run-spawn-latency-gate.sh (~/work/dar-6x4-repro) PROVEN RED on prod (GATE_RC=1).

(3) FEASIBILITY for FULL server-side spawn: server ALREADY fork+execs guest procs -- it IS container init (darling.c spawnInitProcess execl's darlingserver PID1 in guest ns) + spawns launchd via darlingserver.cpp:691 clone(CLONE_NEWPID|SIGCHLD)->mount procfs/drop privs/execl(mldr! vchroot launchd) (spawnLaunchd :255). So .23 = generalize spawnLaunchd to a per-spawn RPC; server already inside guest ns (no setns). HARD: clone+execl from a WORKER mid-event-loop (spawnLaunchd is startup-only pre-loop); marshal path/argv/envp+file_actions over new RPC; fd inheritance; signal/sigmask/pgroup/START_SUSPENDED/SETEXEC/CLOEXEC_DEFAULT; return new pid. MULTI-SESSION feature. Design+plan: ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/PERF12-SPAWN-FIX-DESIGN.md. User approved full .23.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-28 (Opus) 3x REGRESSION ROOT-CAUSED. Diagnostics: DSERVER_SPAWN_DIAG (server per-thread-ctor: stack_hint/costly/ctor_us, always-logged) + DARLING_SPAWN_DIAG (guest posix_spawn child/parent rdtsc phase split). FINDINGS: (a) server-side registration of the spawn-child via task_self_trap is FAST (p50 32us, max 82us) and NEVER hits the ptrace/costly path -> server is NOT the regression. (b) dominant phase = guest [spawnchild] fork_to_machinit (fork-return -> end of posix_spawn_child/mach_init_doit), ~8-9.4M rdtsc ticks, vs machinit_to_execve ~0.1-0.4M. The freshly-cloned child is SLOW TO GET SCHEDULED to run mach_init. ROOT CAUSE OF REGRESSION: the OLD parent fork_wait_for_child BLOCKED the parent = an implicit SCHEDULING HANDOFF (blocked parent donates CPU so child runs promptly). The fix removed it (parent runs ahead, runnable), so the child competes for CPU and starves at mach_init -> 3x slower. KEY INSIGHT: fork-checkin/fork-wait was NOT pure overhead -- the parent's blocking wait is a load-bearing scheduling handoff. => POSSIBLE CHEAP FIX (untried): keep skipping the fork-checkin RPC round-trip BUT restore a lightweight parent->child scheduling handoff (e.g. parent blocks on a cross-fork futex the child bumps once running, or server wakes parent fork-wait when the child first registers) so the child runs promptly without the full RPC. If that recovers perf, the surgical path is viable after all and full .23 may be unnecessary. All diag/fix edits REVERTED; prod restored (e5cd6b12/025aa772/fdad5d19/a13ec2ae). Diag harness scripts in CLAUDE_JOB_DIR/tmp/diag{2,3}-spawn.sh; gates+design in ~/work/dar-6x4-repro.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== NEXT-AGENT HANDOFF (2026-06-28 Opus) — continue here ===

GOAL: flip the RED spawn-latency gate GREEN by recovering the perf the surgical fix lost, WITHOUT the multi-week full server-side spawn (.23). The diagnosis below points to a cheap fix.

WHERE WE ARE (full chain, all proven this session):
1. Root cause of slow fork/spawn (perf#12, bead .21 + memory dar-perf12-fork-checkin-cost): the ~2.8-3ms/spawn is the SERIALIZED CROSS-PROCESS SCHEDULING-HOP CHAIN of a per-process clone+register+exec. Server is ~58us/checkin, never backlogs (loop-intake instrumentation). NOT fixable server-side, NOT by spin (hispin WORSE).
2. posix_spawn = sys_fork() + setup + sys_execve (guest posix_spawn.c). The fork half pays the full cost; spawn/fork+exec IS the real make/clang build hot path.
3. SURGICAL FIX ATTEMPT (skip the spawn-child fork-checkin + skip parent fork_wait_for_child, sync via posix_spawn error pipe): correctness PASSED but spawn went ~3x SLOWER (8.7ms vs 3ms prod). 
4. REGRESSION ROOT-CAUSED (diag DSERVER_SPAWN_DIAG + DARLING_SPAWN_DIAG): server-side child registration via task_self_trap is FAST (p50 32us, no ptrace). The dominant phase is guest [spawnchild] fork_to_machinit -- the freshly-cloned child is SLOW TO GET SCHEDULED to run mach_init_doit. The OLD parent fork_wait_for_child BLOCKED the parent = an implicit SCHEDULING HANDOFF (blocked parent donates CPU so child runs promptly). Removing it made the child starve.

>>> THE FIX TO TRY (cheap, ~1-2 build cycles): keep skipping the fork-checkin RPC round-trip, BUT restore a lightweight parent->child scheduling handoff so the child runs promptly:
  Option A (preferred, no server change): a cross-fork FUTEX in shared memory. Before fork, the parent mmaps a shared anon page (MAP_SHARED) holding an atomic word; the spawn-child bumps+FUTEX_WAKEs it as the FIRST thing after clone (before mach_init); the parent FUTEX_WAITs on it right after sys_fork_for_spawn returns (instead of running ahead). This blocks the parent briefly = the handoff, with NO server RPC. Then parent proceeds to its existing sys_read(pipe[0]) sync.
  Option B: server wakes the parent fork-wait when the spawn-child first registers (via task_self_trap) -- reintroduces parent-block cheaply but needs a server change + a way to mark 'this registration is a spawn-child whose parent waits'.
  Try A first; measure with run-spawn-latency-gate.sh (threshold 1500us). If GREEN AND bare-fork gate unchanged AND correctness (spawn runs, exit status, exec-fail) holds -> the surgical path WINS, full .23 unnecessary. red-tdd: commit spawn-latency-gate.c WITH the fix.

EXACT EDITS to re-apply the surgical base (all REVERTED now; reapply then add the handoff):
  - fork.c: __sys_fork_impl(int for_spawn) + sys_fork()=impl(0) + sys_fork_for_spawn()=impl(1); for_spawn=1 wraps the dserver_rpc_checkin block in if(!for_spawn){...}. (header fork.h declares sys_fork_for_spawn.)
  - posix_spawn.c: call sys_fork_for_spawn() instead of sys_fork() at the fork site. ADD the futex handoff here (parent wait + child wake).
  - libsystem/init.c libSystem_posix_spawn_parent(): remove the _mach_fork_parent() call (the fork-wait round-trip). [WITH option A the parent no longer needs it.]
  Build+deploy: dylib x3 + dyld x2 + libSystem.B.dylib x2 (NEW target: ~/work/darling-prefix{,/libexec/darling}/usr/lib/libSystem.B.dylib). server unchanged for option A. Use deploy-spawn-fix-and-validate.sh (FIX/PROD) -- already handles all 7 copies + gates + correctness.

ASSETS (~/work/dar-6x4-repro): spawn-latency-gate.c + run-spawn-latency-gate.sh (RED gate, proven RED on prod), fork-latency-gate.c (regression check), deploy-spawn-fix-and-validate.sh, PERF12-SPAWN-FIX-DESIGN.md, PERF12-HANDOFF.md. Diag harnesses: /home/ilyagulya/.claude-work/jobs/ae391f52/tmp/diag{2,3}-spawn.sh. Prod backups: darlingserver.prod-a13ec2ae.bak, libsystem_kernel.dylib.prod-e5cd6b12.bak, dyld.prod-025aa772.bak, libSystem.B.dylib.prod.bak (fdad5d19).
DIAG FLAGS (gated, OFF, byte-identical off): DSERVER_SPAWN_DIAG (darlingserver CMakeLists+thread.cpp+logging.cpp), DARLING_SPAWN_DIAG (emulation CMakeLists+posix_spawn.c). GUEST kprintf -> boot DSERVER_LOG_LEVEL=debug; server forktimeline/spawndiag category is always-logged.
GOTCHAS: TSC ticks/us scale on this host is uncertain (fork_to_machinit absolute us looked >total -- rdtsc likely spans deschedule); trust the RATIO not absolute us. clock_gettime wedges guest in RPC atomic region (use rdtsc/mach_absolute_time). __thread aborts in fork path (stack locals). mldr statically links some rpc wrappers -> rebuild+deploy dylib+dyld+libSystem TOGETHER.
STATE: prod RESTORED (server a13ec2ae / dylib e5cd6b12 / dyld 025aa772 / libSystem fdad5d19), guest source pristine, build flags OFF, NOTHING pushed. STANDING: ne push / nichego ne publikuy; red-tdd (commit gate with fix).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
OPTION A (cross-fork futex scheduling handoff) IMPLEMENTED + MEASURED -> FAILED, and the 'missing scheduling-donation' hypothesis is now DISPROVEN (2026-06-29, Opus).

WHAT WAS BUILT: re-applied the surgical base (fork.c __sys_fork_impl(for_spawn)/sys_fork_for_spawn skipping the child fork-checkin; posix_spawn.c uses it; libsystem init.c libSystem_posix_spawn_parent drops _mach_fork_parent) + ADDED the handoff: posix_spawn mmaps a one-word MAP_SHARED|ANON futex page before the fork; the spawn-child stores 1 + FUTEX_WAKE right after posix_spawn_child()/mach_init; the parent FUTEX_WAITs on it (loop, EAGAIN-safe) right after sys_fork_for_spawn, then proceeds to the existing error-pipe sync; munmap at out. Built dylib/dyld/libSystem (7-copy deploy), prod server.

RESULT: CORRECTNESS PASSES (HELLO printed, exit status 7 propagates, exec-fail rc=2). BUT spawn-latency gate p50 = 8937us (min 8067 / p90 10386 / max 14563) vs ~3000us prod = STILL ~3x SLOWER, gate RED. Bare-fork gate unchanged (p50 2947us) as expected (fork path untouched).

DECISIVE DIAGNOSTIC (DARLING_SPAWN_DIAG, gated): instrumented the parent's futex loop. Every spawn logged 'blocked_waits=1 eagain=0 word=1' -> the parent GENUINELY BLOCKED in FUTEX_WAIT and was woken by the child after mach_init. So the handoff worked exactly as designed; it is NOT a no-op. => Restoring the parent-block / 'scheduling donation' does NOT recover the perf. This EMPIRICALLY REFUTES the line-42 hypothesis (the 3x regression was supposedly a lost CPU-donation handoff). 

RECONCILES with the PROVEN root cause (memory dar-perf12-fork-checkin-cost line 36/40): the ~2.8-3ms is the cross-process WAKEUP-LATENCY chain of clone+register, not CPU contention; blocking the parent adds ANOTHER wakeup hop (child must FUTEX_WAKE parent) instead of removing one. The surgical path's 3x regression is from ADDING hops (lazy task_self_trap registration + now a futex round-trip) vs the fork-checkin which registered the child efficiently inside the fork barrier. CONFIRMED: you cannot fix this guest-side by reorganizing RPCs or adding a futex -- the ONLY lever is to NOT create a separate guest process (full server-side spawn .23, or warm-pool .22).

CONCLUSION: Option A is a confirmed DEAD END. Both the surgical-collapse and the futex-handoff variants fail; the missing-donation theory is disproven. The next viable step is genuinely the heavy one: full server-side spawn (.23 -- server clone+execl(mldr) in the guest ns, generalizing spawnLaunchd, marshalling path/argv/envp/file_actions over a new RPC) OR warm-process pool (.22). Both multi-session/high-risk; recommend confirming with the user before committing. STATE: ALL edits reverted, prod restored (dylib e5cd6b12 / dyld 025aa772 / libSystem fdad5d19 / server a13ec2ae), build flags OFF, NOTHING pushed. Gate spawn-latency-gate.c still the correct RED gate for .23. Diag flag DARLING_SPAWN_DIAG left in the design notes (reverted from source).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== .23 SERVER-SIDE SPAWN — SCOPE (2026-06-29, Opus) ===

VERDICT: feasible but a real multi-session FEATURE, not a patch (~2-4 build/validate cycles for first correct spawn of a trivial binary; more for full posix_spawn surface). The blocker is NOT spawn logic -- it's that darlingserver runs OUTSIDE the guest PID namespace and has no machinery to enter it.

ARCHITECTURAL BLOCKER (proven via codebase exploration): the server bootstraps the guest PID ns ONCE via clone(CLONE_NEWPID) (darlingserver.cpp:693), launchd becomes PID1 inside it, the server PARENT stays in the host PID ns and runs the event loop (darlingserver.cpp:731-755). There is NO setns() anywhere in src/. The server SHARES the guest MOUNT ns (unshare(CLONE_NEWNS)+mount @600) so it already sees guest paths -- PID ns is the one hard divider. The server reaches guest procs only REACTIVELY: guest forks/execs ITSELF, checks in over /prefix/.darlingserver.sock, server maps host-PID (SCM_CREDENTIALS, =ID) to guest-ns-PID (RPC header, =NSID) and pidfd_open()s the host PID for death (server.cpp:818, process.cpp:39). No reverse path exists -- nothing lets the server INITIATE a guest process. A naive fork+execl from a worker lands in the HOST ns = wrong ns/getpid, invisible to the guest.

WHAT A SERVER-SIDE SPAWN MUST REPRODUCE (6 streams):
A [HIGH] enter guest PID ns -- a persistent in-ns spawn-helper (or per-spawn setns). FOUNDATION.
B [MED]  new spawn RPC + marshal path/argv/envp + full _posix_spawn_args_desc (file_actions+attrs) over the datagram socket.
C [MED]  re-implement guest posix_spawn.c's between-fork-and-exec logic server-side: dup2/open/close/chdir/fchdir/inherit file actions + RESETIDS/SETPGROUP/SETSIGMASK/CLOEXEC_DEFAULT/ASLR-disable/START_SUSPENDED/binprefs (~15 behaviors).
D [HIGH] birth already-registered: build Process/dtape task+thread+semaphores+parent-linkage synchronously in-server at spawn time (removes the fork-checkin round-trip = the win). The exec'd mldr must skip its own checkin or do a local-only handshake + inherit the server-set-up RPC socket+lifetime pipe. MUST NOT regress the notifyCheckin exec-path fixes (dar-l8k current-thread UAF, dar-6x4 rwlock-across-suspend) -- reuse that exact path or gate against the same fork-storm tests.
E [LOW]  lifetime+death: reuse monitorProcess/pidfd as-is.
F [LOW]  exec-failure: return errno via the spawn RPC reply (replaces the CLOEXEC error-pipe), no half-registered Process leak.

NAMESPACE-ENTRY OPTIONS: (1 RECOMMENDED) persistent in-ns spawn-helper kept alongside launchd, server sends it requests, it fork+execl(mldr) already in-ns -- generalizes the proven spawnLaunchd pattern, no per-spawn setns/privilege juggling; cost = a new always-on supervised component. (2 fallback) per-spawn setns(/proc/<launchd>/ns/pid) from a worker -- fights the early perma_drop_privileges (setns into a PID userns needs CAP_SYS_ADMIN; per-thread; only affects children) -- reintroduces a privilege surface the project shed. (3 orthogonal) warm-pool = bead .22, not this bead.

RECOMMENDED SEQUENCE (fail-fast, session-sized milestones): M1 namespace spike -- prove the helper can make the server fork+execl(mldr! /bin/echo) into a real guest proc w/ correct nspid visible to existing checkin (validates/kills approach A cheaply, no perf claim). M2 minimal spawn RPC (no file_actions/attrs) + synchronous registration -> run spawn-latency-gate.c (<=1500us) = the perf thesis proven-or-killed FOR REAL. M3 port file_actions+attrs (C) + exec-fail return (F) + fork-storm gates green. M4 keep guest-side fork+exec as runtime fallback for unsupported shapes; default fast-path on only when gates hold; red-TDD commit gate with fix. DECISION POINT after M1/M2: if M2 doesn't move the gate below threshold the premise is wrong -- STOP before paying for M3/M4.

Visual scope doc (artifact, private): https://claude.ai/code/artifact/69b780f2-24d6-4380-bd19-c117f170c43d . STATE unchanged: prod restored, source pristine, nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== M1 NAMESPACE SPIKE: DONE + GREEN (2026-06-29, Opus) ===

GOAL (M1): prove the server can make a process land INSIDE the guest PID namespace (correct nspid, can spawn further guest procs) -- the foundation for server-side posix_spawn. Approach validated = the RECOMMENDED persistent in-namespace spawn-helper.

(1) NAMESPACE PRIMITIVE GATE (unit, RED->GREEN, COMMITTED with the spike): tests/spawn_helper_pidns_placement.c + run-spawn-helper-pidns-placement.sh. Creates a stand-in guest PID ns via clone(CLONE_NEWPID) (exactly as darlingserver does for launchd). RED arm 'outside': a fork from OUTSIDE the ns (mirroring a server-worker fork) is NOT in the guest ns (different /proc/self/ns/pid inode, single-entry NSpid) -- this is WHY the current server cannot place a spawn. GREEN arm 'helper': a fork by a MEMBER of the ns (the helper, a child of PID-1) IS in the guest ns (same inode, 2-level NSpid). PASS as root (host blocks unprivileged userns uid_map; harness re-execs via unshare --user --map-root-user when non-root, else run as root): RED outside ns_inode=...155 NOT-IN-GUEST-NS; GREEN helper ns_inode=...156 IN-GUEST-NS.

(2) LIVE SPIKE IN darlingserver (gated DSERVER_SPAWN_HELPER_SPIKE, OFF=byte-identical -- rebuilt server md5 a13ec2ae == prod with flag off, CONFIRMED): added spikeSpawnHelper() in darlingserver.cpp, forked from the launchd clone-child BEFORE spawnLaunchd (so the helper is a member of the guest PID ns). The helper records its own ns identity, then fork()+execl(mldr! vchroot <prefix> <DSERVER_SPIKE_BIN, default /usr/bin/true>) = a SERVER-ORIGINATED guest process. Deployed spike server, clean-booted guest.

RESULT (DECISIVE, <prefix>/.spawn-helper-spike.log):
  [spike-helper] host_getpid=2 ns_pid_inode=4026532284 NSpid=  2803985  2
  [spike-child]  host_getpid=3 ns_pid_inode=4026532284 NSpid=  2803986  3
  [spike-helper] guest child host_pid=3 exited=1 status=0
=> the server-created helper is born IN the guest PID ns (guest-pid 2, getpid()==2 in-ns); it spawned a guest process in the SAME ns (same inode 4026532284, guest-pid 3); that process exec'd mldr!vchroot /usr/bin/true and exited clean. Boot proceeded normally alongside the helper (launchd + children checked in fine; spike did not disturb boot). The mechanism the full feature needs is PROVEN.

VERDICT: M1 GREEN. Approach A (persistent in-ns spawn-helper) is viable -- the server CAN place processes in the guest ns by forking a helper before launchd exec, generalizing spawnLaunchd. NO setns / no privilege juggling needed (helper inherits the ns by being a child of PID-1-to-be). 

NEXT = M2: turn the one-shot spike into a request socket (server worker -> helper: spawn(path,argv,envp)), register the resulting Process SYNCHRONOUSLY server-side (skip the fork-checkin round-trip), and run spawn-latency-gate.c (<=1500us). DECISION POINT after M2: if the gate doesn't drop below threshold the whole premise is wrong -- STOP before M3/M4. M1 caveat for M2: the spike used /usr/bin/true which exits before we separately grep its checkin; M2 must keep the spawned proc alive long enough to confirm registry entry + measure.

STATE: source reverted pristine (darlingserver.cpp + CMakeLists.txt upstream; only pre-existing gated call.cpp remains M), build flag OFF (server rebuilds byte-identical a13ec2ae), prod server restored, NOTHING pushed. New gate files KEPT (uncommitted in tests/): spawn_helper_pidns_placement.c + run-spawn-helper-pidns-placement.sh.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== M2 DECISION POINT: STOP — the perf thesis as framed is WRONG (2026-06-29, Opus) ===

M2 goal: prove-or-kill that server-side spawn (in-ns helper + synchronous registration removing the fork-checkin) gets spawn under the 1500us gate. APPROACH: before building the full RPC plumbing, isolated the CORE latency cheaply -- gated spike (DSERVER_SPAWN_HELPER_SPIKE, OFF=byte-identical, server rebuilds md5 a13ec2ae) where the in-ns helper forks+execl(mldr!vchroot /usr/bin/true) in a timed loop.

MEASURED (same host, same session, prod libs):
- in-ns helper spawn+reap of a trivial macho: p50 10225us (N=80, min 9024 / p90 12750).
- TRUE prod posix_spawn gate baseline (spawn-latency-gate.c, trivial macho int main(){return 0;}, +reap): p50 9044us (N=150). [NOTE: my earlier '~3000us prod' figure was WRONG -- that conflated the fork-return number; the real spawn-of-a-macho gate is ~9ms on prod.]
- prod bare fork() return: p50 2788us.

DECISIVE DECOMPOSITION: spawn(9044us) = fork-return(2788us = the clone+checkin scheduling chain) + ~6256us of exec + mldr + dyld + libSystem init (mach_init) + run + exit + reap. The spawned helper is a REAL Mach-O (loads dyld+libSystem), so it pays the full macOS process-startup cost. THAT ~6.3ms is IRREDUCIBLE -- it exists no matter who creates the process; server-side registration cannot touch it. Even if a perfect server-side spawn drove the entire clone+checkin chain to ZERO, spawn would land ~6ms = STILL ~4x over the 1500us gate. The in-ns helper spike CONFIRMS it: at 10225us it was if anything SLOWER than prod's 9044us (helper-fork buys nothing on its own; it still pays a clone + the child's full checkin+dyld bring-up).

ROOT MISFRAME: the 1500us gate threshold assumed the fork-checkin dominates spawn. It does NOT for spawning real Mach-O binaries -- dyld/libSystem startup dominates (~70%). The checkin chain (~2.8ms) is only ~31% of a spawn, and even THAT is mostly cross-process scheduling wakeups (perf#12 root cause), not removable by moving the fork server-side.

VERDICT (DECISION POINT, as designed): STOP. Do NOT build M3/M4. Server-side posix_spawn would be weeks of HIGH-risk work (namespace helper + RPC marshalling + synchronous registration touching the dar-l8k/dar-6x4 exec-path fixes) for a BEST-CASE spawn of ~6ms = a ~31% improvement that still fails the gate by 4x -- and the realistic improvement is far less since the checkin chain is scheduling latency, not work. The whole .23 premise (and the gate) was built on the wrong cost model.

WHAT WOULD ACTUALLY MOVE BUILD WALL-CLOCK (re-scope candidates, NOT this bead): the dominant cost is per-process dyld/libSystem STARTUP, not process creation. Levers worth scoping: (a) dyld shared-cache / prebinding so each spawned tool doesn't re-resolve libSystem from scratch; (b) reduce libSystem_initializer + mach_init RPC chatter at process start (perf#9/#10 territory -- NO_REPLY/caching already shaved some); (c) for make/clang specifically, the build forks MANY short tools -- a compiler-server / persistent-worker model avoids re-startup entirely (biggest potential win, orthogonal to Darling internals). Recommend bringing this cost model to the user before opening a new bead.

M1 ARTIFACTS RETAINED: the namespace-placement gate (tests/spawn_helper_pidns_placement.c + run-*.sh) stays committed -- it proves the in-ns helper primitive works, useful if server-side spawn is ever revisited for a NON-macho or pre-warmed-dyld scenario. STATE: source pristine, flag OFF, prod restored (a13ec2ae/e5cd6b12/025aa772/fdad5d19), NOTHING pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: JUSTIFIED by perf#15/.27 measurement 2026-06-29 (Opus): per-process startup wall ~2.7-3.0ms is MEASURED 64% RPC (~1.7-1.9ms), and those startup RPCs are a 0%-batchable sequential Mach-bootstrap chain (every adjacent pair has 20-445us CPU between -> data-dependency barriers). Cheap fixes (cache/NO_REPLY=A, batch=C) are ruled out BY MEASUREMENT. This bead is now on the critical path as one of the only remaining startup levers. Use the .29 per-phase RPC accountant (DARLING_RPC_PHASE_COUNT) as the RED->GREEN A/B gate. Still weeks-scale/high-risk per perf#12 -- user decision pending.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: STAYS CLOSED — but scope clarification (2026-06-29, Opus, after the server service-time measurement). This bead's premise = server-side posix_SPAWN to skip the fork+checkin GATE. That is still correctly dead: M2 proved spawn-of-a-real-macho is dominated by per-process startup CPU that exists no matter who creates the process, so moving spawn server-side can't beat the 1500us gate. NOTHING in the new data changes that. What the new measurement DOES re-open is a DIFFERENT lever — server-side / template-replay of the libSystem INIT round-trips (the ~90%-empty-round-trip RPC half of startup, ~1.5ms/spawn). That lever lives in perf#15/.27 (the measurement) and perf#17/.25.1 (the cache/zygote idea), NOT here. Keeping .23 closed so its wrong cost-model premise isn't resurrected; the live work is .27 + .25.1.

title: perf #12-investigation: WHY does a cloned mldr child need ~2.8ms to first-run?
description: BLOCKER for perf#12-A (.22 pool) and perf#12-C (.23 posix_spawn). The perf#12 two-sided instrumentation (bead .21) pinned ~87% of the per-fork ~2.85ms to the freshly-cloned child's Linux scheduling latency to first-run + complete its first cross-process checkin round-trip (NOT server compute=53us, NOT child setup=370us/11%, NOT recv reply-wakeup -- a 20M-spin budget made it WORSE). GOAL: pin WHERE inside that ~2.8ms the time goes, cheaply, before committing to any architectural fix. Investigate: (1) clone-return -> child-first-scheduled (bare scheduler placement latency of a newly cloned task -- instrument the child's very first instruction tick vs the parent's clone-return tick, cross-process via shared mem); (2) does __mldr_postfork_child BLOCK on anything (locks, the loader, a syscall) -- the perf#9 'mldr reset glibc loader locks after raw fork' patch touches this area; (3) CPU affinity / topology -- is the child waking on a cold/different core, is the server pinned, does taskset/affinity change it; (4) scheduler tunables (sched_wakeup_granularity, migration cost, CONFIG_PREEMPT) -- cheap A/B; (5) is the ~2.8ms actually the cross-process FUTEX/socket wakeup of the parent's fork_wait_for_child rather than the child running. DELIVERABLE: a cheap experiment (no architectural change) attributing the 2.8ms across scheduler-placement vs mldr-postfork-block vs cross-process-wakeup, so we know whether a low-risk knob (affinity/tunable) suffices or a pool/spawn-fastpath is truly needed. Reusable probe pattern + instrumented backups in ~/work/dar-6x4-repro (hispin, fork-child-timeline dylib 98373b8e, timeline server 3baaee34).
close_reason: Root cause PROVEN by two-sided gated instrumentation: ~2.85ms/fork = SERVER-SIDE INTAKE/QUEUE LATENCY -- the freshly-forked child's checkin datagram waits ~2.8ms in the single-threaded darlingserver listener queue before the event loop services it (server then handles in 53us; child completes within 124us). NOT transport/scheduling/reply-wakeup/server-compute. Fix lever = server intake responsiveness (perf#2 re-scoped to checkin intake) or sidestep via pool(.22)/posix_spawn(.23). Evidence in comments + memory.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
INVESTIGATION FINDINGS (Opus 2026-06-28). Three cheap experiments on the prod lineage (server a13ec2ae / dylib e5cd6b12):

(1) child-first-USER-instr (sched-probe.c, shared MAP_ANON page): p50=3643us -- but this is AFTER the full sys_fork incl checkin (sys_fork doesn't return to main until checkin done). Combined with the in-fork child timeline (bead .21: clone->checkin-send = postfork 63us + guardpf 115us + sockrefresh 170us = ~370us), the child IS scheduled and running within ~370us of clone. So NOT a scheduler-placement-to-first-run problem.

(2) freshly-forked child's FIRST POST-CHECKIN RPC (firstrpc.c: time mach_reply_port in the child right after checkin): p50=61us, max 208us -- essentially the normal warm floor (~32us). So once checkin completes, the young child's IPC is FAST. The slowness is NOT 'young child IPC/transport is slow' and NOT scheduling.

(3) Topology: AMD Ryzen AI 9 HX 370, 12 logical (6 core x2 SMT), kernel 6.8 EEVDF (no sched_wakeup_granularity/migration_cost knobs).

=> NARROWED: the ~2.9ms is SPECIFIC TO THE CHECKIN HANDSHAKE itself, measured in the CHILD's own dserver_rpc_checkin call (child timeline checkin_rtt=2902us), while the server handles it in 53us and the child's very NEXT rpc is 61us. The distinguishing feature of checkin: it is the FIRST message on a BRAND-NEW per-thread socket the child just created (socket()+connect() in __dserver_per_thread_socket_refresh), to the server's shared listener socket. Hypotheses to test next (cheap): (a) the server accepting/registering the new client connection is deferred (epoll on listener wakes late, or new-conn association is slow); (b) the parent's fork_wait_for_child semaphore wakeup path (dtape_semaphore_up -> microthread resume) is the ~2.8ms, and the child's checkin reply is gated behind it; (c) the checkin reply specifically waits for the parent to be notified (notifyCheckin notifies parent BEFORE _sendReply returns?). NEXT: re-deploy the DSERVER_FORK_TIMELINE server + add a server-side stamp at message-RECEIPT (recvmmsg) vs ctor-enter to measure the QUEUE-WAIT gap (message arrived at server -> server processes it), which neither probe has captured yet.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
SEND/RECV SPLIT (Opus 2026-06-28, instrumented dylib 05adda4a: rdtsc counters in the RPC send/recv hooks bounded to the checkin call). Of the ~2.9ms checkin round-trip (52 samples): checkin_SEND=5.7% (~177us), checkin_RECV-wait=93.5% (~2933us), unaccounted 0.8%. recv_spins=512 EXACTLY THE BUDGET every time -> the child spins the full 512 non-blocking MSG_DONTWAIT polls catching NOTHING, then falls through to BLOCKING recvmsg and sleeps ~2.9ms for the reply.

Cross-referenced with the server-side timeline (earlier in this bead): the server processes the checkin in ~53us and its forktimeline log line lands within ~100-300us of the child's forkchild line (when checkin returns). So the reply is produced ~instantly ONCE THE SERVER RECEIVES the request -- but the child waits ~2.9ms in blocking recvmsg before that happens.

INESCAPABLE CONCLUSION (corrected -- supersedes the earlier 'cloned-child scheduling latency' read): the ~2.9ms is SERVER-SIDE QUEUE/INTAKE LATENCY -- the checkin datagram sits in the server's listener socket UNPROCESSED for ~2.9ms because the single-threaded darlingserver event loop is busy/blocked elsewhere (handling the previous fork's microthread work, or its epoll/recvmmsg not serviced promptly under the fork storm). NOT transport, NOT cloned-child scheduling (the child runs within ~370us and its send takes only ~177us), NOT recv reply-wakeup latency per se (the reply isn't even produced yet -- the server hasn't seen the request). This finally explains why recv-spin (perf#7) and even hispin=20M were neutral/worse: there is nothing to spin-catch because the server hasn't replied. NEXT: confirm with a server-side stamp at recvmmsg-return (datagram arrival) vs callFromMessage/ctor-enter to measure the intake-queue gap directly; the fix lever is server-side concurrency/intake (process checkin datagrams promptly even while a microthread is mid-work) -- which points back at perf#2 server-MT / event-loop responsiveness, re-scoped to 'don't let one in-flight microthread delay intake of the next checkin by ms'. Instrumented split dylib 05adda4a + timeline server 3baaee34 deployed for this measurement; prod a13ec2ae/e5cd6b12/025aa772 backed up.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
PROVEN (server-vs-child wall-clock correlation, 52 paired PIDs): the gap from server PROCESSING the checkin (forktimeline log) to the child LOGGING completion (forkchild log) is p50=124us, max=419us -- TINY vs the child's checkin_recv ~2933us. So the child blocks ~2.8ms BEFORE the server processes its checkin; once processed, completion is ~124us. 

>>> FINAL ROOT CAUSE (perf#12), proven by both-sided instrumentation: the ~2.85ms per-fork cost is SERVER-SIDE INTAKE/QUEUE LATENCY -- the freshly-forked child's checkin datagram waits ~2.8ms in the single-threaded darlingserver listener queue before the event loop services it. NOT transport, NOT cloned-child scheduling (child runs <370us post-clone, its next RPC is 61us), NOT RPC reply-wakeup (recv-spin 512 catches nothing, hispin 20M is worse), NOT server compute (processes in 53us). The single-threaded event loop simply doesn't pick up the new checkin promptly during/around the fork sequence.

This finally reconciles everything: perf#7 recv-spin neutral on fork (nothing to spin-catch -- server hasn't replied because it hasn't RECEIVED), perf#6 '57% recvmsg-wait + 39% reaping' (the recvmsg-wait IS this intake-queue block; reaping is the parent idle-waiting for these slow children). FIX LEVER = server-side INTAKE RESPONSIVENESS: service the listener socket / new checkins promptly even while a microthread is mid-work. This is perf#2 server-MT / event-loop territory ([[dar-perf2-server-multithread-scope]]) RE-SCOPED narrowly to checkin intake (NOT general dtape parallelism, which perf#2 showed regresses). The pre-warmed-pool (.22) and posix_spawn-fastpath (.23) candidates both SIDESTEP this by avoiding the per-fork checkin entirely -- still valid, possibly simpler than making the event loop preemptible. INVESTIGATION COMPLETE -- ready to unblock .22/.23 and/or open a perf#2-intake bead. All instrumentation gated+uncommitted; restoring prod.

title: perf #13: per-process dyld/libSystem STARTUP is the real build lever (perf#12 re-scope after M2)
description: RE-SCOPE FRAMING bead (2026-06-29, Opus), opened from the perf#12 .23 M2 decision point. M2 PROVED the process-CREATION cost model wrong: spawning a trivial Mach-O costs ~9044us on prod (spawn-latency-gate, +reap); decomposed = fork-return 2788us (clone+checkin chain) + ~6256us exec+mldr+dyld+libSystem-init+run+exit+reap. The ~6.3ms is per-process STARTUP (dyld + libSystem/mach_init), IRREDUCIBLE by any process-creation change (server-side spawn .23, warm-pool .22, futex handoff all cannot touch it -- proven). For make/clang builds (thousands of short-lived tool invocations) per-process startup, NOT fork/checkin, dominates wall-clock. The fork-checkin chain (~2.8ms) is real but is only ~31% of a spawn and is mostly cross-process scheduling-wakeup latency (perf#12 root cause), not removable guest-side. THIS bead groups the startup-cost children below (perf #14/#15/#16). DECISION still open: confirm with user which to pursue. Evidence + full decomposition: bead .23 M2 comment + memory dar-perf12-fork-checkin-cost. Constraint: nothing pushed without approval; red-TDD.

title: perf #17: snapshot/cache per-binary process-init keyed by (binary-hash + args + env) — replay a warm process image instead of re-running dyld+libSystem init
description:
USER IDEA (2026-06-29): for the build hot path the SAME tool (clang) is spawned thousands of times with near-identical setup; each spawn re-runs the full ~6.3ms dyld+libSystem startup from scratch. IDEA: cache/snapshot the RESULT of process initialization — if the binary content-hash + argv + relevant env are unchanged from a prior run, REPLAY a pre-initialized process image (post-dyld, post-libSystem_initializer, paused at entry to real work) instead of re-doing init. A memoized 'warm process template' per (hash,args,env).

DISTINCT from the other startup beads: .22 warm-pool keeps GENERIC pre-forked helpers but each still execs+inits a fresh macho -> re-pays the 6.3ms; THIS caches the POST-INIT state per specific binary so a cache HIT skips init entirely. .28 compiler-server keeps clang resident but only helps clang + needs a clang server mode (clang-9 has none); THIS is binary-AGNOSTIC (any repeatedly-spawned macho: clang/ld/sh/conftest) and needs no cooperation from the tool. .26 dyld shared-cache only amortizes load/rebind (~12-21%); THIS can skip the WHOLE init incl. the libSystem_initializer (the dominant ~57%/1.5ms slice, 64% RPC per .27).

MECHANISM CANDIDATES (all HARD): (a) FORK-FROM-WARM-TEMPLATE (Android Zygote model): keep one already-initialized instance of each hot binary suspended right after libSystem init / at main() entry; fork() a fresh child from it per request (COW) — child inherits the initialized address space, skips dyld+init. Per-binary template pool keyed by hash+args. (b) CRIU-style checkpoint/restore of the post-init image. (c) snapshot the dtape task/thread + mapped image server-side and clone it.
CORRECTNESS RISKS: post-init state that must NOT be shared/replayed — pid/task-self port identity, RNG/ASLR seeds, open fds, mach port rights, env-derived state computed during init, addresses baked into the init'd image. Cache key must capture EVERYTHING init reads (argv, env subset, binary hash, cwd?, umask?) or replay diverges. macOS-fidelity of fork-from-template (signal disposition, pgroup) = same hazards as .22. INVALIDATION: content-hash the binary (+ linked dylibs / libSystem version) so a toolchain update busts the cache.
PRIOR ART: Android Zygote, CRIU, Emacs unexec/pdump, V8 snapshot, Firecracker/Lambda snapshot-restore. Zygote (a) is closest fit and avoids re-doing the 64%-RPC libSystem chain (.27).
SCOPE: (1) confirm the per-spawn-varying post-init state is small/capturable — ESPECIALLY whether the 64%-RPC libSystem init creates process-specific SERVER-SIDE port/task state that a forked template would alias incorrectly (overlaps the server-side-init concern). (2) prototype a single-binary zygote (one warm suspended clang, fork per compile) + measure compile vs cold spawn. (3) decide cache-key + invalidation. HIGH effort, HIGH risk (aliased Mach state), potentially BIGGEST win (skips the dominant init slice for EVERY hot binary). DEPENDS ON the server-side-init service-time measurement (filed alongside) to know how much init is empty round-trips (cheap to replay) vs real per-process server state (must be rebuilt). Constraint: nothing pushed; red-TDD.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: SERVICE-TIME MEASUREMENT supports this bead (2026-06-29, Opus; full data in .27 comment). The startup RPCs are ~90% empty round-trip latency: server services each in 4-16us but the guest waits ~100-120us. So the libSystem init the guest re-runs every spawn is dominated by socket round-trip WAKEUP, not server work. A replayed warm-template (zygote) that already HOLDS the post-init Mach state would skip those ~1.5ms of round-trips entirely.
