CRITICAL caveat this measurement also surfaces: those round-trips BUILD process-specific server-side state (task-self/thread/reply ports). A forked-from-template child must NOT alias the template's port rights — the server would have to re-mint per-child task/thread/port state (the cheap 4-16us work) while the child reuses only the address-space init (malloc arenas, dyld-bound images, libSystem globals). So the win is: replay the ADDRESS-SPACE init (the expensive-by-round-trip part) but re-establish the per-process Mach identity server-side (cheap). This is the key feasibility question for scope-step (1).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Priority P1->P2 (2026-06-29, Opus): perf#18/.30 (shmem ring transport) is now the chosen FIRST lever — it attacks the same ~90%-empty-round-trip cost this bead would, but as a faster TRANSPORT, which is lower-risk (no aliased Mach port rights, no fork-from-template semantics) and speeds EVERY RPC not just startup. Keep this open as the next lever if the transport win is insufficient or if removing the round-trips ENTIRELY (server-side/replayed init) is still warranted after the transport is fast.

title: perf #14: dyld shared-cache / prebinding so spawned tools don't re-resolve libSystem from scratch
description: Child of the perf#13 startup-cost re-scope (bead .25). HYPOTHESIS: the dominant ~6.3ms/spawn (M2 decomposition) is per-process dyld work -- resolving + mapping libSystem and its dependency graph from scratch in every spawned tool. On real macOS this is amortized by the dyld SHARED CACHE (a prebuilt, pre-linked image of the system dylibs mapped in one shot). Darling guest dyld likely does NOT use a shared cache (each process re-opens/re-binds the dylibs). LEVER: build/enable a dyld shared cache for the guest system dylibs, or a lighter prebinding, so each spawned process maps a prelinked blob instead of re-resolving. SCOPE TASKS: (1) instrument a single guest process startup to split the ~6ms: dyld map/bind vs libSystem_initializer vs mach_init RPCs (rdtsc markers in dyld + libSystem init, gated). (2) confirm whether guest dyld already has any cache. (3) estimate the prebind/shared-cache effort vs payoff. RED gate = a single-process 'time to main()' probe (not spawn-latency-gate, which conflates fork). HIGH potential payoff, MED-HIGH effort, touches dyld (sensitive). Verify the split FIRST before committing -- don't assume dyld dominates the 6ms vs libSystem init. Constraint: nothing pushed without approval; red-TDD.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: perf#14 MEASURED 2026-06-29 (Opus), DYLD_PRINT_STATISTICS on a trivial guest Mach-O, prod (server a13ec2ae/dylib e5cd6b12), N=8 stable. Total pre-main ~2.96ms. Split: dylib loading ~30% (0.88ms), rebase/binding ~8% (0.24ms), ObjC ~4%, INITIALIZER ~57% (1.70ms) of which libSystem.B.dylib ALONE ~50% (~1.48ms). DETAILS: '39 images loaded, 0 from dyld shared cache' => Darling uses NO shared cache; building one from scratch is large and its CEILING is only the loading+rebase+binding buckets ~1.1-1.9ms of a ~9ms spawn (~12-21%). VERDICT: DE-PRIORITIZE. The real in-startup lever is the libSystem INITIALIZER = .27/perf#15, independent of any shared cache. Also: 'redo shared cached bindings 0.40ms (12%)' is paid DESPITE no cache (unconditional rebind pass) = cheap independent probe target. Harness ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/run-startup-decomp.sh.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: CROSS-CHECK from perf#16/.28 Step-1 (2026-06-29, Opus): the .28 per-exec measurement confirms this bead's de-prioritization. Real-tool spawn floor = 9.36ms (/usr/bin/true), matching M2. A dyld shared cache attacks only the load/rebind buckets (~1.1ms, ~12% of a spawn) at large effort, while the initializer (libSystem ~1.5ms, 64% RPC per .27) is the bigger startup slice and is NOT touched by a shared cache. Keep .26 DE-PRIORITIZED. Net: every per-process / guest-side startup lever (.26 dyld-cache, .27 libSystem cache/batch) is measured and capped/ruled-out; the only lever that removes the per-tool startup tax at all is NOT spawning a fresh process per compile (.28 persistent worker / .22 warm pool), both of which the guest's clang-9 (no server mode) makes high-effort. Decision is the user's.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Priority P1->P3 (2026-06-29, Opus): two measurements now agree this is the weakest startup lever — DYLD_PRINT_STATISTICS gives a shared-cache ceiling of only ~12-21% of a spawn at large effort, and the service-time measurement shows the bigger startup slice (the libSystem initializer, ~1.7ms, 64% RPC ~90% empty round-trip) is untouched by a shared cache. Lead candidates are now .25.1 (perf#17 template-replay) and .28 (compiler-server).

title: perf #15: trim libSystem_initializer + mach_init RPC chatter at process start
description: Child of the perf#13 startup-cost re-scope (bead .25). HYPOTHESIS: part of the ~6.3ms/spawn startup is the serial RPC chatter every guest process does during libSystem_initializer -> mach_init_doit (mach_reply_port, host/task/thread self-traps, set_dyld_info, set_executable_path, vchroot_path, etc). perf#7 (cache task_self_trap, .18), perf#10 (NO_REPLY set_dyld_info/set_executable_path, .17) and perf#3a (fuse checkin+thread_self_trap, .12) already shaved some -- this bead is the REMAINDER at process START specifically (not steady-state). SCOPE: per-callnum RPC accounting of a single process from exec to main() (reuse the perf#9 DARLING_RPC_SLEEP_ACCOUNT machinery), identify which startup RPCs remain serial+synchronous, batch/cache/NO_REPLY them. Depends on the perf#14 split to know how much of the 6ms is RPC vs pure dyld map/bind -- if RPC chatter is a small fraction, this is low-value. LOW-MED effort, MED risk (process-start ordering is delicate). RED gate = time-to-main() probe + per-callnum count. Constraint: nothing pushed without approval; red-TDD.
close_reason: DIAGNOSIS COMPLETE 2026-06-29 (Opus). Measured (not inferred) startup RPC-vs-CPU + per-callnum + batchability. Verdict: libSystem init wall ~2.7-3.0ms is 64% RPC / 36% CPU; the startup RPCs are a 0%-batchable sequential Mach-bootstrap chain; cache/NO_REPLY (A) and batch (C) both ruled out by measurement. No cheap in-startup fix exists -- the only lever is B-class (don't run the per-step guest handshake): .23 server-side spawn, .22 warm pool, .28 compiler-server. Tool kept as gate (.29). All diagnostic, gated OFF, nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: PROMOTED to top in-startup lever 2026-06-29 (Opus) per perf#14 (.26): of ~2.96ms pre-main, libSystem.B.dylib initializer ALONE = ~1.48ms (~50%); shared-cache (.26) ceiling only ~12-21%. Removed the bogus '.26 blocks .27' dep -- .27 is INDEPENDENT of a shared cache. NEXT: instrument the libSystem_initializer sub-chain (init.c:184 kernel/platform/pthread/libc/malloc/keymgr/dyld/libdispatch/libxpc/libtrace/secinit/containermgr/darwin) to find which sub-inits carry the 1.48ms and how many are SYNC RPC round-trips.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: MEASURED 2026-06-29 (Opus): libSystem_initializer sub-chain decomposed via gated rdtsc markers (DARLING_LIBSYSTEM_INIT_TIMELINE), prod, N=10, calibrated ~1857 ticks/us against dyld's own initializer-time. Of the ~1.69ms init: _libc_initializer 444us (26%), __pthread_init 436us (26%), libdispatch_init 290us (17%) = top-3 ~70%; __libkernel_init/mach_init only 162us (10%), __malloc_init 124us, __malloc_late_init 108us, _libxpc 86us; rest <1%. VERDICT (mostly NEGATIVE for the simple thesis): mach_init -- the only obviously-Darling-RPC phase -- is just ~10%; perf#9/#10 already trimmed RPCs and there's no big remaining RPC pile here. The dominant ~70% is pthread+libc+libdispatch = upstream-Apple CPU-bound init; trimming = HIGH-risk hacking of Apple initializers for LOW Darling-specific leverage, and zeroing mach_init's 162us moves a ~9ms spawn by only ~2%. NET perf#12 re-scope: per-process startup (~3ms pre-main) is not cheaply reducible (dyld .26 no-cache ceiling ~12-21%; initializers .27 CPU-bound). Highest-leverage remaining = .28/perf#16 compiler-server/persistent-worker (amortize startup across a build, orthogonal to Darling). Recommend keeping .27 OPEN-but-LOW unless a per-phase RPC-vs-compute split (perf#9 accountant in the dylib) is wanted. Harness ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/run-lsinit-timeline.sh.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RPC-vs-compute split ATTEMPTED 2026-06-29 (Opus), NOT obtained -- 3 instrumentation paths each hit build/runtime plumbing, not the question. (1) Cross-dylib RPC counter (init.c reads a libsystem_kernel symbol): a NEW symbol -- data OR function, even visibility(default)+used+exported-as-T -- does NOT resolve through libSystem's two-level-namespace re-export; guest aborts at boot 'Symbol not found ... Expected in libSystem.B.dylib'. Existing externs (__simple_printf, mach_init) work only because they were already in libSystem's import table. (2) Existing DARLING_RPC_SLEEP_ACCOUNT (per-callnum reply-wait, dumps at sys_exit): rebuilt kernel+libSystem with the flag (verified define reaches both rpc.c and the table TU) + redirected the log to /Users/ilyagulya via -DDARLING_RPC_SLEEP_ACCOUNT_PATH; the dump NEVER wrote (guest's own cat empty, find under prefix empty, host /tmp stale) -- suspect the perf#9 dual-table split (rpc.c compiled into BOTH emulation and emulation_dyld; helper's wrappers may fill the emulation_dyld table while sys_exit dumps the emulation one) OR the trivial helper's exit doesn't reach this sys_exit. (3) strace: the helper is spawned by the persistent darlingserver, NOT a child of the straced , so 0 RPC syscalls visible. SOLID finding stands (libc 444us/pthread 436us/dispatch 290us = ~70%, mach_init ~10%) but whether those 3 are RPC-removable or genuine CPU is STILL OPEN. To resolve next: do the counter+dump ENTIRELY inside libsystem_kernel (no new cross-dylib symbol) -- phase marks set via an already-exported call, dump from the existing sys_exit path after confirming the dual-table issue. All reverted, prod restored, nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
RPC-vs-compute split OBTAINED 2026-06-29 (Opus) via the internal per-phase RPC counter (DARLING_RPC_PHASE_COUNT) -- the cross-dylib trap is fully sidestepped. DESIGN: counter __dar_rpc_phase_total (LOCAL symbol, single def in new rpc-phase-count.c, compiled once into emulation lib) bumped by the RPC send hook (dserver-rpc-defs.h); init.c emits '[lsphase] <name>' via __simple_printf ONLY (the one already-resolved cross-boundary symbol); __simple_printf itself (in libsystem_kernel) recognizes the sentinel and snapshots the per-phase delta to stdout (fd1, no RPC). init.c needs NO new symbol -> NO boot brick. Both gated OFF by default (byte-identical baseline). Boot CLEAN, N=8 runs DETERMINISTIC (min==max every phase).

RESULT -- total 15 RPC sends across the WHOLE libSystem init chain: KERNEL(mach_init)=2, PTHREAD=6, LIBC=5, LIBDISPATCH=1, LIBXPC=1, ALL OTHER PHASES=0 (PLATFORM/MALLOC/KEYMGR/DYLD/PTHREAD_LATE/LIBTRACE/DARWIN/MALLOC_LATE/KERNEL_LATE).

VERDICT -- the user's challenge ('sure there is nothing to cut inside these?') is now ANSWERED, and the answer is NEGATIVE for the dominant phases, now PROVEN per-phase not inferred: (1) libdispatch (290us, #3 phase) issues EXACTLY 1 RPC -> at most ~200us removable, and that single init RPC is already the floor; ~90us is pure compute. (2) libc(444us)=5 RPCs and pthread(436us)=6 RPCs: at the pessimistic perf#9 200us/RPC these would be 1000us/1200us -- but the MEASURED wall-clock is far less, and total-init RPC at 200us each = 3000us > the entire ~2960us pre-main, which is IMPOSSIBLE. So these startup RPCs are NOT 200us slow-path round-trips -- the perf#3 recv-spin is catching them at ~8-40us (server p50 ~8us). The bulk of pthread/libc/dispatch init is GENUINE upstream-Apple CPU, not removable Darling RPC. mach_init's 2 RPCs already trimmed by perf#7/#10. CONCLUSION stands: no cheap RPC pile to cut in startup; highest remaining leverage = .28/perf#16 compiler-server (amortize startup across a build). Evidence ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/rpcphase-result-1782700546.txt + run-rpcphase.sh. All diagnostic, gated OFF; prod being restored; nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FINAL, MEASURED (not inferred) 2026-06-29 (Opus). The earlier count-only entry was WRONG and is RETRACTED -- it counted 15 RPC sends and INFERRED they were cheap (circular). Extended the counter to MEASURE rdtsc time IN the RPC path (send + reply-wait) per phase AND per callnum, calibrated ~1074 ticks/us against dyld's own pre-main ms (independent clock, self-checking). N=8 deterministic.

RESULT: of ~2.7-3.0ms init wall, **64% is RPC (~1.7-1.9ms), only 36% CPU**. Per phase: PTHREAD 92% RPC, LIBC 85%, KERNEL(mach_init) 80%, LIBXPC 91%; only LIBDISPATCH (23% RPC / rest malloc CPU) and MALLOC/MALLOC_LATE (100% CPU) are genuinely compute. So the dominant phases are RPC-bound, NOT CPU -- the opposite of my retracted conclusion. By callnum (us/run): mach_msg_overwrite 542-609 (4x, real IPC), mach_reply_port 216-232 (2x), mach_port_deallocate 205-236 (2x), host_self_trap 201-223 (2x), vchroot_path 108-128, uidgid 108-116, set_thread_handles 101-114, task_self_trap 98-117, thread_self_trap 96-111.

WHY no cheap fix (each ruled out by inspection, not guess): task_self_trap+uidgid ALREADY cached (the 1 measured call is the mandatory first fetch); host_self_trap caching REJECTED by perf#7 (result is caller-deallocated, macOS contract); mach_msg_overwrite is genuine Mach IPC (real work); mach_port_deallocate NO_REPLY would drop its error-abort check AND is global (not just startup). So Variant A (cache/NO_REPLY) is ~empty.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
BATCHABILITY MEASURED 2026-06-29 (Opus) -- KILLS the batch idea (Variant C / bead .17). Added a per-RPC sequence trace: for each startup RPC, gap_before = CPU ticks between the PRIOR reply landing and THIS send. A near-zero gap = back-to-back = batch candidate; a large gap = the program used the prior reply to compute this RPC's args = a data-dependency BARRIER a batch cannot cross. RESULT (representative run, 15 RPCs): EVERY adjacent pair has 20-445us of CPU between them -- 0 back-to-back, **0% of the 1189us in non-first RPCs is batchable, 100% sits behind a barrier**. The startup RPCs are a SEQUENTIAL Mach-bootstrap chain (task_self -> reply_port -> host_self -> msg_overwrite uses both -> ...), each step depending on the previous. You cannot collapse them into one round-trip.

=> CONCLUSION: NO cheap (A) and NO medium-safe (C) startup fix exists -- both ruled out by MEASUREMENT, not opinion. The only remaining lever is B-class = do NOT make the guest run the per-step server handshake at all: server-side spawn/pre-seed (.23), warm pool (.22), or amortize via compiler-server (.28). This MATCHES perf#12's root cause (cross-process per-step handshake; only removable by not-creating/ reusing the guest process). All B-class are weeks-scale / high-risk per perf#12 -- user decision required. Evidence ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/rpcphase-MEASURED-*.txt + run-rpcphase.sh. Recommend: keep .27 as the DIAGNOSIS bead (now complete), pursue the fix under .23/.22/.28.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== SERVER-SIDE SERVICE-TIME MEASUREMENT (answers 'почему закрыт .23 как dead-end' + 'что с инициализацией в сервере') — 2026-06-29 (Opus) ===

QUESTION: of the MEASURED 64%-RPC libSystem startup, how much is EMPTY round-trip latency (would vanish if the server answered itself / a template replayed it) vs REAL server-side CPU work (must be rebuilt, can't be skipped)? perf#15 proved it's RPC by guest-side TIME but never measured the SERVER's service time per call.

METHOD (no new code): the prod server a13ec2ae already has perf#9's per-call SERVICE-TIME histograms (metrics.cpp per_call) exposed over the darlingserver-stat: abstract socket. Clean-booted, read per_call via tools/darling-stat after a /usr/bin/true (startup-only RPC mix).

RESULT — guest reply-WAIT per call (.27) vs SERVER service p50:
  mach_reply_port      guest ~108us  | server  8us
  host_self_trap       guest ~105us  | server  8us
  task_self_trap       guest ~117us  | server  8us
  thread_self_trap     guest ~104us  | server  8us
  vchroot_path         guest ~118us  | server 16us
  uidgid               guest ~116us  | server  4us
  set_thread_handles   guest ~108us  | server  4us
Server-wide rpc_latency at boot: mean 5us / p50 4us / p95 16us over 1947 RPCs.

=> The server spends 4-16us SERVICING each startup call, but the guest WAITS ~100-120us. **~90%+ of each startup RPC is EMPTY ROUND-TRIP LATENCY** (socket sendmsg + recvmsg + cross-process scheduler WAKEUP), NOT server work. This is the SAME wakeup-latency finding as perf#12 (the fork/checkin chain) and perf#6 (57% __skb_wait reply-sleep) — now confirmed for the STARTUP RPC mix specifically.

IMPLICATIONS:
1. WHY .23 was closed (correct at the time, on M2's model): M2 measured spawn=9ms as a black box and called the ~6.3ms startup 'irreducible, exists no matter who creates the process'. TRUE for the CPU part. But this measurement shows the startup's RPC half is ~90% empty round-trips — so an init that runs WITHOUT the guest<->server socket hop (server-side init, or replayed-template init) could reclaim most of that ~1.7ms RPC time. M2 didn't decompose service-vs-wait, so its 'irreducible' verdict was too strong for the RPC portion. The CPU portion (malloc, dispatch, real dyld bind ~1ms+) IS still irreducible.
2. CEILING of removing the round-trips: the startup RPC reply-WAIT is ~1.7ms (64% of ~2.7ms init); the server WORK underneath is only ~9 calls x ~4-16us ~= well under 0.2ms. So in principle ~1.5ms of the ~2.7ms libSystem init is reclaimable IF init happens without the per-call socket round-trip. That is a ~50% cut of libSystem init = ~1.5ms off a ~9ms spawn (~17%) PER PROCESS, on every spawned tool.
3. BUT the catch (the real .23 wall): you only avoid the round-trip if init runs in the SAME address space/thread as the server (server-side) OR is REPLAYED from a warm template (perf#17/.25.1). Both mean the process-specific Mach state (task-self port, thread ports, reply ports) is built without the guest asking — i.e. exactly the synchronous server-side task/thread construction .23 scoped as multi-week/high-risk (touches dtape, dar-l8k/dar-6x4 exec-path fixes). The service-time being tiny (4-16us) is GOOD NEWS for that: the server-side WORK to build this state is cheap; the cost was never the work, only the round-trip. So a server-side or template path is bounded by ~0.2ms of real work + whatever the address-space/correctness machinery costs.

NET: server-side init (or perf#17 template-replay) could reclaim ~1.5ms/spawn (~17%) that M2 wrote off as irreducible. It is NOT free (same high-risk Mach-state-construction as .23) but the payoff is real and the server-side WORK is proven cheap. This re-opens server-side init as a candidate worth a prototype, alongside perf#17. Measurement only; prod a13ec2ae untouched, nothing pushed.

title: perf #16: compiler-server / persistent-worker model for make+clang (avoid per-tool startup entirely)
description: Child of the perf#13 startup-cost re-scope (bead .25). BIGGEST potential build-wall-clock win, ORTHOGONAL to Darling internals. A make -jN build of a C project forks THOUSANDS of short-lived tool invocations (clang, ld, conftest, shell). Each pays the full ~9ms Darling spawn (mostly ~6ms dyld/libSystem startup per M2). The startup cost is irreducible PER PROCESS -- so the win is to not start a fresh process per compile: a persistent compiler-server / worker pool that stays warm (dyld+libSystem already loaded) and services many compile requests, amortizing startup across the whole build. PRIOR ART: clang has -cc1 server modes; ccache/distcc-style daemons; Bazel persistent workers; sccache. SCOPE: (1) measure how many fork+exec a representative build does and the per-exec startup share. (2) evaluate whether a drop-in (ccache/sccache, or a clang persistent-worker wrapper) running INSIDE the guest avoids the per-tool startup. (3) prototype + measure end-to-end build wall-clock vs baseline. This may need NO Darling source changes at all (just guest-side tooling/config) = lowest-risk highest-leverage. MED effort, LOW Darling-internals risk. Strongest candidate to bring to the user first. Constraint: nothing pushed without approval.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: JUSTIFIED by perf#15/.27 measurement 2026-06-29 (Opus): per-process startup wall ~2.7-3.0ms is MEASURED 64% RPC (~1.7-1.9ms), and those startup RPCs are a 0%-batchable sequential Mach-bootstrap chain (every adjacent pair has 20-445us CPU between -> data-dependency barriers). Cheap fixes (cache/NO_REPLY=A, batch=C) are ruled out BY MEASUREMENT. This bead is now on the critical path as one of the only remaining startup levers. Use the .29 per-phase RPC accountant (DARLING_RPC_PHASE_COUNT) as the RED->GREEN A/B gate. Still weeks-scale/high-risk per perf#12 -- user decision pending.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== STEP 1 (measure) DONE — prove-or-kill MEASURED in-guest, 2026-06-29 (Opus). Thesis CONFIRMED; but the cheap drop-in is NOT available here. ===

GUEST TOOLCHAIN: clang = Apple LLVM 9.0.0 (clang-900, 2017), make = GNU 3.81, dtrace present, NO ccache/sccache, brew only a renamed backup (not usable as-is). The darling clang WRAPPER drives cc1+ld in-process (one exec per compile; no separate ld/as exec observed).

PER-EXEC COST (mach_absolute_time, posix_spawn+wait, median of N=30, prod libs a13ec2ae, caches warmed):
  /usr/bin/true (trivial macho)   9.36 ms   <- pure process-startup FLOOR (clone+checkin + dyld + libSystem)
  /bin/sh -c 'exit 0'            10.00 ms
  clang --version                18.53 ms   <- startup + clang driver init, ~0 compile work
  clang -c (compile 1 TU)        32.74 ms   <- startup + real compile
  clang compile+link             69.60 ms
=> STARTUP SHARE of a real compile = ~9.4ms / 32.7ms = ~29% irreducible per-process startup BEFORE clang does any useful work. For the trivial conftest probes that dominate ./configure (compile work ~0), startup is ~50%+ of each invocation. The 9.4ms floor MATCHES M2's ~9ms spawn-of-trivial-macho exactly -> it IS the dyld+libSystem startup, irreducible PER PROCESS (confirms .25/perf#13).

REPRESENTATIVE BUILD (40-conftest configure-storm + 12 TU compile + link): 54 clang execs, 4256ms wall. ~54 x 9.4ms = ~507ms (~12%) is pure startup here (compile-heavy skews it down); a real ./configure of hundreds of trivial probes would be DOMINATED by the floor.

VERDICT: the .28 thesis (per-process startup is a large recurring tax worth removing) is TRUE. BUT the bead's hoped-for lowest-risk path -- a drop-in ccache/sccache or a clang persistent-worker -- is NOT viable on this guest: (a) ccache/sccache not installed and brew unusable; (b) ccache wouldn't even remove the startup tax -- each cache hit still spawns a process paying the 9.4ms floor; it removes RE-compute on incremental rebuilds, orthogonal to a cold build's startup; (c) this clang (v9) has NO -cc1 server / persistent-worker mode, so a true compiler-server needs a newer toolchain or a bespoke wrapper = HIGH effort.

KEY REDIRECT: the 9.4ms floor is the SAME dyld+libSystem startup that perf#14 (.26, dyld shared-cache / prebinding) targets. Attacking the FLOOR makes EVERY spawned tool faster -- transparent to make/clang, no per-tool wrapper, no toolchain rebuild, no compiler-server. That is strictly lower-risk and higher-leverage than a persistent-worker here. RECOMMENDATION: pursue perf#14 (.26 dyld shared-cache) as the actual B-class build first; keep .28 (compiler-server) as a later/orthogonal win that needs a newer toolchain. Bringing this to the user for the go/no-go. Harness: ~/dar-perf16 (spawnbench.c, measure2.sh, make-build.sh). Prod untouched (server a13ec2ae, submodules on base branches, 0 dirty), nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Priority P1->P2 (2026-06-29, Opus): perf#18/.30 (shmem ring transport) chosen as first lever (lower-risk, binary-agnostic, no toolchain dependency — unlike this bead which needs a clang server mode clang-9 lacks). Keep open as an orthogonal later win.

title: perf #15-tool: per-phase/per-callnum/batchability RPC accountant (startup RPC-vs-CPU gate)
description: Reusable diagnostic + RED->GREEN gate built during perf#15 (.27). Internal per-phase RPC accountant in libsystem_kernel with ZERO new cross-dylib symbols (counter is a LOCAL symbol bumped by the RPC send/recv hooks; init.c drives phase boundaries via __simple_printf only -- the one symbol already resolved across libSystem's re-export; __simple_printf self-attributes the per-phase delta). Three gated metrics per libSystem-init phase: (1) RPC send COUNT, (2) rdtsc TIME in the RPC path (send + reply-wait) -> CPU = wall - rpc, (3) per-callnum breakdown, (4) per-RPC BATCHABILITY trace (gap_before = CPU between prior reply and this send => back-to-back vs data-dependency barrier). Harness calibrates ticks/us against dyld's own pre-main ms (independent clock). Gated OFF by DARLING_RPC_PHASE_COUNT (kernel) + DARLING_LIBSYSTEM_PHASE_MARK (libSystem) + env DARLING_RPCPHASE=1; byte-identical baseline when off. This is the measurement that overturned the 'startup is CPU-bound' guess (it is 64% RPC) and proved the startup RPCs are 0% batchable. KEEP as the gate to A/B any B-class startup fix (.22/.23/.28). Branches: xnu perf/rpc-phase-count-diagnostic, libsystem perf/rpc-phase-mark-diagnostic. Harness ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/run-rpcphase.sh. NOT pushed.
close_reason: DONE 2026-06-29 (Opus): tool built, deployed (kernel 4f00a570), validated N=8 deterministic across all 3 metric layers (phase RPC%, per-callnum us, batchability gaps). Produced the perf#15 verdict (startup 64% RPC, 0% batchable). Committed on branches xnu perf/rpc-phase-count-diagnostic + libsystem perf/rpc-phase-mark-diagnostic. Diagnostic-only, gated OFF, nothing pushed. Reusable as the A/B gate for any B-class startup fix.

title: perf #3: lighten the per-thread checkin RPC cost (socket reuse / fast-path)
description: MEDIUM payoff. Each new guest thread calls __mldr_create_rpc_socket (socket+dup2+fcntl+autobind) THEN a full checkin send+recv round-trip (mldr threads.c:216,238). Investigate reducing this fixed per-thread cost: (a) pool/reuse RPC sockets across short-lived threads instead of create+close each time; (b) a lighter checkin wire format / batch; (c) avoid the autobind+bind dance if a cheaper addressing works. Measure socket-setup vs RPC-roundtrip split of the ~2ms/create. Independent of #1/#2 but compounds with them. Build from darling-build. Risk MEDIUM. Nothing pushed.
close_reason: perf #3 DONE: socket-pool path rejected (10us/5%); fixed the real cost (checkin RPC round-trip) with an adaptive recv spin-then-block in mldr -> per-checkin latency 237->183us (~23%), RED->GREEN tested, integrated d8c602b70, deployed, NOT pushed. Throughput A/B noise-dominated on saturated host (caveat recorded).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: DONE 2026-06-28 (Opus), NOT pushed. Measured the per-thread create cost split FAITHFULLY in-guest (env-gated mldr instrumentation around the two real native calls): socket setup (__mldr_create_rpc_socket) ~10us (~5%) -- NOT the bottleneck, so bead option (a) socket pooling REJECTED; the checkin RPC round-trip is ~95% (~205-237us), of which the server's own work is ~8us (post perf #2b) -- the rest is the recvmsg sleep/wakeup latency. FIX = mldr's RPC recv hook (dserver-rpc-defs.h dserver_rpc_hooks_receive_message) now does a bounded NON-BLOCKING recv spin (MSG_DONTWAIT + CPU pause between polls) before the blocking recvmsg, grabbing a fast reply without sleeping; bounded so it falls through to a real blocking wait on a slow reply (symmetric to perf #1's creator-side adaptive wait). pause (not sched_yield) keeps it on-core so it doesn't donate cycles to an oversubscribed host. Default ON (DARLING_PERF3_RECVSPIN=512; =0 = legacy). RESULT: single-storm per-checkin RPC latency 237us->183us (~23%), reproduced across builds (same-run A/B cancels warmup); fork/exec/wait/pipe/exit-code 200/200 PASS; clean-boot smoke + 4000-thread storm PASS on the integrated canonical binary. CAVEAT: aggregate-throughput A/B was NOISE-DOMINATED on this oversubscribed host (load 68/12 cores, node+clickhouse) -- one ordering even showed ON slightly slower -- so the verified claim is a per-checkin LATENCY win, not a measured throughput gain; needs a clean-host throughput run + review of the default-on choice before upstreaming. RED->GREEN test committed WITH the fix (tests/perf3_recv_adaptive_test.c + runner: client voluntary ctx-switches/round-trip, blocking 0.79 FAIL -> adaptive 0.00 PASS). Branch fix/mldr-recv-adaptive-spin @a7d458546 base 4cbf9ba5b, patch darling/mldr-recv-adaptive-spin.patch sha256 5129cf19, west verify=62/status 0-missing, integration d8c602b70, canonical mldr md5 3faad772 deployed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: e2e A/B attempt 2026-06-28 (Opus): on an IDLE host (load <1, the contention that spoiled the storm A/B was gone) I ran (1) a clean-host multistorm checkin-rate interleave and (2) a real make -j8 compile wall-clock A/B (ON=default spin vs OFF=RECVSPIN=0), fresh boot per arm. BOTH harnesses proved UNRELIABLE for a perf verdict: (a) the checkin-rate metric is still warmup/stat-socket-flaky (first OFF arm 3692/s, values swing, darling-stat intermittently returns empty); (b) the make -j8 e2e WEDGED on the OFF arm at 60/120 .o -- server frozen (rpcs_serviced/checkins/forks identical across a 3s sample, 26 mldr stuck). That hang is the KNOWN unfixed dar-6x4 fork/checkin stall class (lost-RPC-reply/S2C under fork storms), NOT caused by perf #3 and NOT proof that ON avoids it (n=1). NET: the e2e build harness exercises the underlying stall bug rather than cleanly measuring this fix; aggregate throughput/wall-clock remains UNMEASURED. The trustworthy verified result stands = per-checkin RPC LATENCY 237->183us (~23%, same-run instrumented A/B) + correctness PASS + RED->GREEN. A clean e2e needs the dar-6x4 stall fixed first (or a workload that doesn't trip it). Guest recovered, nothing pushed.

title: perf #18: shared-memory SPSC ring transport for the hot-path RPC (replace UDS sendmsg/recvmsg round-trip with shmem + futex)
description:
USER-PROPOSED, FIRST PRIORITY (2026-06-29). Source: gist https://gist.github.com/IlyaGulya/ac0a047d81d796c5cfa6acd5701d63af . Replace the per-RPC UDS sendmsg/recvmsg round-trip on the HOT PATH with a shared-memory SPSC ring + futex wakeup, keeping UDS for everything that needs it.

WHY THIS IS THE RIGHT FIRST LEVER (directly justified by THIS session's measurement, memory dar-perf-startup-rpc-90pct-empty-roundtrip): the per-call SERVICE-TIME measurement proved ~90% of each startup RPC is EMPTY ROUND-TRIP latency — the server services each call in just 4-16us (rpc mean 5us/p50 4us over 1947 boot RPCs) but the guest WAITS ~100-120us. That gap is socket sendmsg + recvmsg + cross-process scheduler WAKEUP, NOT server work. The SAME signature is perf#6's 57% __skb_wait build reply-sleep and perf#12's fork/checkin wakeup chain. So a faster TRANSPORT attacks the dominant cost across startup AND builds AND fork — orthogonal to (and lower-risk than) the high-risk server-side Mach-state construction that blocks .25.1/.23. Cited bench in the gist: ~270ns round-trip for 32B over an SPSC shmem ring vs ~4.6-5.9us over UDS (author cautions not to apply directly to Darling — must measure in-situ).

HYBRID ARCHITECTURE (the gist's core recommendation — do NOT rip out UDS):
 KEEP UDS for: initial connection + process authentication (SCM_CREDENTIALS), file-descriptor passing via ancillary data, control/lifecycle messages, and as a FALLBACK transport for any RPC shape the ring can't carry.
 MOVE TO SHMEM for: the frequent small RPCs on the hot path — Mach IPC request/response cycles, port lookup, psynch/synchronization ops, notification batching.

CORE DATA STRUCTURES (minimal ABI): per-client SPSC ring(s) in a shared anon mapping — atomic head/tail indices (cache-line separated), fixed-size message slots carrying {opcode/callnum, sequence id, thread id, inline payload 64-128B}. Larger payloads via an arena/offset region. Futex on a control word for sleep/wake when the ring is empty/full (mirrors the existing adaptive spin-then-block: perf#3 recv-spin + perf#7 generalize already spin before blocking — the ring's producer bumps + FUTEX_WAKEs, consumer spins-then-FUTEX_WAITs).

SECURITY / CORRECTNESS (the gist's critical list — these are the hard part, server is the trust boundary, guest is UNTRUSTED):
 - NEVER store raw pointers in shmem — use offset-based addressing; the server bounds-checks every offset/length against the mapping size before deref (malicious guest can write any bytes at any time).
 - Validate every slot server-side: callnum in range, lengths within slot/arena, seq monotonic-enough; treat the ring as adversarial input, not a trusted struct.
 - Process CRASH with a half-written slot: a torn/partial message must be detectable (commit via a single atomic tail publish AFTER the slot body is written; reader never reads past published tail) and a dead client's ring must be torn down without wedging the server (reuse pidfd death detection).
 - Backpressure + deadlock: ring full -> producer must block/fallback without holding a lock the consumer needs; avoid the inline-on-main-loop (perf#2b) deadlock class (dar-6x4 rwlock-across-suspend) — do NOT process a ring message inline while holding a lock a sibling needs.
 - Priority inversion / NUMA / thread migration: futex wake ordering + the server's single-threaded-by-default loop (DSERVER_SINGLE_THREADED) interplay; measure, don't assume.

PHASED PLAN (from the gist, measure-gated — STOP at any phase that doesn't beat UDS):
 P1. Profile current Mach-messaging bottlenecks (LARGELY DONE this session: per-call service-time vs guest-wait via darlingserver-stat per_call + the .27/.29 guest-side rdtsc accountant). Confirm the round-trip-not-work split on the BUILD hot path (not just startup) before building transport.
 P2. Introduce the shmem ring transport ALONGSIDE UDS (negotiated at checkin over UDS; feature-gated, default OFF, byte-identical when off).
 P3. Migrate the SIMPLEST ops first — null call / self-traps / port lookup (the cacheable/tiny ones) — and A/B the round-trip latency vs UDS with the existing gates.
 P4. Add general Mach-message support (inline payload + arena for big messages).
 P5. Synchronization primitives (psynch) last.

RED-TDD: each migrated op needs a deterministic latency gate (reuse the per-call accountant + darling-stat) proven faster than UDS before it ships; correctness gates = the existing fork-storm / build-correctness suites (must not regress dar-l8k / dar-6x4 / dar-6x4.1). GOTCHA: mldr statically links some rpc wrappers -> rebuild+deploy dylib+dyld+libSystem+server together. Constraint: nothing pushed without approval.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== P2 ABI DESIGN DONE (2026-06-29, Opus) — full doc ~/work/dar-6x4-repro/PERF18-SHMEM-RING-ABI.md ===

Grounded in the real code (read this session): guest chokepoint = dserver_rpc_hooks_send_message/receive_message in dserver-rpc-defs.h (+ mldr copy); server intake = server.cpp epoll loop (_listenerSocket/_outbox.sendMany/epoll_wait@722) + call.cpp callFromMessage (callnum switch@62, checkin@79/@113); precedent for a 2nd epoll fd + side channel = _statListenerSocket (perf#0); adaptive-spin precedent = the guest recv-spin@114 (DARLING_GUEST_RECVSPIN). S2C reverse calls ride the recv path@165.

ABI SUMMARY:
 - PER-THREAD SPSC ring PAIR (c2s request + s2c reply) in one shared mapping the guest creates (memfd) and hands the server via SCM_RIGHTS at attach. Synchronous-per-thread => no cross-thread lock guest-side.
 - Layout: control block {magic 'DRNG', abi_version, slot_size, slot_count(pow2), arena, offsets, total_size, guest_tid, two cache-line-isolated futex words} + two rings {head/tail on separate cache lines} + slots {length, arena_off/len, seq, callnum, flags, inline payload (slot 256B / inline ~192B)} + arena for big payloads. OFFSETS ONLY, never pointers.
 - Publish protocol: write slot body -> SINGLE release-store of tail (so a crash mid-write leaves a slot BELOW tail = never observed = torn-write-safe); consumer never reads past published tail. Reply via s2c ring; guest spins-then-FUTEX_WAITs exactly like the existing recv-spin (reclaims the ~100us empty round-trip). Adaptive wake: FUTEX_WAKE only if peer not spinning (reuse perf#3/#7), so the fast case is syscall-free both sides.
 - Server-wake = per-ring EVENTFD joined to the epoll set (futex can't be epoll'd); single-threaded loop drains serialized (no MT race).

HYBRID — STAYS ON UDS: checkin (SCM creds + lifetime pipe), ANY fd-bearing call (SCM_RIGHTS; generator already knows fd_count_in_call), S2C, push_reply/s2c/interrupt + UNMANAGED, and FALLBACK when ring full/un-negotiated/oversized. Ring carries only small reply-bearing fd-free C2S calls. First migrations (P3) = the ~90%-empty-round-trip self-traps: mach_reply_port/host_self/task_self/thread_self/uidgid/set_thread_handles/vchroot_path.

NEGOTIATION (over UDS): checkin gains an additive cap bit DSERVER_CHECKIN_CAP_SHMRING (old servers ignore unknown bits = compat); server replies accept+abi_version; per opting-in thread a new fd-bearing ring_attach RPC passes the memfd; server fstat-size-checks, mmaps, VALIDATES the control block (magic/abi/pow2/all offsets in-bounds/guest_tid==SCM nsid), registers ring + eventfd. Any failure => stay on UDS, no error.

TRUST BOUNDARY (the hard part): guest is UNTRUSTED, can rewrite shmem anytime. Server copies-in + validates every field before use, never deref's a guest pointer (offsets bounds-checked vs mapping/arena size), reuses call.cpp callnum validation verbatim; a bad ring => drop ring + fall thread back to UDS + log once, never crash/OOB. Dispatch reuses the SAME Call path (just a Ring vs UDS MessageSource) so all exec-path fixes (dar-l8k UAF, dar-6x4 rwlock) still apply; do NOT process inline under a lock a sibling needs (perf#2b/dar-6x4 hazard class).

FLAGS: guest -DDARLING_RING_TRANSPORT=1 (CMake, default OFF, byte-identical off, 7-copy deploy), server -DDSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT=ON (default OFF, off=never advertises cap), runtime DARLING_RING=0 env A/B kill-switch read once at checkin (not in atomic region).

RED-TDD gates: (1) latency — migrated op's guest reply-wait on ring < UDS beyond noise (per-call accountant .27/.29 + darling-stat per_call + run-rpcphase.sh); (2) correctness — fork-storm/build + dar-l8k/dar-6x4/6x4.1 regressions unchanged; (3) ADVERSARIAL (new, required) — feed the ring garbage (bad callnum/length>slot/arena OOB/tail-jump/magic-corrupt), assert server REJECTS + UDS-fallback WITHOUT crash/OOB; committed RED->GREEN.

NEXT IMPL STEP (smallest trust-boundary exercise): control block + negotiation handshake + a no-op ring the server validates+maps+tears-down but no call uses yet, plus the adversarial gate. THEN migrate self-traps (P3). Nothing built/pushed; prod a13ec2ae untouched.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== P2 IMPL increment #1 DONE: ABI structs + trust-boundary validator + adversarial RED->GREEN gate (2026-06-29, Opus) ===

Smallest trust-boundary-exercising brick, committed. NO call migrated, NO transport wired yet -- this is the wire format + the security gate, which is the riskiest/most-reviewable piece and is pure logic (no boot/socket/rebuild coupling).

WHAT LANDED (darlingserver branch perf/shmem-ring-abi-validator @4107e7f, base 92049f0 = the dar-l8k fix branch; only 3 files, call.cpp + spawn-spike left untouched):
 - include/darlingserver/rpc-supplement.h (+150): the ring ABI as a portable C/C++ wire format, ALL gated behind DSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT (default OFF, byte-identical off -- verified: header compiles clean C11 + C++17, flag on AND off; zero footprint off). Structs: dserver_ring_shm_t (control block: magic 'DRNG'/abi/slot_size/slot_count/arena/ring offsets/total_size/guest_tid + two cache-line-isolated futex words, plain uint32 storage so it's a portable wire format -- atomicity at the access site), dserver_ring_t (head/tail on separate cache lines), dserver_ring_slot_t (length/arena/seq/callnum/flags + inline payload). Bounds consts (slot_size 64..4096, slot_count 2..1024 pow2, total <=16MiB).
 - dserver_ring_shm_validate(): the PURE trust-boundary gate. Takes a server-owned COPY of the guest control block + the fd's real size + the SCM-credentialed nsid; returns dserver_ring_ok or the first reject reason. uint64 math throughout (32-bit fields can't wrap), DEREFERENCES NOTHING -> safe on fully attacker-controlled input. Rejects: bad magic/abi, slot_size out of range or < slot header, slot_count non-pow2 or over cap, total_size != fd real size or over cap, ring/arena span escaping the mapping or overlapping another region/the header, guest_tid != SCM nsid.
 - tests/ring_shm_validate_test.cpp + tests/run-ring-shm-validate.sh: adversarial RED->GREEN. PROVEN RED against an accept-all stub (-DRING_VALIDATE_STUB -> all 15 malformed cases fail) and GREEN against the real validator. Gate script runs both arms, exits 0 only if RED fails AND GREEN passes. Verified: gate exit=0.

WHY this first: the validator is the security contract for putting guest-writable shmem in the RPC path; it's pure logic, so it gets a hermetic host-side RED->GREEN with no guest/server rebuild loop. NEXT increment (still P2): the negotiation handshake -- a new fd-bearing ring_attach RPC (NOT modifying checkin, to avoid touching the dar-l8k/dar-6x4 exec path), server handler that fstat-size-checks + mmaps + runs this validator + registers the ring's eventfd in the epoll set, then a no-op ring that attaches/tears-down but carries no call. THEN P3 migrate self-traps.

STATE: source-only on the fix branch, nothing built/deployed, prod a13ec2ae untouched, NOT pushed.

darlingserver branch perf/shmem-ring-abi-validator @5e68f16 (on top of #1 @4107e7f). Staged ONLY my hunks -- the pre-existing fork-timeline call.cpp diff + spawn-spike files left untouched (used git apply --cached of just my 3rd call.cpp hunk to avoid sweeping them in).

WHAT LANDED (5 files, +320):

Old server rejects unknown callnum => guest UDS-fallback. checkin NOT touched (dar-l8k/dar-6x4 exec path safe).  - include/darlingserver/rpc-supplement.h: dserver_ring_attach_check() (header-only, gated DSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT + __linux__) -- the bridge from the pure validator to real kernel state: fstat the fd for its TRUE size (never trusts guest mapping_size), mmap read-only, COPY the control block out (never validate in place -- guest can race a write), run dserver_ring_shm_validate(), munmap.  - tests/ring_attach_check_test.cpp + run-ring-shm-validate.sh: attach-check RED->GREEN with REAL memfds (memfd_create+ftruncate+mmap). RED via accept-all stub (-DRING_ATTACH_STUB, 8 cases fail: size-lie< />, header total_size!=real, undersized fd, malformed block in valid-size fd, tid-spoof, bad fd no-crash). GREEN real. === P2 IMPL increment #3 DONE: RingBuffer + epoll Monitor lifecycle on ring_attach (2026-06-29, Opus) ===

darlingserver branch perf/shmem-ring-abi-validator @6c5ca4f (on #2 @5e68f16). 6 files, +320/-14. Staged ONLY my hunks again (call.cpp still carries the pre-existing fork-timeline diff -- isolated my RingAttach hunks via git apply --cached --unidiff-zero of a -U0-filtered patch; thread.cpp/thread.hpp/CMakeLists/ring.* are cleanly mine).

WHAT LANDED:
 - ring.hpp/ring.cpp (NEW, gated DSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT): class RingBuffer = server-side owner of one attached ring. RingBuffer::attach() runs dserver_ring_attach_check(), and ONLY on accept dups the memfd + mmaps RW + creates the wake eventfd (::eventfd, name-qualified to dodge the method/ libc collision); dtor munmaps + closes. Typed accessors controlBlock/c2sRing/s2cRing/arena from the validated offsets.
 - call.cpp Call::RingAttach::processCall(): now builds a RingBuffer, registers its wake eventfd as a Readable|HangUp Monitor (the addMonitor pattern used for proc death, server.cpp:820), hands both to Thread::attachRing(). Monitor callback drains the eventfd + tears down on HangUp; dispatches NO call yet (no C2S migrated -- that's P3), so drain-the-wake is the correct no-op. Proves attach->map->watch->teardown end-to-end.
 - thread.hpp/thread.cpp: Thread owns _ring + _ringMonitor (gated). notifyDead() moves them out from under _rwlock and releases AFTER unlocking (removeMonitor touches Server state -- the dar-6x4 lock-across-suspend discipline). attachRing() replaces any prior ring under the same discipline.
 - CMakeLists: ring.cpp in sources + option DSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT (default OFF).

VERIFIED: the FULL darlingserver target BUILDS + LINKS with feature OFF (ring.cpp = empty TU; call/thread relink clean) in ~/work/darling-build -> binary 1b4893d3, NOT deployed. call.cpp/thread.cpp/ring.cpp all -fsyntax-only clean with feature ON against the regenerated rpc.internal.h (RingAttach present, callnum 81). comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== P3 IMPL DONE: ring datapath + first migrated op (task_self_trap), end-to-end both sides, host-proven (2026-06-29, Opus) ===

The shared-memory ring fast path now carries a real call, server AND guest, all gated default-OFF. Wake design = Variant 2 (user-chosen 'наиболее аккуратно и красиво'): server owns the wake eventfd + hands the guest a dup in the ring_attach reply; guest writes it to wake the epoll-bound server; server wakes the guest via FUTEX_WAKE on s2c_futex (guest FUTEX_WAITs, not in epoll).

SERVER (darlingserver branch perf/shmem-ring-abi-validator @5a73f80, on @6c5ca4f):
 - rpc-supplement.h (+191): pure bounds-safe SPSC datapath -- free-running uint32 head/tail (idx = ctr & (count-1)), single release-store publish, consumer/producer refuse a corrupt (tail-head) distance (>slot_count -> empty/full) so a hostile peer can't drive an OOB index. Ring message convention (reply payload = dserver_ring_reply_hdr{code} + body; flags bit0=error). dserver_ring_service_c2s() = the server drain loop: copy each slot's transport header out, clamp length to the slot, hand the body to a servicer callback, publish the reply on s2c. Offsets-only, derefs no guest pointer.
 - generate-rpc-wrappers.py: ring_attach reply gains wake_fd @fd (the generator already fd-passes reply @fd via SCM_RIGHTS, like console_open). callnum still 81.
 - ring.cpp/.hpp: RingBuffer::publishReply (claim s2c slot, write code+body, one release-store), wakeGuest (bump s2c_futex + SYS_futex FUTEX_WAKE, NON-private = cross-process), drainWake.
 - call.cpp: darRingServiceC2S() rebuilds each request as the SAME UDS-format {callhdr,body} Message (callhdr synthesized from thread pid/id/arch), arms a one-shot ring-reply sink (Thread::beginRingReply(seq)), and runs it INLINE via the perf#2b doWork() path -- REUSING the entire Call dispatch, so dar-l8k UAF / dar-6x4 rwlock / dar-gwn reply-on-drop fixes all still apply; only transport-in + reply-sink-out differ. P3 allowlist = task_self_trap only (anything else dropped -> guest UDS-falls-back). Monitor cb: drainWake then darRingServiceC2S. ring_attach now dups the eventfd TWICE (one for the Monitor, one returned to the guest).
 - thread.cpp: pushCallReply, when _ringReplyPending, publishes the reply onto s2c + wakeGuest (UDS fallback if s2c full); beginRingReply sets the one-shot {pending,seq} under _rwlock.

GUEST (xnu branch perf/shmem-ring-guest @e513bdb, base 1169ebe = recv-spin HEAD):
 - dserver-ring.c/.h (NEW, gated DARLING_RING_TRANSPORT, libc-free raw LINUX_SYSCALL): __dserver_ring_try_attach builds the memfd (memfd_create+ftruncate+mmap; kernel zero-fill = empty rings), fills the control block, negotiates via dserver_rpc_ring_attach, maps the returned wake eventfd. __dserver_ring_task_self_trap publishes a c2s request, writes the wake eventfd, bounded-spins (DARLING_GUEST_RECVSPIN) then cross-process FUTEX_WAITs on s2c_futex, validates seq+callnum+length before trusting the reply, UDS-falls-back on any miss. P3 SCOPE: ONE ring per process owned by the first attaching thread (others UDS-fall-back) to sidestep fragile early-boot guest TLS (memory: __thread aborts here) -- per-thread rings are P4. postfork_reset drops the parent ring in the child (called from fork.c by the socket-refresh).
 - mach_traps.c: __task_self_trap_rpc tries the ring first, UDS fallback (folds away flag-off).
 - fork.c: __dserver_ring_postfork_reset alongside __dserver_per_thread_socket_refresh.
 - CMakeLists: option DARLING_RING_TRANSPORT into BOTH emulation (dylib) + emulation_dyld (dyld) -- trap path lives in both (perf#7-generalize / gate-hotpath-kprintf dual-target lesson).

RED->GREEN GATE (the adversarial requirement from the bead): tests/ring_datapath_test.cpp on a REAL memfd ring, under ASan. GREEN = real dserver_ring_service_c2s + real SPSC: happy round-trip (right port), FIFO batch (seq echoed), and CONTAINMENT of length>slot / corrupt-tail-jump / s2c-full-backpressure(retry-not-loss) / refused-callnum -- no OOB, no crash, no bogus reply. RED = accept-all stub that trusts the claimed length + indexes blindly -> MUST fail (it does: 6 contract violations). run-ring-shm-validate.sh now runs validator(15)+attach(8)+datapath(GREEN+RED), exit 0.

VERIFIED: full darlingserver BUILDS+LINKS flag-OFF (default, binary 6c51e981) AND flag-ON (cmake -DDSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT=ON, links clean) in ~/work/darling-build, restored to OFF after. Guest dserver-ring.c/mach_traps.c/fork.c compile clean flag ON+OFF in the dyld target. All ring gates green.

NOT YET (honest, deploy-gated -- standing 'ничего не деплоить/пушить'): the LIVE guest<->server round-trip and the latency A/B (per-call accountant .27/.29 + darling-stat per_call + run-rpcphase.sh) need a coordinated dylib+dyld+server deploy (7-copy, perf#10 lesson) + a guest boot, which I did not do. task_self_trap is ALSO cached per-process (perf#7) so it's a correctness-first op, not the high-frequency latency lever; the measurable A/B win wants an uncached hot op (mach_reply_port) = P3-next. Prod a13ec2ae UNTOUCHED, source-only on the two fix branches, nothing deployed/pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LIVE A/B DONE (2026-06-29, Opus) — deploy WAS authorized (user: "деплой я не запрещал, только пуш"). Deployed the ring-ON set to the homebrew-test guest and ran a real round-trip + latency A/B for mach_reply_port.

FOUND + FIXED a live-boot bug (the original P3 commits would have wedged ANY real boot): darRingServiceC2S synthesized the call header with process->id()/thread->id() (server-internal Linux ids), but callFromMessage resolves via the registry keyed on NSID (guest-namespace ids). Lookup missed → "non-existent thread" → ESRCH → ring drops the reply → guest stuck in cross-process FUTEX_WAIT forever → launchd wedged at boot (futex_wait_queue), shellspawn never came up. FIX: header pid/tid = nsid(); + reqMsg.setPID(process->id()) so callFromMessage's pid-consistency check holds. Committed darlingserver @1418bee (on @bb9197f), gated, flag-OFF still 6c51e981.

VALIDATION (server debug counter): clean boot, shellspawn up, 20k-iter mach_reply_port microbench null=0, 2358 #36 calls confirmed SERVICED OVER THE RING (vs ~2390 boot/other-proc #36 over UDS). Note "Trying to lock mutex without an active thread" warnings = pre-existing boot/timer noise (928/1030 in first 2s, then ~4/s background), NOT ring-caused (60k ring calls produced none).

A/B RESULT (5×20k each, same guest, same bench binary): RING median ~26.0us/call (runs 27.1/22.0/26.2/18.8/26.0) vs UDS-prod median ~29.7us/call (28.1/27.4/29.7/31.3/29.9). => ring ~12% faster, real + consistent (ring best 18.8 < UDS best 27.4). BUT far from the gist's ~270ns: my P3 wake design still pays a per-call eventfd-write + epoll-wake + futex-wake + futex-wait = 4 syscalls + 2 scheduler hops. The ring removed the socket marshalling (the ~12%) but NOT the cross-process WAKEUP, which perf#17 measured as ~90% of cost. LESSON: transport was never the bottleneck — the WAKEUP is. Beating it needs server-side adaptive ring-POLLING before it sleeps (so the hot case never makes a syscall/sleeps), mirroring the gist's busy-polled SPSC assumption = P4.

Bench harness: ~/.claude-work/jobs/ae391f52/tmp/rpbench.c (times mach_reply_port only; ports freed after the timing window). Prod a13ec2ae/e5cd6b12 fully RESTORED to all 7 deploy locations; build dir flags OFF. NOTHING pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=== ARCHITECTURE REFRAME (2026-06-29, Opus) — plan.md supersedes the original P1–P5 framing of this bead ===

Source of truth now: ~/work/darling-dev/plan.md "perf#18 / Darling IPC: Hybrid Ring Architecture Brief". The original .30 body framed perf#18 as "ring = a faster UDS replacement for the hot path" with a linear P1..P5 op-migration plan. plan.md §0 EXPLICITLY RETIRES that: "We are no longer treating the shared-memory ring as 'a faster UDS replacement for every RPC'."

TARGET PICTURE = THREE-LANE HYBRID IPC (put each op on the cheapest lane that preserves semantics):
 - Lane 0 UDS = control/fallback (bootstrap, creds, fd-passing, ABI-mismatch fallback, unknown/complex ops, destroy-capable/caller-S2C ops until duplex-proven). Not failure — the conservative compatibility lane.
 - Lane 1 simple fast request->reply ring = CLOSED transactions only, no caller-S2C while parked. Tier-1 (ring + generic fiber) and Tier-2 (no-fiber direct, only mach_reply_port). Frozen set = {task_self_trap, mach_reply_port, mach_port_allocate, mach_port_insert_right}.
 - Lane 2 FUTURE duplex/reentrant ring = caller-S2C-capable ops (deallocate, destroying mod_refs). Separate protocol/ABI/gates; opt-in; NOT the default.

The original P3/P4/P5 WORK STILL HAPPENED and is real (P4 v3 wake model ~6x; P6.1 mach_reply_port ~0.95us; P5 allocate/insert_right migrated; deallocate+mod_refs audited OUT as destroy-capable). What changed is the FRAMING: "migrate more ops onto the ring" is NO LONGER the next step. The simple ring is FROZEN and the work splits into explicit phases.

NEW BEAD STRUCTURE (under this epic-task / under dar-1il.3):
 - PHASE A = dar-dar6x4-perf-5dq.30.1 (P1, immediate next task): freeze the three-lane architecture, terminology cleanup (SimpleRingC2SEligible != NoFiberFastEligible != DuplexRingEligible != UdsOnly), static guardrails near DSERVER_RING_C2S_OPCODES. No migration. plan.md §17 calls this the immediate recommended task.
 - PHASE B = simple-ring DEFAULT-ON product decision (separate from duplex). NOT a new bead — tracked by the existing rollout checklist (tests/PERF18-RING-DEFAULT-ON-CHECKLIST.md) + the dar-my8 lost-wake gate, both already DONE; flipping default is a product call. plan.md §Phase-B lists the prerequisites (all already green: P4/P6 perf, P7 race gates, opcode-hash/ABI fallback, destroy-capable ops removed, NOTICE, kill switches, transport-OFF compiles out, FAST_OPS=0, balanced p50/p99 documented).
 - PHASE C = dar-1il.3 (repurposed): DESIGN-ONLY spec for the duplex lane (no broad impl).
 - PHASE D = dar-1il.3.1: minimal duplex prototype, ONE scenario (deallocate of a mapped-region-backed port).
 - PHASE E = dar-1il.3.2: duplex acceptance + migrate destroy-capable/caller-S2C ops off UDS.
Ordering: C (parent) -> D (parent) -> E, with E blocks-dep on D.

EXPLICIT NON-GOALS (plan.md §16) carried into all duplex beads: no re-adding deallocate/mod_refs to the SIMPLE ring; no generic fast-op table yet; no full-duplex simple ring; no routing unknown ops through a ring; no silent drop of env-disabled opcodes; no claiming stress covers near-zero paths; no kern_return_t-only correctness. NOTHING PUSHED policy stands.

PACKET CONTENTS: (1) blast radius (only 4 ops move off UDS, byte-identical, opt-in per process); (2) evidence

title: perf #18 Phase A: freeze the THREE-LANE hybrid IPC architecture (terminology + static guardrails, no migration)
description:
perf#18 PHASE A (plan.md §17 — the IMMEDIATE next task, architecture-oriented NOT an implementation sprint). Source of truth: ~/work/darling-dev/plan.md "Hybrid Ring Architecture Brief". This bead REFRAMES perf#18 from the original ".30" framing ("ring = faster UDS for every hot RPC", P1–P5) to the TARGET picture: a THREE-LANE hybrid IPC architecture. No behavior change; this is freeze + document + guardrails.

=== TARGET ARCHITECTURE (the north star) ===
Multi-lane IPC. Put each op on the CHEAPEST lane that preserves semantics:
 - Lane 0 = UDS control/fallback lane (bootstrap, creds, fd-passing, ABI-mismatch fallback, unknown/complex ops, destroy-capable/caller-S2C ops UNTIL duplex-proven). UDS is NOT failure — it is the conservative compatibility lane.
 - Lane 1 = simple fast request->reply ring (the existing P4/P6/P5 ring). CLOSED transactions only: one request, one final reply, NO synchronous S2C upcall to the caller while parked. Two sub-tiers: Tier-1 = ring + generic fiber doWork() (~2–6us p50); Tier-2 = ring + no-fiber/direct (sub-µs, ONLY mach_reply_port proven).
 - Lane 2 = FUTURE duplex/reentrant ring for caller-S2C-capable ops (see dar-1il.3 design bead). Opt-in, separate protocol/ABI/gates. NOT the default for fast ops.

The simple ring is valuable BECAUSE it is small. Do NOT make it universal; do NOT make it full-duplex. Fast lane must not pay duplex complexity (separate queues/offsets/cache-lines/state-words/stats/ABI bits even if same memfd).

=== DELIVERABLES (Phase A — no broad migration) ===
1. Document the three-lane target architecture in perf#18 memory + on .30 (DONE-ish via reframe comment; this bead owns the full writeup).
2. Terminology cleanup in code+docs — introduce explicit, separate concepts and STOP overloading "fast op"/"allowlist"/"ring op":
     SimpleRingC2SEligible  — may use the closed request->reply simple ring
     NoFiberFastEligible    — may bypass generic fiber/Call machinery
     DuplexRingEligible     — may use the future reentrant lane
     UdsOnly                — must use UDS
     DestroyCapable         — may drop last ref / trigger cleanup / cause S2C
     CallerS2CCapable       — may synchronously upcall the caller before final reply
   Policy comments to assert (plan.md §6):
     SimpleRingC2SEligible != NoFiberFastEligible != DuplexRingEligible
     DestroyCapable  => NOT SimpleRingC2SEligible
     CallerS2CCapable => NOT SimpleRingC2SEligible
3. Static guardrails / asserts near DSERVER_RING_C2S_OPCODES preventing a destroy-capable/caller-S2C op from entering the simple-ring set. Build on the membership canon already at rpc-supplement.h @f997803 (the 5-rule checklist + "wrong lane not bad op" frame) — this bead turns the prose canon into named concepts + (where feasible) compile-time guardrails.
4. Make explicit in code that mach_port_deallocate + mach_port_mod_refs are UDS-only until a duplex proof exists.
5. Confirm + FREEZE the current simple-ring set: {task_self_trap, mach_reply_port, mach_port_allocate, mach_port_insert_right}. Confirm deallocate/mod_refs UDS-only.
6. Keep prod default OFF (DSERVER_RING_TRANSPORT / DARLING_RING_TRANSPORT). Default-ON is a SEPARATE product decision (Phase B), not part of this bead.

=== ACCEPTANCE (plan.md §Phase-A) ===
 - docs/memory updated with the three-lane model; bead updated; NO new simple-ring migration; ALL existing ring gates still GREEN (validator/attach/datapath/drift/fastpath/wake-race/wake-predicates via run-ring-shm-validate.sh).
 - terminology/guardrails landed RED->GREEN where a guardrail is testable (e.g. a static_assert / compile-fail arm that a destroy-capable op can't be added to DSERVER_RING_C2S_OPCODES, mirroring the existing -DC2S_READD_MODREFS drift arm).

=== POINTERS ===
HEADs darlingserver @f997803 / xnu @412e15c. Membership canon: rpc-supplement.h next to DSERVER_RING_C2S_OPCODES. Memory: dar-perf18-ring-c2s-membership-canon.md, dar-perf18-shmem-ring-transport.md. plan.md is the authoritative brief. NOTHING PUSHED policy stands; local build/deploy allowed.
close_reason: done
