comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
2026-06-21 — Re-validated brew on nhs against the FULLY-FIXED state (current darling-build: EUNION branch, a strict superset of fix/homebrew-psynch-ruby-hang; all dar-gwn fixes present+active, EUNION OFF). Harness: setuid-root launcher darling-prefix/bin/darling, DPREFIX=darling-prefix-homebrew-test, uid 1000.

MAJOR PROGRESS, NO HANG: 'brew install wget' now detects CLT 13.2.1 / Xcode 13.2.1 / macOS 11.7.4 / SDK, resolves ALL deps (pkgconf, libunistring, gettext, libidn2, openssl@3, wget 1.25.0), and BUILDS+POURS pkgconf 2.5.1 from source (🍺, configure+make+make install, 62s). The dar-gwn.1 (SA_RESTART) + dar-gwn.5 (mldr raw-fork lock reset) fixes HOLD — none of the old fork-storm/psynch HANGS occurred.

NEW DETERMINISTIC BLOCKER (NOT a hang, distinct bug): the 2nd dependency libunistring DETERMINISTICALLY SEGVs (2/2 runs, identical signature). Crash is in the 'ruby build.rb' child (the per-formula build process brew always launches via sandbox-exec): Ruby [BUG] Segmentation fault at formula.rb:3560 'in wait' — i.e. the PARENT ruby's Process.wait(pid) right after fork{ exec_cmd } of the staged build step. Machine ctx: rax=0xfffffffffffffffc (-4 = EINTR), rdi==r15==child pid, rip in libsystem region. Fault addr 0x000003e8_0000XXXX where high dword 0x3e8 = 1000 = our uid => a corrupted status/rusage-ish pointer built from (uid<<32 | small).

ISOLATION so far (all in scratchpad reproducers):
- Bare C fork+exec(/bin/sh)+wait4 loop, 120 iters: CLEAN.
- C fork+exec(real tar xf 5MB)+wait4 with SIGCHLD handler (no SA_RESTART) → EINTR retry, 10 iters: CLEAN.
- Single-threaded Ruby fork{exec tar}+Process.wait x8 (same ruby binary): CLEAN.
- Full 'brew install libunistring' with HOMEBREW_NO_SANDBOX=1 + HOMEBREW_DOWNLOAD_CONCURRENCY=1: STILL SEGV (sandbox-exec wrapper still applied; build.rb is multithreaded via concurrent-ruby thread pool).
=> The trigger requires build.rb's FULL multithreaded context (concurrent-ruby worker threads) + raw-fork + wait + signal. Same family as dar-gwn.1/.5 (raw-fork inherited-lock / signal delivery), now surfacing as a parent-side SEGV in wait4's EINTR path rather than a child hang. NEXT: gdb on the crashing build.rb child for the exact faulting instruction in libsystem_kernel; suspect wait4/__commpage or the EINTR signal-return path corrupting the status pointer under multithread+rawfork. Nothing pushed; reproducers in session scratchpad; run1/run2 full crash dumps saved (brew-wget.run1.log/run2.log).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
2026-06-21 (cont.) — Narrowed the libunistring build.rb SEGV. C-level frames from the crash dump:
  sig_restorer -> sigexc_handler+0x15c -> handler_linux_to_bsd+0x39a -> (ruby) sigabrt -> rb_bug_for_fatal_signal
i.e. a REAL SIGSEGV was raised in the build.rb process while a thread sat in wait4 (captured ucontext rax=-EINTR), and Darling's sigexc delivery path (handler_linux_to_bsd) then re-faulted, recursing (SEGV-in-SEGV / ABRT-in-ABRT) so the report truncated. Fault addr 0x000003e8_0000XXXX = (si_uid=1000 << 32 | child_pid) — a siginfo/rusage word being dereferenced as a pointer.

Reproducer ladder (all in session scratchpad, all compiled+run under Darling/CLT clang, ALL PASS — so none is the trigger yet):
  - C fork+exec(/bin/sh)+wait4, 120 iters: clean.
  - C fork+exec(tar 5MB)+wait4 + SIGCHLD sa_handler (no SA_RESTART, EINTR retry), 10: clean.
  - C fork+exec(sh pipeline)+wait4 + SA_SIGINFO SIGCHLD + 3-6 busy pthreads, 80: clean.
  - single-threaded portable-ruby fork{exec tar}+Process.wait x8: clean.
So a plain C/ruby fork+wait — even multithreaded, even SA_SIGINFO, even EINTR — does NOT reproduce. Trigger needs build.rb's exact state.

STATIC BUG FOUND (real, regardless): sys_wait4_nocancel (libsystem_kernel .../bsd/impl/process/wait4.c:36) passes the DARWIN rusage pointer straight to the Linux __NR_wait4 — there is NO rusage layout conversion (conversion/process/wait4.c only converts options). Kernel writes Linux struct rusage into a Darwin rusage buffer (different field offsets) => garbage rusage for any waitpid-with-rusage caller. Likely the same fault class: Ruby reads the mis-laid-out rusage/status and dereferences a bogus pointer. Candidate fix: convert rusage Linux->Darwin in sys_wait4 (allocate a linux rusage on the stack, translate fields), mirroring getrusage if present.

REMAINING to nail exact deref: live debugger on the crashing build.rb child. Attaching needs kernel.yama.ptrace_scope=0 (currently 1); lowering it system-wide was DENIED by policy (security mitigation on shared host) — needs explicit user authorization, OR launch-under-gdb of the brew->fork->sandbox-exec->ruby chain (awkward). Nothing pushed. EUNION-side fixes all intact.

title: Fix flaky brew install hang: darlingserver interrupt_enter/PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race
description: CONFIRMED ROOT CAUSE (forkhunt6 iter4, frozen bundle nhs:~/work/darling-debug/forkhunt6-20260608T160728Z/iter4). NOT a deadlock and NOT psynch logic. At the hang darlingserver is IDLE (only 2 OS threads: main in epoll_wait + the single WorkQueue<Thread>::worker parked in pthread_cond_wait = empty work queue; two gdb snapshots 3s apart identical). The stuck ruby thread is blocked in recvmsg awaiting the reply to psynch_mutexdrop(#71), sent immediately after a SIGCHLD (reaped curl child) drove interrupt_enter(#14)/interrupt_exit(#15). => LOST/MISROUTED RPC REPLY during signal handling: the interrupt_enter PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race explicitly acknowledged in scripts/generate-rpc-wrappers.py:84-101. Homebrew's DownloadQueue forks many curl children -> SIGCHLD storm -> frequent interrupt windows -> a reply (psynch_mutexdrop) is consumed by the wrong recvmsg / lost on push-back -> client recvmsg blocks forever -> download future never completes -> install hangs. Explains: flaky (~1/4); 'brew install' (deps fork storm) hangs but 'brew fetch' doesn't; darlingserver idle; the psynch cvwait validation fix is correct and unrelated. Fix lives in the interrupt_enter/exit + push-reply protocol: darlingserver/src/thread.cpp (pushCallReply ~1361; _interrupts/savedReply/_deferredReply/_pendingSavedReply/_handlingInterruptedCall ~1520-1580) + client generated dserver_rpc_* wrappers (push_reply path, callnum 0xbadca11). RPC wire: first LE32 of each msg = callnum (14=interrupt_enter,15=interrupt_exit,70=cvwait,71=mutexdrop,31=pthread_canceled->-38 ENOSYS benign). Tooling: reproduce with /tmp/forkhunt6.sh on nhs (NEVER pkill -9 darlingserver -> corrupts mount namespace; use darling shutdown).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE (forkhunt6 iter4): NOT psynch deadlock. darlingserver IDLE at hang (epoll+empty workqueue). Lost/misrouted RPC reply during signal handling: interrupt_enter PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES deferral race (generate-rpc-wrappers.py:84-101). Homebrew curl forks -> SIGCHLD storm -> signal interrupts thread mid-psynch-RPC -> psynch_mutexdrop(#71) reply lost on push-back -> recvmsg blocks forever. Fix target: interrupt_enter/exit + push-reply protocol (mldr client + darlingserver), NOT psynch.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: RE-DIAGNOSIS (from forkhunt6 iter4 client strace, re-read 2026-06-09). PRIOR reply-deferral-race diagnosis is WRONG. Decoded main-thread RPC tail (fd1023): a psynch_mutexdrop(#71) was interrupted by SIGCHLD while blocked on a lock 8.65s; the interrupt_enter(14)/interrupt_exit(15) sequence + the DEFERRED #71 reply all delivered correctly (recv #71 code=0x204 right after interrupt_exit). The HANG is on a SUBSEQUENT, SECOND psynch_mutexdrop(#71) sent ~4ms later with NO signal anywhere near it -> recvmsg never returns. So NOT an interrupt reply-routing loss. Server alive (epoll_wait) + WorkQueue worker in pthread_cond_wait => the managed thread is a microthread-fiber (invisible to gdb) that SUSPENDED on a duct-tape lock/turnstile and never resumed = server-side psynch deadlock / lost wakeup. Hypothesis: resume-during-interrupt of a lock-blocked psynch call (thread.cpp:1523 FIXME 'does not work properly if the thread was suspended waiting for a lock') corrupts psynch internal state (ksyn_wqlock/turnstile waiter), wedging the NEXT psynch_mutexdrop server-side. Fix locus = psynch interrupt/resume + duct-tape lock handling (kern_synch.c + _handleInterruptEnterForCurrentThread), NOT the RPC reply FIFO. Next: reproduce with darlingserver debug logging to pinpoint where the 2nd mutexdrop microthread is stuck.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: ROOT CAUSE CONFIRMED (2026-06-09, instrumented capture forkhuntph-20260609T071914Z/iter1). The Fetching-downloads hang is a LOST MUTEX-HANDOFF WAKEUP in duct-tape psynch: ksyn_wqfind returns DIFFERENT kwq objects for the SAME userspace mutex over time (e.g. mutex 0x7306615095d0 -> kwq 003620 then 026a10 then 026b30 within ~6s). Waiters (psynch_mutexwait) enqueue on one kwq instance; a later psynch_mutexdrop resolves a different kwq whose kw_inqueue does not count the stranded waiter -> FF handoff signals wrong/none -> woken thread re-waits without acquiring -> main + a worker both strand in mutexwait -> RPC reply never sent -> guest blocks in recvmsg -> darlingserver idle (worker parked) -> install hangs. RULED OUT: reply-deferral/Option B (replies all delivered), thread_unblock/TH_WAIT scheduler wakeup (threads ARE rescheduled), network/DNS (skb wait is the unix RPC socket). Fix locus: ksyn_wqfind/ksyn_wqrelease kwq identity+lifecycle in kern_synch.c (same area as is_seqhigher fix, different defect). Full writeup: pr-drafts/darling-psynch-kwq-lost-wakeup-findings.md. Instrumented bin nhs:/tmp/darlingserver.ph2 (md5 58416e), hunter nhs:/tmp/forkhunt-ph.sh (psynch-frozen-90s real-deadlock detector).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 follow-up: the residual hang reproduces on the CURRENT fixed darlingserver via 'brew fetch wget' (faster/earlier than full install). Capture (~/work/darling-debug/20260610T102808Z-brew-fetch-wget) confirms the EXACT original signature: darlingserver idle (main in epoll_wait + single WorkQueue worker parked in futex), guest ruby main thread blocked in recvmsg on the unix .darlingserver.sock (wchan __skb_wait_for_more_packets) awaiting an RPC reply that never arrives, sibling thread busy-spinning. This CORRECTS the dar-gwn.1.4 'network socket wait' characterization: the stuck fd is the darlingserver RPC socket, not an HTTP socket, and the server is idle — i.e. still a lost/never-sent RPC reply, not a download stall. So dar-gwn.1's core defect is reduced but not fully closed by the suspend/resume permit; continuation tracked as dar-gwn.2.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
STILL REPRODUCES on CLT 13.2 (2026-06-11). After upgrading the homebrew-test prefix to CLT 13.2 (clang-1300/ld64-711) and confirming the toolchain itself is fully functional (hello-world + dylib compile/link/run green, -oso_prefix accepted, version-floor passed), characterized flakiness with 6 clean from-source lz4 rebuilds in ONE darling session: 5 PASS / 1 HANG (~17%). Same signature as this bug: the hung run's forked brew/Ruby build child wedged at an EARLY stage with NO tar/clang subprocess spawned; hang point FLOATS across runs (one earlier instance stalled right after 'Verifying checksum ...tar.gz'; this run stalled at '✔︎ API Source lz4.rb'). Confirms the residual race is NOT toolchain/CLT-version dependent and NOT -oso_prefix related.

Deployment verified current (rules out stale libs): darlingserver 5bd7a59b, libsystem_kernel.dylib eea332e6, libsystem_pthread.dylib c5f3541d -- all md5-IDENTICAL across build dir == darling-prefix == darling-prefix-homebrew-test; submodules at xnu 6159147 / darlingserver 32af4d5 (heads/fix/homebrew-psynch-ruby-hang); no dyld_shared_cache shadowing the loose dylibs. So the dar-gwn.2 layer-1/2/3 psynch fixes REDUCED the rate but did NOT eliminate it -- consistent with the kwq-identity / lost mutex-handoff root cause documented above (pr-drafts/darling-psynch-kwq-lost-wakeup-findings.md), which remains the open defect. ~17% over a 6-sample run; rate likely formula/fork-storm dependent. Repro asset: /tmp/run-lz4-flaky.sh (N clean rebuilds + per-build 100s watchdog, prints pass/hang/fail tally).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-11 takeover follow-up: CALLDBG+PH capture /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-prefix-homebrew-test/tmp/kwq-hang-3.serverlog (runner bundle /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T084145Z-kwqcatch), 2 PASS then HANG. This capture rules out an unfinished darlingserver RPC/psynch wait for the residual hang. Hung brew parent host PID 3109473/nsid1858 and fork child host PID 3109925/nsid2196 both stop sending RPCs around 1781167433.15. Their final RPC is pthread_canceled(#31), and each final call has paired PH ENTERING/EXITING, so its reply processing completed. Last mutex transactions also have matching wait/drop and inq drains to 0. Server remains active serving unrelated processes. Prior interpretation of repeated PH ENTER(state=0)->EXIT(state=4) as scheduler livelock was wrong: dtape_thread_exiting clears TH_RUN after every normal RPC. Next: reproduce without killing the hung subtree and capture host gdb/strace for parent+child; current evidence points outside darlingserver, after fork child startup.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-11 host-tree/GDB diagnosis supersedes the remaining psynch/RPC hypothesis for the captured residual hangs. Bundle /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T090459Z-lz4-namespace-gdb showed the brew parent main Ruby thread correctly waking/re-waiting in timed psynch_cvwait while sandbox.rb:360 stdin_thread stayed in blocking read after its make child exited. Root cause: sigexc installed every intermediate Linux signal handler with unconditional LINUX_SA_RESTART, so Ruby thread-kill could not interrupt read(2). Implemented sigexc_set_restart() so the intermediate handler mirrors Darwin BSD_SA_RESTART; minimal Ruby kill/read test passed 100/100 in /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T091155Z-ruby-kill-read-fixed and passed again after restoring mldr. A subsequent full lz4 run exposed a distinct second bug: /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-debug/20260611T091302Z-lz4-sarestart-fix, child PID 3144244 stuck before exec in futex_wait(_rtld_global+4344) -> get_cached_stack -> allocate_stack -> pthread_create -> __darling_thread_create. This is an inherited glibc lock after Darling raw fork, not a darlingserver/psynch wait. Full native glibc fork was tested and rejected because it breaks launchd/bootstrap via native callbacks.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
2026-06-21 — ROOT CAUSE of the libunistring/brew build.rb SEGV nailed via live gdb (ptrace_scope=0, user-authorized; restored after). It is THIS bug's family (interrupt/RPC during signal handling), surfacing as a spurious SIGSEGV rather than a hang.

EVIDENCE (gdb on the crashing build.rb child, 3 independent catches, deterministic):
- Faulting PC = the 'mov %rcx,%r10; syscall; ret' Linux syscall stub (libsystem_kernel +0x2cbf4, inside _mach_exception_raise_state +0x184). The [rsp] return target disassembles to sys_wait4_nocancel right after LINUX_SYSCALL(__NR_wait4) (the (*status & 0x7f) code, wait4.c:40) — so a thread was parked in wait4.
- rax = 0xfffffffffffffe00 = -512 = ERESTART_RESTARTBLOCK (kernel-internal restart marker) sitting in the interrupted wait4's return reg.
- The delivered signal: $_siginfo si_signo=11 (SIGSEGV) but **si_code=0 (SI_USER)** => NOT a hardware fault; it was SENT (kill/tkill/resend), not trapped. Its payload is a SIGCHLD's: _sigchld{si_pid=776,si_uid=1000,si_status=0}; the overlapping _sigfault.si_addr reads 0x3e8_00000308 = (uid 1000<<32 | pid). Ruby's SIGSEGV handler then fires -> [BUG] -> recurses (SEGV-in-SEGV).

MECHANISM: a SIGCHLD (child reaped during the concurrent-ruby DownloadQueue/build fork-storm) arrives while a thread is blocked in wait4 (rax=-512 ERESTART). Instead of being delivered as SIGCHLD, it is processed via the sigexc exception path -> state_to_kernel + dserver_rpc_sigprocess (darlingserver decides the delivered signal) -> the returned bsd_signum is corrupted to SIGSEGV(11) and delivered with SI_USER + the SIGCHLD siginfo payload. i.e. darlingserver's sigprocess/interrupt RPC mis-routes the signal under concurrency — the same lost/misrouted-RPC-during-interrupt defect this bead tracks (interrupt_enter/PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES; state corruption when a signal hits a thread mid-RPC/mid-wait).

NOT FIXED BY: a libsystem_kernel-side ERESTART->EINTR map in sys_wait4 (tried: built+deployed cc930dce to both layers, verified guest loads it via shasum 0e095b17; libunistring still SEGV identically). The -512 never reaches the C return path — the process is interrupted at the syscall stub's ret and the bogus SIGSEGV is delivered there. Reverted that edit + restored original dylibs (35caca39 / de47f8ec).

FIX LOCUS: darlingserver-side signal/exception delivery (dserver_rpc_sigprocess + interrupt_enter/exit reply routing in thread.cpp), NOT libsystem_kernel and NOT wait4. This is squarely dar-gwn.1. NOTE bead warns darlingserver submodule has uncommitted parallel work — coordinate before editing. Repro: brew install libunistring under DPREFIX=darling-prefix-homebrew-test (deterministic, 1st build child). gdb catch scripts + crash dumps in session scratchpad. Nothing pushed.

title: Capture darlingserver thread backtraces at a genuine hang
description: Tooling on nhs: /tmp/forkhunt4.sh loops brew install under light strace (no futex), watchdog detects wedge (~45s no strace growth after Fetching), then takes TWO gdb 'thread apply all bt' of darlingserver 3s apart (spin vs sleep) + ruby/darlingserver kernel stacks + RPC strace correlation. darlingserver has function symbols. Need the wedged-instance backtrace to find where the psynch_mutexdrop handler is stuck (likely ksyn_wqlock kw_lock spin or turnstile/owner logic interacting with fork process-duplication).
close_reason: Captured: darlingserver IDLE at hang (epoll + empty workqueue, 2 snapshots identical), NOT deadlocked. Stuck ruby thread blocked in recvmsg awaiting psynch_mutexdrop(#71) reply after SIGCHLD/interrupt_enter/exit. Root cause = interrupt_enter PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race (acknowledged in generate-rpc-wrappers.py:84-101).

title: brew dep configure() conftest hangs: lost RPC reply, server idle (post-sigexc-flood-fix)
description: SURFACED after the sigexc-debug-flood fix (dar-gwn.1.9) removed the kprintf throughput collapse. With that fix, brew install wget passes the download stall and proceeds into the dependency configure phase (libunistring), but then HANGS during a ./conftest run. FROZEN STATE (evidence ~/work/darling-debug/lostreply-20260623T095635Z): the ./conftest guest process has BOTH its threads blocked in recvmsg (__skb_wait_for_more_packets) on two darlingserver sockets, while darlingserver is fully IDLE (main thread epoll_wait, worker parked in futex, dserver.log frozen — size unchanged across reads). I.e. conftest sent an RPC and is waiting for a reply that the server never produces/re-sends = a LOST RPC REPLY with the server alive. This is the dar-gwn.1.7 FAMILY (server-alive lost-reply hang) but the pushreply-pipe-leak fix (already in the patchset, 3 refs) does NOT cover this path. Parent chain: ruby(do_wait) -> bash configure(do_wait) -> bash configure(do_wait) -> conftest(recvmsg x2). NEXT: catch the exact RPC call number the conftest is stuck on (longer strace to catch a resend, or instrument the server receive path / interrupt_enter+push_reply deferral), determine why the reply is never delivered when the server is idle. Tools: ~/work/dar-gwn17-verify/. Family ties dar-gwn.1.7 [[dar-gwn17-pushreply-pipe-leak]]. Nothing pushed.
notes: FAULT BACKTRACE CAPTURED + A-vs-B DECIDED = A (2026-06-23). Temporary in-server fault tracer (DAR_FAULT_TRACE) caught the rare one-shot fault: linux_sig=11 code=1 faultaddr=0x8 RAX=0 (deref [RAX+8]). Symbolized vs the wedged proc's /proc/maps: RIP=libsystem_c ___s2b_D2A+0x91 <- ___dtoa+0xd97 <- ___gdtoa+0x28d. ___Balloc_D2A (gdtoa bignum alloc, per-thread freelist via pthread TSD + calloc/malloc) RETURNED NULL -> __s2b_D2A unchecked NULL deref. Freshly-forked conftest child parsing numbers => malloc-after-fork / gdtoa-TSD-after-fork NULL in the forked child. VERDICT A: genuine guest-side libsystem_c dtoa fault (cousin of dar-gwn.1.6 strcpy(NULL), DIFFERENT site), NOT Mach/psynch/syscall. The dar-gwn.1.10 HANG is DOWNSTREAM (fault -> in-proc exc handler on sibling fresh thread -> handler blocks psynch_mutexwait on lock held by faulting thread parked in exception_triage -> deadlock). FIX TARGET = fault source (why Balloc returns NULL in forked child). Tracer reverted, trees clean, clean darlingserver 86cdfb89 redeployed. Evidence: ~/work/darling-debug/conftest-dbglog-20260623T125459Z/FAULT-VERDICT.txt

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: CROSS-REF from dar-gwn.6.4 (2026-06-26): a server-side primary-fault tracer caught the libunistring-build conftest fault with a full symbolized backtrace and it is EXACTLY this bead's gdtoa-OOM fault: __s2b_D2A <- __dtoa <- __gdtoa <- __vfprintf <- _addtype <- conftest+0x3df8 (gnulib CHECK_PRINTF_SAFE probe), Balloc()==NULL deref -> SIGSEGV NULL+8. The fatal_exception_delivery bound (this bead's fix, task #55) IS deployed and WORKING (faulting pid dies clean ~3.1s, no wedge). BUT the conftest now dies by SEGV instead of its own nocrash_init EXC_BAD_ACCESS handler catching it + _exit(0) like macOS -> ruby build.rb RC=1 -> libunistring install FAILS. So the .1.10 bound cured the DEADLOCK but did NOT make the conftest survive. To get libunistring green, Darling must DELIVER the fatal EXC_BAD_ACCESS to the guest task handler (vs the bound-fallback terminate). Tracked as dar-gwn.6.4 task #77 (open decision: re-open this bead vs distinct follow-up). See dar-gwn.6.4 HANDOFF comment for the full backtrace + tooling. Nothing pushed.

title: Lost-RPC startup stall: ruby hangs in recvmsg during interpreter init while darlingserver idle (post-dar-gwn.6 fork storm)
description: Surfaced 2026-06-25 once the dar-gwn.6 os_unfair_lock SIGILL was no longer masking it. Under the fork/exec storm (~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro RUNS=4 NWORK=12 ROUNDS=400) a LATER ruby HUNG (not crashed) during interpreter startup: guest ruby has only ~2 threads, main thread wchan=futex_wait_queue, the other thread wchan=__skb_wait_for_more_packets (blocked in recvmsg on its per-thread darlingserver RPC socket) — i.e. waiting for an RPC reply that never arrives. darlingserver is IDLE at the same moment (main thread ep_poll, worker futex_wait_queue), NOT crashed and NOT a zombie. Classic lost-RPC-reply (same family as dar-gwn.1.7 server-alive recvmsg hang and dar-gwn.1.10 silent reply stall). Deployed darlingserver 437407b2 ALREADY carries the prior fixes: dar-gwn.1.7 push_reply-pipe RAII (358d453), dar-gwn.1.10 fatal-EXC reply bound (1ace361), event-loop exception containment (33ac149). So this is either a NEW lost-RPC site or a rarer race those fixes do not cover; the ~2-thread state means it stalls VERY early in ruby init (not the mid-build window of the earlier hangs). NEXT: re-repro with the darling-rpc-loss-watch.sh harness + DSERVER_LOG_LEVEL=debug to NAME the lost call; snapshot the stuck guest /proc/PID/task wchans + the in-flight RPC opcode. Constraint: nothing pushed/published without explicit per-action approval. Split out of dar-gwn.1.5 (which closed as a dar-gwn.6 cascade). See memory dar-gwn15-was-dargwn6-regression, darling-rpc-loss-watch, dar-gwn17-pushreply-pipe-leak.
close_reason: done

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: NAMED 2026-06-25 (live capture): the lost RPC is psynch_mutexwait (call #73), NOT a startup call. The earlier '~2-thread startup' reading was the SAME wedge at thread TEARDOWN (process finished its work; only main + dispatch thread left). Hard deadlock captured under RUNS NWORK=12 ROUNDS=400: ruby guest PID 25 main thread futex_wait_queue, dispatch thread guest-TID 26 in __skb_wait_for_more_packets awaiting the reply to a psynch_mutexwait it just sent; darlingserver IDLE. dserver.log repeating cycle for TID26: recv #73 mutexwait -> recv #14 interrupt_enter (signal) -> repl #73 code=4 EINTR retval=125955 (saved-reply force-completes the wait) -> pthread_canceled(-38) -> interrupt_exit -> 'Going to send saved reply' -> guest retries __psynch_mutexwait -> recv #73 again ... loops hundreds of times, EVERY completion via signal-interrupt (code 4), NEVER via genuine ksyn_mtxsignal handoff. FINAL retry: signal storm quiesced at teardown -> no signal to force saved-reply, and the real unlock wakeup was LOST across interrupt/retry -> permanent deadlock. Mechanism: window where a contended mutexwait waiter is made runnable by sigexc_enter clear_wait_internal(THREAD_INTERRUPTED) (thread lock) while still queued on kwq (kwq spinlock), so a concurrent psynch_mutexdrop ksyn_signal/ksyn_mtxsignal grant is neither delivered nor reliably stashed as kw_intr for the retry (_kwq_handle_interrupted_wakeup kern_synch.c:699 finds nothing). Files: duct-tape/pthread/kern_synch.c (_psynch_mutexwait L672, psynch_mtxcontinue L801, mutexdrop L584-628, ksyn_signal L1967, _kwq_mark/handle_interrupted_wakeup L361/L509); src/thread.cpp _handleInterruptEnterForCurrentThread L1603 + dtape_thread_sigexc_enter thread.c:452. Evidence: ~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro/stall-snap-1782356848/. TDD oracle: ~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro/run-dargwn111.sh (RED=RED-WEDGE, GREEN=ALL_RUNS_DONE). Synthetic C repro mutexwait_eintr.c did NOT repro (insufficient contention). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: REFRAME 2026-06-25: dar-gwn.1.11 is almost certainly NOT a new bug — it is the DEADLOCK FACE of a REGRESSED dar-gwn.1.9 (stale wait_timer). Deployed darlingserver 437407b2 was built from integration/homebrew @1ace361, whose committed tree is MISSING assert-wait-cancel-stale-timer (7344b17) even though patches.yml line 329 registers it (see dar-gax for why the drift check didn't fire — it reflects the worktree, and was never run pre-deploy). Same drift class as dar-gwn.6. With the fix ABSENT: a stale wait_timer fires THREAD_TIMED_OUT on a subsequent untimed wait -> bogus ETIMEDOUT (ruby [BUG] pthread_cond_wait crash, observed: '[BUG] pthread_cond_wait: Operation timed out (ETIMEDOUT)' at sleep_stress2.rb:20, then a SIGCHLD-shaped 0x3e8<<32 mis-tag crashes the crash handler = the old dar-gwn.1.5 cascade) on the cvwait path, OR strands a psynch_mutexwait handoff -> the recvmsg deadlock I captured (this bead) on the mutex path. BOTH are the regressed stale-timer bug. CAVEAT/OPEN: rebuilding darlingserver WITH 7344b17 (c9ef337b) and re-running the ruby-storm oracle hit a NEW panic at RUN1 ROUND225: 'darlingserver duct-tape panic: thread already waiting on <ptr> @waitq.c:2835' (thread->waitq != NULL on re-entry to waitq_assert_wait64_locked) — a SIBLING stale-state bug (a wakeup path that doesn't clear thread->waitq), same family as the wait_timer leak my fix closes. Determining now whether that panic is PRE-EXISTING (reproduces on old binary 437407b2 too) or fix-induced, via an A/B oracle run. Oracle: run-dargwn111.sh. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: DEPLOYED FIX from clean branch: darlingserver c9ef337b now carries 7344b17 (assert-wait cancel) sourced from the regenerated integration/homebrew (see dar-gax). A/B PROVEN: OLD 437407b2 (no fix) -> [BUG] ETIMEDOUT crash at RUN1 (the regressed dar-gwn.1.9 stale-timer = the cvwait FACE; the mutexwait deadlock is the other FACE of the SAME bug). NEW c9ef337b -> 0 ETIMEDOUT, ran far past the old crash point (ROUND 225 vs early RUN1), then hit a SEPARATE deeper panic 'thread already waiting @waitq.c:2835' (thread->waitq not cleared on some wakeup path) which the old binary never reached because it crashed earlier. So the fix is CORRECT+necessary; it unmasks a sibling waitq stale-state bug (new bead). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DEADLOCK STILL REPRODUCES on c9ef337b (carries 7344b17). 2026-06-25 ~09:05: diagnostic build (canonical + a pre-panic printf at waitq.c:2835, functionally identical to c9ef337b) hit the LOST-RPC DEADLOCK at RUN1 ROUND 0 (fast + reliable), NOT the panic.

Captured live (wedge-snap-1782378305): 11 guest threads parked in __skb_wait_for_more_packets, darlingserver FULLY IDLE (main thread = epoll_wait, WorkQueue worker = pthread_cond_wait waiting for work, MessageQueue empty). One TRANSIENT sample caught the main thread mid-__sendmmsg(fd=3,vlen=2,MSG_DONTWAIT) inside MessageQueue::sendMany (holding _lock) while the worker microthread was blocked in TaskSelfTrap::_sendReply -> Thread::pushCallReply -> MessageQueue::push wanting the SAME _lock — but re-sampling 3x showed the main thread back in epoll_wait, so that lock-coupling was transient, NOT the deadlock.

CONCLUSION: 7344b17 (wait_timer cancel) and the waitq:2835 panic are BOTH downstream symptoms. The PRIMARY bug is the kern_synch.c psynch_mutexwait interrupt/retry LOST-WAKEUP race already named in the 03:21 comment: a ksyn_mtxsignal grant is lost across a sigexc interrupt + retry because the waiter is made runnable by clear_wait_internal(THREAD_INTERRUPTED) while still queued on the kwq, and _kwq_handle_interrupted_wakeup finds no stashed kw_intr. The timer-race FACE (ETIMEDOUT cvwait) is now fixed; the mutexwait-handoff FACE (this recvmsg deadlock) and the waitq-clear FACE (waitq:2835 panic) remain — all three are the same signal-vs-grant race.

Variance (ROUND0 deadlock vs ROUND225 panic vs clean) = the timing race. NEXT: fix the kern_synch interrupt/grant-stash race so a signal-interrupted contended mutexwait reliably re-finds its grant on retry. Diagnostic printf left in worktree (waitq.c:2835) under DARLING_SKIP_DRIFT_GATE=ON; canonical c9ef337b redeployed. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE PINNED + candidate fix written (NOT yet verified; verification blocked by harness fragility). 2026-06-25 ~09:20.

MECHANISM (confirmed by reading the full psynch grant/interrupt protocol):
dtape_thread_sigexc_enter (duct-tape/src/thread.c:452) unconditionally did:
    state &= ~(TH_UNINT|TH_WAIT); wait_result = THREAD_INTERRUPTED; clear_wait_internal(THREAD_INTERRUPTED);
This RACES the mutex grant. When psynch_mutexdrop unlocks, ksyn_signal (kern_synch.c:1985) FIRST removes the kwe from the kwq queue, then waitq_wakeup64_thread -> (thread still parked) waitq_select_thread_locked clears thread->waitq + thread_go(THREAD_AWAKENED). If a signal arrives in the window after that grant made the thread runnable but before its continuation runs, sigexc_enter OVERWRITES wait_result AWAKENED->INTERRUPTED. psynch_mtxcontinue then sees error=EINTR; since ksyn_signal already removed the kwe (kwe_kwqqueue==NULL) it does nothing; and because mutexdrop took the ksyn_signal KERN_SUCCESS branch (not KERN_NOT_WAITING), NO kw_intr stash was made. So the grant is silently lost: userspace retries __psynch_mutexwait, _kwq_handle_interrupted_wakeup finds nothing, blocks forever while the unlock already happened => the lost-RPC recvmsg deadlock (and, in the timer-coupled variant, the waitq:2835 panic).

CANDIDATE FIX (duct-tape/src/thread.c dtape_thread_sigexc_enter): guard the interrupt with `still_waiting = (state & TH_WAIT) && thread->waitq != NULL` and only clobber/clear_wait when genuinely still waiting; otherwise let the real wakeup stand (signal stays pending, taken after the syscall returns the grant). NOTE duct-tape thread_unblock does NOT clear TH_WAIT (unlike XNU), so the load-bearing test is waitq!=NULL (the grant's waitq_select_thread_locked clears it). Built d251c70e, deployed.

VERIFICATION BLOCKED / not yet conclusive: the run-dargwn111 watchdog harness is unreliable (daemonized guest detaches; pkill -9 used in teardown CORRUPTS the mount namespace -> next boot fails 'Cannot open mnt namespace file' / semaphore -111, which masquerades as a server CRASH). Wrote run111-clean.sh (uses `darling shutdown` not pkill, tracks pgrep -x mldr). First fix-build clean run reported CRASH at t=3s, but the immediately-following CANONICAL A/B run ALSO failed boot ('Cannot mount') => that CRASH was the poisoned-namespace artifact, NOT the fix. Swept all orphan mldr + stale .darlingserver.sock/.init.pid; env recovered (canonical re-boots clean, no leftover mounts). Fix verification still PENDING a clean A/B. Fix is in the worktree only; canonical c9ef337b currently redeployed. Diagnostic printf at waitq.c:2835 still in worktree (build with -DDARLING_SKIP_DRIFT_GATE=ON). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FIX ATTEMPTS + STATISTICAL A/B (2026-06-25 ~10:45). Built a settle-tolerant, namespace-safe verification harness (~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro/oracle111.sh + ab111.sh): warm boot-probe before the storm, darling-shutdown teardown (NEVER pkill -- it corrupts the mount ns -> next boot 'Cannot open mnt namespace file'/-111), progress via mldr fork-churn (not bursty output size), direct wedge test (stressor ruby in recvmsg + server idle). Verdicts GREEN/CLEAN(pass) / RED-WEDGE / CRASH / BOOT-FAIL.

CONTROL: canonical c9ef337b = 6/6 CLEAN (full WALL each, no crash, no wedge). NOTE the dar-gwn.1.11 deadlock did NOT reproduce in those 6 -- it is RARE (manifested once at ROUND 0 in an earlier diagnostic run); 6 runs is not enough to see its rate, which makes proving a fix statistically hard.

FIX v1 (WRONG LAYER, crashes immediately): guarded dtape_thread_sigexc_enter (duct-tape/src/thread.c) to skip clear_wait when not still_waiting. -> server CRASH at ROUND 0 (dserver_rpc_interrupt_enter failed -111). Reverted. Reason: _handleInterruptEnterForCurrentThread (src/thread.cpp:1603) has ALREADY committed to interrupting and runs the interrupt continuation; skipping clear_wait leaves that RPC state machine inconsistent.

FIX v2 (RIGHT LAYER, runs far, intermittent crash): in psynch_mtxcontinue (kern_synch.c:811) error!=0 branch, when kwe->kwe_kwqqueue==NULL (a grant already dequeued us + set kw_owner + stored kwe_psynchretval) convert the consumed-but-interrupt-clobbered grant into a _kwq_mark_interruped_wakeup(KWQ_INTR_WRITE, lockseq, retval) so the __psynch_mutexwait retry's _kwq_handle_interrupted_wakeup hands it back. Build 2c9e3d3f (a4e0e13 w/ a DARGWN111-STASH diag log). PROVEN the branch FIRES correctly: 2 stashes captured, valid grants (retval 0xa603/0x31403), intr.count=0 (no clobber), server stayed alive that run = CLEAN. BUT across fix2 runs: ~2 CRASH / ~4 valid runs (iter at ROUND150 and ROUND25) vs canonical 0/6 -> fix2 introduces an INTERMITTENT server crash (server goes defunct, dserver.log empty = raw SIGSEGV/abort, not a duct-tape panic). Likely cause (unconfirmed): turnstile/owner bookkeeping conflict -- the original grant already set kw_owner + did turnstile_update_inheritor; psynch_wait_complete (shim turnstile_complete) then runs, and on retry _kwq_handle_interrupted_wakeup re-sets owner while the turnstile was torn down -> inconsistency. NEEDS the actual backtrace.

CAPTURE BLOCKER: the crash is intermittent + timing-sensitive; gdb-attach PERTURBS timing so it didn't reproduce under gdb. Per memory darling-debug-runner, transient crashes need the IN-SERVER fault tracer (instrument Thread::processSignal to dump faulting thread_state+stack at fault time), not external gdb. That is the next concrete step.

HARNESS DEFECT still open: a CRASH leaves orphan mldr + stale .sock/.init.pid that the recover() step does not fully clear -> subsequent A/B iters cascade to BOOT-FAIL. recover() needs to actually restore a bootable state (the manual sweep sequence does, but inside the loop it doesn't always).

STATE: fix v2 + diag log are in the worktree (kern_synch.c) + the waitq.c:2835 diag printf; canonical c9ef337b being redeployed; nothing pushed. Harness: oracle111.sh/ab111.sh/catch-crash.sh/crash.gdb in ~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
STALE-WAITQ FIX landed + verified (build 3f580823db9a, worktree only, NOT pushed). 

FIX: dtape_thread_sigexc_enter (duct-tape/src/thread.c) now enforces XNU's post-wakeup invariant — after clear_wait_internal, if thread->waitq still non-NULL, waitq_pull_thread_locked it (proper unlink), falling back to a direct field-clear (waitq/wait_event/at_safe_point) if the waitq lock-try fails (grant-dequeued case: already off-list, just a dangling ptr). ROOT: the interrupt path cleared TH_WAIT BEFORE clear_wait_internal, so clear_wait_internal returned KERN_NOT_WAITING early and left thread->waitq stale across the grant-vs-interrupt race; the userspace retry's next ksyn_wait -> waitq_assert_wait64_locked then tripped 'thread already waiting'.

VERIFIED via fault-traced A/B (catch-fwl.sh, 10 completing storms): the waitq panic class is GONE — 0 DARGWN-WAITQPANIC / 0 'thread already waiting' across the whole run (was the dominant crash before). STASH still fires (recovery active).

NEXT LAYER EXPOSED (caught by fault tracer, iter 3, signal=6 SIGABRT, NOT the waitq panic): an UNCAUGHT C++ throw 'Thread has both a pending call and a pending continuation' (thread.cpp:518) in Thread::doWork() -> std::terminate -> abort. Resolved chain: doWork (0x9c8fa, runtime_error @rodata 0x109e7c) -> WorkQueue worker. MEANING: same grant-vs-interrupt race, deeper — the thread suspended in ksyn_wait (so _continuationCallback=psynch_mtxcontinue set, line 684) gets a NON-InterruptEnter _pendingCall injected before the continuation runs; doWork's line 506-514 only re-homes the continuation when the pending call IS InterruptEnter, else line 516 throws. This is the RPC/interrupt state-machine layer (same area fix-v1 died in). Investigating next. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
EMPIRICAL UPDATE after stale-waitq fix (build with call-cont diagnostic 1195a8bb45d5):
- Run A (10 storms): 1 SIGABRT (call-cont throw, iter 3), 0 waitq panic.
- Run B (12 storms): 0 crashes, 0 waitq panic, 6 STASH fired clean, 0 CALLCONT.
=> The stale-waitq fix ELIMINATED the dominant crash class (waitq 'thread already waiting' panic: 0 across ~22 storms, was frequent before). The residual 'both pending call and pending continuation' SIGABRT (thread.cpp:518) is RARE (~1/22 storms) and the grant-stash recovery now runs clean most of the time. Cannot pin the call-cont interleaving yet because it didn't reproduce under the DARGWN111-CALLCONT diagnostic in run B; running a denser batch (24 iters, NWORK=14) to force it. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FAST-RED ATTEMPT — NEGATIVE RESULT (worth recording). Tried to replace the 3-min/iter guest storm with a targeted repro (red_psynch_race.rb + red111.sh: boot once, persistent-server rounds, one hot contended Mutex + SIGCHLD storm). Three designs, all FAILED to reach the kernel psynch path:
- v1 (tight shared+=1 spin): 647M increments, 0 STASH -> pure userspace os_unfair_lock fast path, never enters kernel psynch_mutexwait.
- v2 (sleep 0.001 while holding, separate sigger threads): shared dropped to ~391 (real blocking) but STASH=0 -> SIGCHLD landed on non-waiting threads.
- v3 (workers both contend AND fork children): shared ~1900, STILL 0 STASH, 0 PSYNCH kernel activity, 10 rounds clean.
CONCLUSION: synthetic Ruby Mutex contention does NOT generate the psynch_mutexwait grant-vs-SIGCHLD-interrupt traffic the real brew storm does (which fires STASH 6x/run). The real signal comes from concurrent-ruby/ConditionVariable + DownloadQueue poll timing, not raw Mutex#synchronize. REVERTING verification to the proven full storm (sleep_stress2.rb via catch-fwl.sh); accepting the ~3-min/iter cadence as the cost of fidelity. The fast-RED scripts are kept for reference but are not a valid oracle. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
A/B CONTROL — STRONGEST EVIDENCE YET (2026-06-26): the dar-gwn.1.11 stale-waitq fix is NOT load-bearing for the fork-storm workload, and the stale-waitq face cannot arise on this path.

Built an A/B-capable binary 3c2d7c7f6af6 with a runtime DSERVER_DARGWN_FIXOFF=1 gate that disables ONLY the corrective actions (the thread.c:510 waitq-clear block + the kern_synch.c:830 STASH stash) while keeping the RACE/LEFTOVER/STASH detection logging. Ran the proven ruby fork-storm oracle (catch-fwl.sh, ITERS=10 ROUNDS=70 NWORK=12, fresh dserver.log per arm) in BOTH arms on the SAME binary:

  FIX-ON  (gate-off): 9/10 iters clean +1 watch-timeout, NO crash. RACE=6646 LEFTOVER=0 STASH=0 WAITQPANIC=0 ETIMEDOUT=0 CALLCONT=0.
  FIX-OFF (FIXOFF=1):  9/10 iters clean +1 watch-timeout, NO crash. RACE=7073 LEFTOVER=0 STASH=1 (detected, corrective skipped) WAITQPANIC=0 ETIMEDOUT=0.

IDENTICAL clean across ~6.6k/7.1k race-window hits per arm. The corrective code NEVER fired in either arm (LEFTOVER=0 both).

MECHANISTIC ROOT (from source, not just empirics): the grant path waitq_wakeup64_thread -> waitq_wakeup64_thread_locked (waitq.c:3298) -> waitq_select_thread_locked dequeues the thread AND calls thread_clear_waitq_state, so thread->waitq is NULLED atomically with the grant/dequeue. By the time a SIGCHLD-driven dtape_thread_sigexc_enter runs, waitq is ALREADY NULL: (1) clear_wait_internal early-returns KERN_NOT_WAITING at thread.c:1731 (TH_WAIT was cleared at L488 first, so the (state & (TH_WAIT|TH_TERMINATE))==TH_WAIT test is false) WITHOUT re-touching waitq; (2) the fix's `if (waitq != NULL)` clearing block is skipped; (3) the waitq.c:2834 "thread already waiting" panic (still intact, NOT cancelled by assert-wait-cancel-stale-timer which is absent from this committed tree) is never reached. So the stale/dangling-waitq face the fix targets does not occur on this path.

CONCLUSION: with the prior dar-gwn.1.7/.1.8/.1.9/.1.10 fixes present (ff0268d wait_timer cancel, ce7ce5e balanced cvwait, 1ace361 fatal-EXC reply bound, 358d453 pushreply RAII), NO residual stale-waitq bug reproduces. The three original "faces" (recvmsg deadlock / waitq:2835 panic / ETIMEDOUT cvwait) were those already-fixed bugs. The earlier ROUND0-deadlock / ROUND225-panic single-shot observations predate the current build's full fix set; this 20-run A/B + source analysis supersedes them. dar-gwn.1.11 is NOT a confirmed residual bug on the current build.

DISPOSITION OPTIONS (for decision): (a) drop the dar-gwn.1.11 patch as redundant defensive code; (b) keep it as cheap belt-and-suspenders but DEMOTE the bead to "cannot reproduce / defensive-only"; (c) if a residual is still suspected it needs a workload that leaves thread->waitq dangling after a grant — which this analysis argues cannot happen via the standard waitq_select_thread_locked path.

RED test red111.c (guest-side pthread-mutex + SIGCHLD, faithful RPC path; replaces the rejected in-server approach) is built + host-syntax-clean but will NOT distinguish these since it targets the same race; deferred pending disposition. Worktree + build restored to clean fix-ON 7b674434e3a9 (A/B gate removed). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DISPOSITION = DROP (decided by user 2026-06-26). The dar-gwn.1.11 corrective patch is dropped as redundant defensive code, per the conclusive A/B above.

Actions taken:
- Reverted duct-tape/src/thread.c (sigexc clear-block + dtape_dargwn metrics) and duct-tape/pthread/kern_synch.c (psynch_mtxcontinue STASH stash) to committed integration/homebrew. Also reverted the hunt-only inline diagnostics duct-tape/xnu/osfmk/kern/waitq.c (DARGWN-WAITQPANIC) and src/thread.cpp (DARGWN111-CALLCONT).
- KEPT the dar-fwl in-server fault tracer (src/darlingserver.cpp, DSERVER_FAULT_TRACE-gated) — reusable standalone tool, tracked under dar-fwl.
- Rebuilt + redeployed darlingserver 612f8cd096d3 (no DARGWN111 strings; fault tracer present). Worktree now carries ONLY the fault tracer diff.

Rationale: with dar-gwn.1.7 (358d453 pushreply RAII), dar-gwn.1.8/.1.9 (ff0268d wait_timer cancel, ce7ce5e balanced cvwait), dar-gwn.1.10 (1ace361 fatal-EXC reply bound) present, the stale-waitq scenario this patch targeted does not occur: the grant path nulls thread->waitq atomically (waitq_select_thread_locked -> thread_clear_waitq_state), so a SIGCHLD-driven sigexc never finds a dangling waitq, never trips the waitq:2834 panic, never loses the grant. 20-run A/B (fix-ON vs fix-OFF, ~6.6k/7.1k race hits each) = identically clean, corrective never fired (LEFTOVER=0 both arms).

CLOSING dar-gwn.1.11 as CANNOT-REPRODUCE / FIXED-UPSTREAM: the three original faces (recvmsg deadlock, waitq:2835 panic, ETIMEDOUT cvwait) were the already-fixed dar-gwn.1.7/.1.8/.1.9/.1.10 bugs. Re-open only if a real workload is found that leaves thread->waitq dangling after a grant (analysis argues the standard path cannot). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
BREW-INSTALL REAL-WORKLOAD VALIDATION (2026-06-26) on the no-patch build 612f8cd096d3 (dar-gwn.1.11 corrective dropped, fault tracer kept):

GOAL: confirm dar-gwn.1.11 does NOT recur in a real fork-storm workload (brew reinstall wget: dep resolution + sandboxed ruby per formula step = the bug's natural habitat).

RESULT re dar-gwn.1.11: NOT RECURRING — confirmed across 2 runs. In BOTH, every dar-gwn.1.11 server signature was ZERO: no DSERVER FAULT, WAITQPANIC=0, ETIMEDOUT/[BUG]=0, Dropping-message=0, both-pending=0; dserver.log empty. Consistent with the A/B + source analysis (the grant nulls thread->waitq atomically; the stale-waitq face cannot arise).

BUT brew install hit TWO OTHER, pre-existing failures (NOT dar-gwn.1.11, both in the already-tracked dar-gwn.6 / fork-storm family):
- RUN #1: guest-side Ruby [BUG] Segmentation fault at 0x000003e80000130e during `libunistring ./configure`. Address high32 = 0x3e8 = 1000 = the SIGCHLD-mistag signature of the dar-gwn.6/.1.5 cascade (os_unfair_lock ud2 from ulock_wait -EINTR; death-rattle re-enters: "SEGV received in SEGV handler / ABRT received in ABRT handler"). Server clean. BREW_REINSTALL_RC=1, INSTALLED_MISSING.
- RUN #2: HUNG at the same libunistring phase. After ~51min: guest process set FROZEN (youngest mldr 51min old, zero new forks), 16 guest threads in recvmsg (__skb_wait_for_more_packets), server idle/near-idle in ep_poll, 1 guest thread on-CPU not forking. A wedge, not a crash. No server fault/panic. Snapshot: ~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro/brew-wedge-snap-1782437687.

INTERPRETATION: crash (run1) vs hang (run2) at the same dependency = the two faces of the intermittent fork-storm dar-gwn.6 family, NOT a deterministic path bug and NOT dar-gwn.1.11. The dar-gwn.6 EINTR-retry fix IS present in source (xnu .../psynch/ulock_wait.c has the `do{futex}while(ret==-EINTR && timeout==0)` loop) but the DEPLOYED libsystem_kernel.dylib is md5 228d19a33a9c (NOT the memory's clean-fix 8393f649) — strongly suggests the deployed dylib drifted / was built without the fix. This is the recurring patchset-drift trap (see memory patchset-drift-integration-homebrew, dar-gwn6-ulock-eintr-sigill REGRESSION TRAP).

HARNESS FIX en route: brew-install-validate.sh originally reported exit 0 despite RC 127 inside the guest (three swallow points: last guest cmd was echo not `exit $rc`; `darling shell` does not forward guest RC; no set -e). Hardened so the guest script does `exit $rc` and the verdict is decided on stdout evidence (BREW_REINSTALL_RC + INSTALLED_OK + signature counts), never on exit code. Also: in-guest `timeout` does not exist (macOS userland) — removed (was causing RC 127).

NEXT (separate from dar-gwn.1.11, needs a decision): rebuild+redeploy libsystem_kernel.dylib WITH the dar-gwn.6 ulock-EINTR fix (verify via `west patch verify` / disasm `_sys_ulock_wait` untimed path shows the -4/EINTR retry), then re-run brew install to confirm both the crash and the hang clear. Env clean, nothing pushed.

title: Design fix for interrupt_enter/PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race
description: Design the fix for the interrupt_enter/PUSH_UNKNOWN_REPLIES reply-deferral race (root cause in dar-gwn.1). Read fully: (a) client generated interrupt wrapper + push_reply/unknown-reply handling in the generated dserver_rpc header (from generate-rpc-wrappers.py ~1340-1500); (b) darlingserver server-side interrupt_enter/exit + deferred-reply state machine in src/thread.cpp (pushCallReply ~1361; _interrupts stack savedReply/interruptedCall, _deferredReply, _pendingSavedReply, _handlingInterruptedCall ~1520-1580). Pinpoint the exact lost-reply window. Options: (B, preferred) surgical fix of the deferral state machine, low-risk; (A, long-term) eliminate the race class via seq-id reply matching + client-side buffering of out-of-order replies instead of bounce-back-to-server; (C, mitigation only) reduce SIGCHLD pressure during psynch RPC. Validate red->green with forkhunt6 (now reliably catches the hang).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Design complete -> pr-drafts/darling-interrupt-reply-deferral-race.md. Chosen Option B: per-thread FIFO of deferred replies (replace single-slot _interrupts.top().savedReply / _pendingSavedReply), deferral predicate widened from instantaneous _interruptedForSignal to !_interrupts.empty(), flush whole FIFO only at the OUTERMOST interrupt_exit (when _interrupts becomes empty). Root cause = positional single-slot reply stash (Defect A) + lock-blocked resume-during-interrupt (thread.cpp:1523 FIXME, Defect B). Files: thread.hpp(+_deferredInterruptReplies queue), thread.cpp(pushCallReply ~1361, _handleInterruptEnterForCurrentThread ~1528), call.cpp(push_reply ~147, InterruptExit ~754).

title: Fix lost DarlingServer timer expiry delivery
description: Fix intermittent Homebrew/Ruby hangs caused by timerfd expiry delivery being stranded in the shared Thread::kernelAsync runner queue. Route each timerfd expiry through a registered dedicated kernel microthread that self-terminates after dtape_timer_fired(), avoiding the lossy shared queue without leaking per-expiry thread resources.
acceptance_criteria: Targeted Ruby timed-wait stress has zero hangs across a long run; heavy timed-wait stress completes without unbounded darlingserver RSS or host-thread growth; darlingserver builds cleanly; original Homebrew scenario is attempted and any unrelated blocker is documented.
close_reason: Timer expiry lost-wakeup fixed and validated: targeted 50/50, heavy 40k stable resources; original Homebrew network stall documented.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: TDD: added regression tests/regression/ruby_fork_signal_interrupt.rb (+run-*.sh): hand-rolled Mutex/ConditionVariable thread pool (psynch traffic) + storm of fork/exec/reap short-lived children (SIGCHLD storm), mirroring Homebrew DownloadQueue. RED baseline = git-HEAD darlingserver (md5 ee15dd, byte-identical to validated 09:02 prefix binary): intermittently FAILS (crash: SIGILL/ABRT in sigexc_handler->free_tiny, i.e. signal delivered mid-malloc / corrupted sigprocess state restore). Initial A/B: orig run1 PASS, run2 FAIL(crash) -> reproduces ~prod flakiness. Fixed binary md5 c47d26 boots clean (DARLING_OK). GREEN run in progress. NOTE: manifestation is crash not pure recvmsg-hang; same signal/interrupt root area, validating whether the reply-deferral FIFO fix resolves it.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: NEGATIVE RESULT. A/B (TASKS=400, 5 iters each): orig(ee15dd) pass=1/fail=4, fixed(c47d26) pass=2/fail=3. ALL failures identical: ruby_fork_signal_interrupt.rb:89 reader.read -> [BUG] Illegal instruction at 0x...f4f. Crash rate UNCHANGED by the fix (within noise). Crash = corrupted register context on sigexc save/restore: signal interrupts ruby in read/free_tiny -> sig_restorer -> sigexc_handler -> handler_linux_to_bsd -> fatal signal on garbage RIP (rax=-1,r11=0x246 = post-syscall state; faulting rip in a higher module than libsystem_kernel/malloc). => Option B reply-deferral FIFO does NOT fix this. Either (a) root-cause diagnosis (reply-deferral race) is wrong, or (b) reproducer triggers a DIFFERENT bug than the production idle-server recvmsg HANG. Reproducer is reliable (~70% crash) unlike the rare hang. Fix code is committed-worthy as a robustness improvement but is UNVALIDATED against the real bug. Need direction: re-diagnose the SIGILL crash (gdb) vs dial reproducer down to confirm hang-vs-crash identity.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: SCOPE CORRECTED: the fix is NOT in the interrupt state machine (Option B/dar-gwn.1.2 ruled out). Real fix = stabilize kwq identity in ksyn_wqfind/ksyn_wqrelease (kern_synch.c) so the same userspace mutex always maps to one kwq while waiters are enqueued. See dar-gwn.1 confirmed-root-cause comment + pr-drafts/darling-psynch-kwq-lost-wakeup-findings.md. Validate with the new real-deadlock detector hunter nhs:/tmp/forkhunt-ph.sh (psynch-frozen-90s), many cold iters ZERO hangs. NOTE old forkhunt6 stdout-stall heuristic gives FALSE POSITIVES (slow downloads); use the psynch-frozen detector.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 local diagnosis update: kwq identity/process-shared-object and interrupt reply FIFO hypotheses were ruled out by lifecycle logs/A-B. The observed Ruby watchdog hang is a lost timed wait: watchdog sleep(30) remained blocked while unrelated 60s timed waits fired. Found a concrete DarlingServer timerfd race: timer expiry is processed asynchronously, and its stale timer_queue_expire result could overwrite a concurrently armed earlier deadline; _currentTimerDeadline also remained active during the async window. Local fix in src/server.cpp clears the fired deadline before async processing and never moves an active timerfd later (a cancelled earlier timer produces one harmless spurious wakeup). Validation so far: darlingserver rebuild passes; Ruby sleep(30) returned in 30.001s; 40-thread/4000-short-sleep Ruby timer stress completed; Rust runner gentle-timeout smoke leaves no Darling processes. Full Homebrew/grace validation is pending because the disposable test prefix Homebrew checkout is damaged/missing its original .git. Task title/description remain stale and should not drive a kwq-identity implementation.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 A/B update: do NOT commit the current timerfd fix yet. Built baseline/fixed pairs differing only in src/server.cpp, then clean pairs with all unconditional [PH] psynch tracing removed. Initial traced A/B was invalid due logging overhead (baseline 4/5 completed, fixed 3/5). Clean targeted timer stress also failed to validate the fix: large workload timed out on both; small workload had baseline complete with max_sleep≈1.005s while fixed timed out. This means the timerfd race remains a useful lead, but the current implementation is incomplete or introduces another lost-timer ordering. Next step: narrow timer_arm/timerfd-fired/timer_queue_expire tracing with deadline values and ordering, then deterministic A/B. Also fixed Rust runner semantics so non-zero child exit is RESULT=failed rather than success.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 confirmed root cause and A/B: timerfd itself fires correctly, but timer expiry was submitted through Thread::kernelAsync and could be lost in the shared runner queue. Narrow tracing captured terminal sequence: timerfd fired -> kernelAsync push queue=1 available=1 -> timerfd expiry queued, with NO worker pop / dtape_timer_fired start afterward. No worker-interrupted or spurious-empty path appeared. The stale available=1 state causes producers not to spawn a replacement; because timerfd is one-shot and _currentTimerDeadline still holds the fired deadline, later timer arms are skipped and all timed waits wedge. Scoped candidate fix routes timerfd expiry through a new kernelAsyncDedicated path, bypassing the unreliable shared queue. Clean A/B on identical Ruby timed-wait stress: baseline pass=8/12 timeout=4/12; dedicated fix pass=12/12 timeout=0. Longer fixed run: 30/30 pass. Important unresolved tradeoff: heavy 80-thread/40k-sleep fixed run completed but DarlingServer RSS grew from ~139 MiB to ~454 MiB while host thread count stayed 2; baseline stayed ~27 MiB. This suggests per-expiry dedicated microthreads/stacks are too expensive or retained. Do not finalize/commit this implementation as-is; next step is a reliable dedicated persistent timer runner or fixing shared kernelAsync wakeup/accounting, then repeat A/B and original Homebrew scenario.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 resource-safe candidate update: replaced thread-per-expiry prototype with one reusable Server::_timerThread. Timerfd expiry is scheduled directly on this dedicated duct-taped kernel microthread via startKernelThread(), bypassing the shared kernelAsync runner queue whose semaphore wakeup was proven lossy. Validation: reusable candidate 20/20 targeted runs completed; heavy 80 Ruby thread / 40,000 timed-wait run completed; DarlingServer RSS stayed flat at ~27 MiB (same as baseline) instead of the thread-per-expiry prototype growing to ~454 MiB; host thread count stayed 2; no "attempt to re-run already running microthread" warnings. Clean baseline comparison remains baseline 8/12 pass, 4/12 timeout; thread-per-expiry/dedicated delivery was 12/12 and 30/30 but rejected for memory cost. Reusable timer-thread candidate is now the implementation to validate with a longer run and Homebrew.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 reusable timer microthread long-run result: 49/50 targeted Ruby timer-stress iterations completed, iteration 19 hit a genuine hard timeout before printing done. No "attempt to re-run already running microthread" warning appeared, but Thread::startKernelThread() is not a reusable work-queue API: setupKernelThread() unconditionally overwrites continuation state, so the reusable Server::_timerThread approach remains racy and must not be committed. Replaced it with a new candidate: one dedicated kernel microthread per timerfd expiry whose callback calls dtape_timer_fired() and then self-terminates, allowing resources to be reclaimed. Build passes; memory/long-run validation pending.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 self-terminating per-expiry candidate update: first attempt crashed because directly created kernel Thread was not registered; fixed by calling registerWithProcess() and threadRegistry().registerEntry(..., true), matching the standard duct-tape kernel-thread creation hook. After cleaning the crashed run wrapper, clean targeted smoke passed 5/5. Heavy 80-thread / 40,000 timed-wait Ruby run completed; darlingserver RSS rose only from 26.9 MiB to a 28.2 MiB-ish steady/28.9 MiB peak and host thread count stayed 2, versus the earlier non-terminating per-expiry prototype reaching ~454 MiB. Long-run flake validation pending.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-09 final candidate validation: registered self-terminating per-timer-expiry microthread passed targeted long run 50/50 (0 timeouts), versus clean baseline 8/12 and rejected reusable-thread candidate 49/50. Heavy 80-thread/40,000-wait run completed with host thread count 2 and peak darlingserver RSS 28.9 MiB. brew --version completed. Original brew install grace reached Fetching downloads and fetched packages.big_sur.jws.json, then timed out at 300s while Ruby threads were blocked in socket receive and darlingserver continued servicing a 5-second timed-wait cycle; this is a network/download stall, not the prior lost-timer hang. Candidate is ready for a scoped commit; no push/PR.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Implementation committed locally (not pushed): darlingserver abe18e0 Fix lost timer expiry delivery; darlingserver 6a1bb01 Remove disproven hang experiment changes; top-level 2f3c4c6 Add reusable Darling debug runner; 170bdf7 Update darlingserver timer delivery fix; 11dbf04 Record Homebrew debugging submodule state. comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 underlying shared-queue root cause refined: kernelAsyncRunnersAvailable is only the victim-visible stale state; the actual lost wakeup is at the duct-tape blocking boundary. Concrete interleaving: kernelAsync worker increments available, semaphore_wait marks its XNU thread TH_WAIT, and thread_block_parameter() observes waiting=true. Before it calls dtape_hooks->thread_suspend()/C++ Thread::suspend(), a producer signals the semaphore. thread_unblock() sets THREAD_AWAKENED and calls Thread::resume(), but resume() sees C++ _suspended=false and returns without scheduling. The signal has already selected/consumed this semaphore waiter, then the worker calls suspend(), sets _suspended=true, and sleeps forever. It never returns from dtape_semaphore_down_simple(), so available remains 1; future producers neither spawn a worker nor get their signaled token consumed. This is a generic check-then-suspend race, not queue locking corruption. The timer fix safely bypasses it, but the common Thread suspend/resume protocol still needs a separate fix.

title: Fix duct-tape Thread suspend/resume lost wakeup
description: Fix the generic lost-wakeup race between duct-tape waits and DarlingServer Thread::suspend()/resume() using a coalesced resume permit. A wake arriving before or during physical suspension is retained until suspend consumes it or doWork safely reschedules the stopped microthread. Restore timer expiry delivery to shared Thread::kernelAsync and remove the dedicated timer workaround.
acceptance_criteria: Amplified wake-before-suspend reproducer fails on baseline and passes with fix; ordinary Ruby timed-wait stress has zero hangs across long runs; heavy timed-wait stress completes with stable resources; timer delivery uses shared kernelAsync; build and Homebrew scenario are validated.
close_reason: Generic suspend/resume lost wakeup fixed and proven; timer workaround removed; amplified and long stress validation passed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 deterministic RED/GREEN: temporarily inserted a 20ms delay after duct-tape thread_block_parameter() observed TH_WAIT and before C++ Thread::suspend(), directly widening the proven wake-before-suspend window. With timer delivery restored to shared Thread::kernelAsync, baseline protocol timed out 3/3 on a small 50-wait Ruby workload; new handshake passed the identical amplified workload 10/10. The amplifier has been removed. The generic fix handles both windows: resume before _suspended sets _resumePending, consumed by suspend; resume after _suspended but while _running cancels that in-progress suspend instead of scheduling a concurrently-running microthread.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 negative result: deterministic amplified RED/GREEN proves the identified resume-before-suspend race exists and the handshake closes that widened window, but it is NOT sufficient to replace the timer workaround. With amplifier removed and timer restored to shared kernelAsync, ordinary long stress produced timeouts at iterations 25, 34, and 35 (34/37 before stopping). Therefore shared kernelAsync has at least one additional failure mode; do not remove the dedicated timer delivery fix yet. Next step: narrow tracing of the new terminal state.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-06-10 final proof/results: implemented a coalesced _resumePending permit covering early, in-progress, and late wakeups. resume() records one permit only while running/suspended; suspend() consumes it before sleeping or after context capture; if a wake arrives after the final suspend-side check, doWork() safely reschedules after the microthread physically stops. Removed the dedicated per-timer microthread workaround and restored Thread::kernelAsync(dtape_timer_fired). Deterministic 20ms amplified race: baseline timeout 3/3, full permit fix pass 10/10. Ordinary full-fix runs: 50/50 plus final edge-case smoke 20/20. Heavy 80-thread/40,000-wait run completed, peak RSS 27.6 MiB, host threads 2. brew --version completed. brew install grace again reached network socket wait and timed out at 300s while server remained healthy; not a timer hang. No concurrent microthread re-run warnings.

Renamed _resumePending to _resumePermit and documented invariants/early-wake/context-capture/late-wake transitions inline. Added tools/repro-darlingserver-resume-race.sh plus durable amplified binaries under ~/work/darling-debug/bin. Script verification: baseline pass=0 timeout=3; fixed pass=3 timeout=0.
