title: Fix SECONDARY cascade: reaped-child SIGCHLD to parent ruby is mis-delivered as linux=11/SI_USER (SIGCHLD-as-SIGSEGV)
close_reason: Resolved-by-dar-gwn.6: not a distinct bug. The SIGCHLD-as-fatal-SI_USER signature was cascade aftermath of the os_unfair_lock ud2 (dar-gwn.6), which had silently regressed via an integration/homebrew build missing fix 9d5c222. Fix restored -> 3x clean fork-storm runs (RUNS=4 NWORK=12 ROUNDS=400), 0 SIGILL, 0 mistag. Removed the stale dar-pot blocker (artifact of the disproven orphaned-process hypothesis). Distinct lost-RPC startup stall split into dar-gwn.1.11.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
SCOPE / DEFINITION OF DONE (this is the 'довести до конца' task):
1. Fix the darlingserver-side defect so a SIGCHLD is no longer mis-delivered as a spurious SIGSEGV.
2. Capture the fix as a PROPER PATCH in the workspace patchset (patches/homebrew/darlingserver/<name>.patch + register in patches/homebrew/patches.yml with module darling/src/external/darlingserver, source SHA + sha256, pr-draft), mirroring the existing darlingserver patches (a82cff6-lineage: microthread-resume-race, cancel-stale-wait-timer, etc.).
3. APPLY the patch in the test build (rebuild darlingserver, deploy to install root darling-prefix + the homebrew prefix) and prove it loads.
4. VERIFY end-to-end: 'brew install wget' completes green under DPREFIX=darling-prefix-homebrew-test (all deps incl. libunistring build+pour from source, wget --version runs), repeated enough times to show the flaky race is gone. Then close dar-gwn.1, and (with dar-pk4) the dar-gwn epic.

ROOT CAUSE (confirmed, live gdb 2026-06-21, 3 deterministic catches; full evidence in dar-gwn.1 comment + memory brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause):
A SIGCHLD (child reaped during the concurrent-ruby fork-storm) arriving while a thread is parked in wait4 (rax=-512 ERESTART) is delivered to Ruby as SIGSEGV. Proof it is a RESENT signal not a hardware fault: $_siginfo si_signo=11 but **si_code=0 (SI_USER)**, payload is SIGCHLD's (_sigchld si_pid=776 si_uid=1000), si_addr=(uid<<32|pid)=0x3e8_00000xxx. Delivery path: sigexc_handler -> state_to_kernel + dserver_rpc_sigprocess (darlingserver picks delivered signal) -> bsd_signum corrupted to 11(SIGSEGV). Same family as this bead's interrupt/RPC-during-signal misroute. FIX LOCUS = darlingserver: src/external/darlingserver signal-reprocessing / sigprocess + interrupt_enter/exit reply routing (thread.cpp; the a82cff6 'RPC changes for better interrupt/signal handling' + 316ce5d 'signal reprocessing' lineage).

RULED OUT (do not re-tread): libsystem_kernel ERESTART->EINTR map in sys_wait4 — built cc930dce, guest-load verified (shasum 0e095b17), libunistring still SEGV'd identically; the -512 never reaches the C path (interrupted at the syscall stub ret). That trial was reverted; dylibs restored.

REPRO / HARNESS (see memory homebrew-run-harness): setuid-ROOT launcher darling-prefix/bin/darling (re-chown root after any host reboot), DPREFIX=darling-prefix-homebrew-test, uid 1000, ONE 'darling shell --login -c'. Deterministic trigger: 'brew install libunistring' (1st build child SEGVs). gdb catch scripts + run1/run2 crash dumps + reproducer ladder in session scratchpad. NOTE: earlier sessions flagged darlingserver submodule as having uncommitted parallel work — VERIFIED CLEAN on 2026-06-21 (homebrew pointer 32af4d5, eunion pointer 2870a59, no uncommitted/stash); still coordinate if parallel work resumes. Constraint: nothing pushed without explicit per-action approval.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LIVE-TRACING UPDATE (2026-06-21): the ROOT CAUSE above is DISPROVEN. Attached gdb to the live darlingserver and dprintf-traced Thread::processSignal AND Thread::sendSignal across 2 clean deterministic crash repros (ptrace_scope=0 during, restored to 1 after; darlingserver left idle, never pkill -9'd).

FINDING: darlingserver NEVER sends or processes signal 11. Zero SIGSEGV in its signal traffic. The ONLY traffic during the crash window: SIGVTALRM(26) — Ruby MRI's timer thread — sent via tgkill (code=-6 SI_TKILL) in bursts across several ruby Thread objects, followed by SIGABRT(6) which is the AFTERMATH of Ruby's own crash handler calling abort(). So the SIGSEGV is NOT injected by darlingserver via sigprocess/sendSignal.

The SIGSEGV is a GENUINE client-side fault. Client-side gdb at the stop: Thread 1 'mldr' parked in wait4, RIP at the libsystem_kernel syscall-stub 'ret' (rax=-512 ERESTART). But the ret insn is mapped+executable, RSP is in a valid mapped rw stack region (NO overflow), and [rsp] holds a VALID return address into wait4's post-syscall code. A ret with valid SP + valid target cannot fault on a memory access => the SIGSEGV is delivered to the thread from outside while it is parked here, OR a signal handler (SIGVTALRM/SIGCHLD) that ran on this thread faulted and the kernel reports the interrupted (wait4) frame's PC. The si_code=0 / si_addr=(uid1000<<32|pid) cited in the old root cause is gdb reading a STALE/overlapping siginfo union (the prior SIGCHLD's _sigchld fields viewed through _sigfault) — NOT proof of injection.

IMPLICATION: this is NOT a darlingserver sigprocess bug, so the current bead title/scope (fix darlingserver sigprocess) is wrong. The true trigger is unidentified and needs HANDLER-SIDE instrumentation (catch the fault INSIDE the SIGVTALRM/SIGCHLD handler, not at the interrupted frame) — likely a fault in the client signal-handling / sigaltstack / SA_RESTART path under the Ruby fork+timer storm. RULED OUT this session: darlingserver-side injection. Awaiting user steer on whether to keep drilling the client-side handler fault or reframe the bead. Memory brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause updated with the correction.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: FLAKINESS FINDING (2026-06-21): the SEGV is NOT 100% deterministic. A clean 'brew install libunistring' run completed INSTALL_RC=0 / GUEST_DONE with no crash (libunistring built+installed: now in Cellar 1.4.2). So the failure is a flaky RACE in the fork+wait+timer storm during a formula's BUILD phase, not a hard deterministic fault. Earlier '100% deterministic' framing was from a specific timing window. Currently looping 'brew install gettext' (unbuilt wget dep, fork-heavy) with a continuous watcher that attaches gdb to the ruby Library/Homebrew/build.rb child and stops only on SIGSEGV, to capture a real fault with full regs/siginfo. Working suspect: corrupted context restore on sigreturn after a signal handler runs while parked in wait4 — handler_linux_to_bsd (sigaction.c:71-76) runs the app handler on a converted bsd ucontext then writes it BACK into the linux ucontext (mcontext_bsd_to_linux, line 75) that sigreturn restores; a lossy/garbage round-trip would restore a bad RSP/RIP, making the next 'ret' in linux_syscall fault. Symbol map (libsystem_kernel.dylib x86_64 slice, via llvm-nm): linux_syscall=0x2b9fc (fault at +9 = the ret), sigexc_handler=0x2b2f0, handler_linux_to_bsd_wrapper=0x38480, handler_linux_to_bsd=0x28f40, sigexc_altstack(bss)=0x6b000. NOT YET CONFIRMED — awaiting a clean live capture.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: BLOCKER (2026-06-21): live re-repro under the loop harness is stalled by host hygiene. The shared host has accumulated ~48 orphaned darling 'mldr' guest processes across MANY prior sessions (PIDs from 659xxx up to 1993xxx — stuck SN/S, darlingservers dead, unreaped). New 'darling shell' boots now fail at startup ('semaphore_timedwait failed code=-111', darlingserver immediately becomes a zombie), so 'brew install gettext' can't run cleanly to re-trigger the flaky SEGV. Cleaning ALL of them needs a sudo mass-kill of processes NOT created in this session — auto-policy blocked that (correctly; out of scope without user OK). Cleaned up only my own session's loop/watcher; ptrace_scope restored to 1; nothing pushed. NEEDS USER DECISION: either (a) OK to sweep the stale darling mldr guests for darling-prefix-homebrew-test so fresh boots work, or (b) reboot the host / clean the prefix manually. Hypothesis to confirm once boots work again: corrupted ucontext restore on sigreturn (mcontext_bsd_to_linux writeback, sigaction.c:75) after a handler runs while parked in wait4.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
BREAKTHROUGH (2026-06-21, live capture of a HUNG build): caught iter-1 of 'brew install gettext' wedged for 1h+. Root mechanism now UNIFIED and confirmed:

The hung ruby Library/Homebrew/build.rb (pid 2056593) main thread is parked in syscall 47=recvmsg, wchan=__skb_wait_for_more_packets, on fd 1048575 -> socket:[24846842] = its per-thread DARLINGSERVER RPC socket. Its RIP is at libsystem_kernel linux_syscall+9 (off 0x2ba05) with rax=-512 — the IDENTICAL instruction/PC as the spurious-SIGSEGV crashes. So the HANG and the SIGSEGV are the SAME defect at the same spot: a client thread doing a darlingserver RPC recvmsg, whose reply never arrives.

WHY the reply never arrives: darlingserver (pid 2053643) is a ZOMBIE — it EXITED (state=Z, exit_signal=17=SIGCHLD to parent; field37=0, i.e. NOT killed by an uncaught signal; dmesg shows NO darlingserver segfault). So darlingserver terminated on its own (clean-ish exit / internal abort / premature self-shutdown) WHILE a client thread had an in-flight RPC. The RPC socket isn't seen as EOF by the blocked recvmsg, so it waits forever in __skb_wait_for_more_packets. The setuid-root 'darling shell' launcher (pid 2053642, still S) never wait()ed darlingserver -> it stays a zombie, and the guest tree is orphaned (== the dar-pot leak; SAME root event).

UNIFIED PICTURE: darlingserver exits mid-RPC -> (a) in-flight client recvmsg hangs forever (this capture), OR (b) under signal timing, the same parked linux_syscall+9 frame is hit by a signal and the restart/return corrupts -> spurious SIGSEGV. Plus (c) orphaned guests never reaped = dar-pot.

NEW PRIMARY QUESTION: WHY does darlingserver exit mid-session? Need to catch darlingserver's own exit (attach gdb to it, break on exit/_exit/abort/main-return, or run it under strace/with its logging) during a hang/crash repro. Fix likely twofold: (1) darlingserver must not exit while clients have live RPCs (or must close/EOF all client sockets on shutdown so recvmsg returns); (2) client recvmsg on the dserver socket must detect peer death (EOF/ECONNRESET) and fail the RPC instead of blocking forever. dar-pot fix (reap/kill guests on darlingserver death) is the third leg. Captured live; nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LIVE SESSION 2026-06-21 (cont'd) — two key results; SEGV and darlingserver-exit are SEPARATE outcomes (correcting the prior "unified" framing):

(1) SEGV REPRODUCED TWICE under a darlingserver that STAYED ALIVE. brew install libunistring/gettext build child died by SEGV (then Ruby's own handler -> ABRT), but the darlingserver serving it did NOT exit (pgrep alive, init tree launchd/securityd/memberd/iokitd/shellspawn intact). Client-side gdb at the SIGSEGV: $_siginfo si_signo=11 BUT si_code=0 (SI_USER) and si_addr=0x3e80000081c = (uid1000<<32)|pid -> a RESENT-SIGNAL payload, NOT a hardware fault. RIP sits on a `ret` (verified x/5i: ret;nop;nop) with valid RSP and a valid return target on the stack — a ret with valid SP+target cannot fault. CONCLUSION: the SEGV is a signal DELIVERED to a thread parked in a syscall and re-tagged as signal 11; it is a CLIENT-SIDE signal-delivery defect and does NOT require darlingserver to exit. This DECOUPLES the SEGV variant from the hang/exit variant.
   Symbolization caveat: resolving the bt against the on-disk libsystem_kernel.dylib gives NONSENSE offsets — the rebuilt loose dylib (Jun 21 03:51) has linux_syscall=0x2bbf0, sys_wait4=0x373e0, sigexc_handler=0x2b470, handler_linux_to_bsd=0x29090, handler_linux_to_bsd_wrapper=0x38680 (ALL different from prior sessions' map), AND the guest may map from the dyld_shared_cache, so /proc maps base + on-disk nm don't align. Need cache-aware symbolization (guest-side atos, or dump the actual mapped page) for the in-handler frames.

(2) WHY darlingserver EXITS (the hang variant) — STATIC ROOT CAUSE FOUND + partially confirmed live. darlingserver's microthread worker calls processCall() with NO surrounding try/catch: src/thread.cpp:346 (Thread::microthreadWorker, runs ON a makecontext fiber stack) and src/call.cpp:240 (kernelAsync). call.cpp:567 literally says "TODO: wrap all processCall calls in try-catch like this" — most processCall handlers are UNWRAPPED. Many handlers throw: process.cpp:324 std::runtime_error("Main thread for process died?") [exec/replacement path — fires on every posix_spawn/execve], :484/:508 std::system_error(ESRCH) memoryInfo/memoryRegionInfo, :556 EFAULT / :650 ESRCH writeMemory/readMemory; thread.cpp:1306-1343 S2C mmap/mprotect/msync. These are exactly the races that fire when a guest thread/process dies concurrently with an in-flight RPC referencing it. An uncaught throw unwinding OUT of a makecontext-entered fiber (microthreadWorker ends in __builtin_unreachable, never returns normally) is UB -> std::terminate -> process exit, with NO darlingserver log line — matching the observed signalless "just stopped logging" exit (and the zombie's field37=0 = not killed by a signal, so NOT abort-SIGABRT; consistent with terminate->_exit-path or normal-ish exit).
   LIVE CONFIRMATION: dprintf on __cxa_throw in the live darlingserver logged 29 throws of std::system_error (typeinfo matched libstdc++ "std::system_error" EXACTLY, +0x2704f8 delta=0; throw originates inside libstdc++) DURING the fork-storm crash window. All were CAUGHT this run (server lived) — the fatal exit happens when one is thrown from an UNWRAPPED processCall path. Prior log line "procmem Error: Failed to write N bytes ... No such process" = darlingserver writeMemory to a dead guest = a std::system_error(ESRCH) source.

FIX DIRECTION (darlingserver, two INDEPENDENT legs):
- Leg A (exit/hang + dar-pot): wrap the processCall dispatch in a top-level try/catch at BOTH call sites — thread.cpp:346 (microthreadWorker) and call.cpp:240 (kernelAsync) — so a thrown std::system_error/runtime_error becomes an error REPLY to the client (or a logged-and-dropped call) instead of terminating the whole server. This stops the server-exit -> client-recvmsg-hangs-forever chain AND the dar-pot guest leak (same root event). Belt-and-suspenders: client recvmsg on the dserver socket should detect peer death (EOF/ECONNRESET) and fail the RPC instead of blocking forever.
- Leg B (SEGV): client-side signal delivery — a parked-in-syscall thread receives a signal that arrives tagged as SIGSEGV (si_code=0). Locus is the guest signal path (sigexc / handler_linux_to_bsd / dserver_rpc_sigprocess bsd_signum selection), NOT darlingserver exit. Needs the in-handler capture with cache-aware symbols.

Harness note: darlingserver is REUSABLE across boots while alive — a 2nd `darling shell` with sockets left in place attaches to the running server + init tree (no re-clean needed); only clean sockets for a cold boot. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LEG A IMPLEMENTED + BUILDS + BOOTS (2026-06-21, not yet shipped as patch / not brew-verified):

CHANGE (darlingserver source, uncommitted, NOT pushed):
- src/thread.cpp Thread::microthreadWorker (~line 346): wrapped _activeCall->processCall() in try/catch. On std::system_error -> log (call name + what + code) and attempt _activeCall->sendBasicReply(-code) inside a nested try (calls that return data have no basic-reply override and the base throws "cannot send a basic reply" -> swallowed). On std::exception / ... -> log + sendBasicReply(-EINVAL) best-effort. Then the normal post-call setcontext flow runs, so the SERVER SURVIVES instead of std::terminate-ing when an uncaught exception unwinds off the makecontext fiber stack. Added #include <system_error>,<cerrno>.
- src/call.cpp kernelAsync processCall (~line 240): same containment for the thread-less path (no client to reply to -> log + drop). Added #include <system_error>. Uses DarlingServer::Call::callNumberToString(result->number()) (scoped enum needs the static cast helper, not raw dserver_callnum_to_string).

BUILD/DEPLOY LESSON (cost a cycle; now in memory darling-build-deploy): there are TWO build trees on the same source. MUST build in /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-build (CMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX=/home/ilyagulya/work/darling-prefix), NOT darling-dev/darling/build (prefix=/usr/local). The /usr/local one bakes LIBEXEC_PATH=/usr/local/libexec/darling into the binary -> every boot fails "Cannot mount overlay: No such file or directory". Sanity-check before deploy: `strings <bin> | grep darling-prefix/libexec/darling$` must print the darling-prefix path. Deploy target: /home/ilyagulya/work/darling-prefix/bin/darlingserver (single copy; homebrew-test prefix shares it). Backup at .pre-trycatch.bak.

STATUS: built clean in darling-build (md5 286f784b1b6c55902e61de3cb1aac873, correct prefix baked), deployed, boots cleanly (zero "Cannot mount overlay", darlingserver comes up + shuts down clean). NOT YET: (a) multi-iteration brew install verification that the hang/zombie/orphan-guest outcome is gone; (b) captured as a patch in patches/homebrew/darlingserver/ + registered in patches.yml; (c) pr-draft. Nothing committed or pushed.

CAVEAT on Leg A's reach: sendBasicReply only exists for zero-reply-param calls; the throwing memory ops (writeMemory/readMemory/memoryInfo, process.cpp) mostly RETURN data, so their clients won't get an unblocking reply and could still hang on that specific RPC — BUT the server no longer dies, so all OTHER guests keep working and the whole-system wedge + dar-pot mass leak is prevented. Belt-and-suspenders (client-side recvmsg peer-death detection) still wanted for full hang elimination.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LEG A MULTI-BOOT VERIFICATION (2026-06-21, fixed darlingserver md5 286f784b deployed, 5 cold boots of `brew install gettext` = build libunistring-from-source, 300s/iter watchdog):

Per-iter (post-boot dsrv/zombie/guests, then after-cleanup):
  1: build SEGV (INSTALL_RC=1)  | post-boot dsrv ALIVE, zombie=NONE, mldr=6 | after-cleanup dsrv=0 mldr=0 zombie=0
  2: WEDGED@300s (fetch-phase hang) | hung dsrv ALIVE, zombie=NONE, mldr=9 | after force-teardown: mldr=0 zombie=0
  3: build SEGV  | dsrv ALIVE zombie=NONE mldr=7 | cleanup 0/0/0
  4: build SEGV  | dsrv ALIVE zombie=NONE mldr=6 | cleanup 0/0/0
  5: build SEGV  | dsrv ALIVE zombie=NONE mldr=7 | cleanup 0/0/0
Tally: 0 SUCCESS, 4 build-SEGV, 1 hang. Host clean after loop (0 darlingserver, 0 mldr).

KEY RESULT for Leg A / dar-pot: in ALL 5 boots darlingserver was ALIVE and NON-ZOMBIE; ZERO orphaned mldr guests survived once the boot ended (clean shutdown OR force-teardown of a wedge). The dar-pot mass-leak symptom (zombie darlingserver + dozens of reparented mldr guests) did NOT occur in any iteration. The mldr counts shown post-boot (6-9) are the NORMAL resident Darling init tree (launchd/securityd/memberd/iokitd/shellspawn + transient build/download children), all cleared on teardown — not leaks.

HONEST SCOPE — what this does and does NOT prove:
- Does NOT prove Leg A "prevented" an exit, because NO iteration hit the darlingserver-exit path even with the fix (that path is flaky and didn't fire in these 5). So this is a non-regression + no-leak result, not a direct before/after of the exit bug. The fix's value is established by the static analysis + the live __cxa_throw evidence (std::system_error IS thrown during the storm and is now contained); these 5 boots confirm the fix doesn't break boot/build/teardown and leaves no zombies/leaks.
- The TWO remaining failures are SEPARATE, PRE-EXISTING, still-open bugs, NOT Leg A's target:
  * Leg B spurious SEGV: 4/5. darlingserver stays alive; client-side signal re-tag. Unfixed (next).
  * dar-gwn.1 fetch-phase hang: 1/5 (iter 2). build.rb stuck in wchan=pipe_read (waiting on a curl child via pipe) during "Fetching downloads", darlingserver ALIVE+idle, NOT a zombie. This is the ORIGINAL dar-gwn.1 curl-fork-storm reply-loss hang, a THIRD distinct flavor — not the darlingserver-exit hang.

So there are really THREE failure flavors, not the two I split earlier: (A) darlingserver-exit-mid-RPC hang [Leg A target; did not fire this run], (B) spurious SEGV [client signal], (C) dar-gwn.1 fetch/curl reply-loss hang [darlingserver idle-alive]. Leg A addresses (A)+dar-pot; (B) and (C) remain.

NEXT: (1) capture Leg A as a patch in patches/homebrew/darlingserver/ + patches.yml + pr-draft (the fix is sound + verified non-regressive); (2) to get a clean green `brew install`, (B) and (C) must also be resolved. Nothing pushed.

- registered in patches.yml: source-branch fix/processcall-exception-guard, source-commit 23101838, bead dar-gwn.1.5, publication-status: BLOCKED (brew not yet green end-to-end; leg B SIGSEGV + dar-gwn.1 fetch-hang still open).
