comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
DUG dar-gwn.6.4 2026-06-26 (server 53e24e16, deployed dylib b66bb1b41d7b). Established the mechanism from the existing crash report; NOT a dar-gwn.6 regression.

(1) dar-gwn.6 NOT regressed: deployed libsystem_kernel.dylib b66bb1b41d7b DOES have the ulock-EINTR retry compiled. _sys_ulock_wait untimed path: after callq _linux_syscall, `cmpl $-0x4,%eax; je <back to futex>` (re-issues NR_futex 0xca) + `cmpl $-0xb,%eax` (EAGAIN). So the 0x3e8 SEGV is NOT the os_unfair_lock ud2 death-rattle of dar-gwn.6.

(2) The crash is in the SIGNAL-DELIVERY path while a thread is parked in a raw Linux syscall. Ruby [BUG] crash report (formula.rb:3560 ':wait' = Process.wait reaping the libunistring build child; portable-ruby):
    - faulting rip = vmaddr 0x2b785 = `_linux_syscall+0x9` = the `retq` IMMEDIATELY after the `syscall` insn (_linux_syscall is just: movl 0x8(%rsp),%eax; movq %rcx,%r10; syscall; retq).
    - rax = 0xfffffffffffffffc = -4 = EINTR (the wait4 was interrupted by an incoming signal). rcx==rip==0x2b785 = normal post-syscall state. rdi=0x5e6 (=1510, the child PID being waited on).
    - C backtrace: sig_restorer+0x39 -> sigexc_handler+0x15c -> handler_linux_to_bsd+0x39a -> ruby sigabrt -> "SEGV received in SEGV handler" + "ABRT received in SEGV handler" = the 0x3e8 line is the CASCADE death-rattle, not the first fault.
    - Server sigprocess (#12) for the crashing PID 124 ALWAYS returns new_bsd_signal_number=26 = SIGVTALRM (Ruby's thread-time-slice timer via setitimer). 27 of them for PID 124.

(3) sigexc_handler flow (emulation/src/linux_premigration/signal/sigexc.c): on a Linux signal -> dserver_rpc_interrupt_enter -> state_to_kernel(ctxt) -> dserver_rpc_sigprocess(bsd,linux,si_pid,si_code,si_addr,&tstate,...) -> state_from_kernel(ctxt) writes regs BACK into the linux ucontext -> if app has a handler, handler_linux_to_bsd(linux,info,ctxt) calls it -> dserver_rpc_interrupt_exit. The faulting rip being EXACTLY the post-syscall retq, with rax=-4, strongly implicates the interrupted-syscall context round-trip (state_to_kernel/state_from_kernel) OR the si_addr=(uid1000<<32|...) payload reaching Ruby's handler and Ruby's handler faulting (the classic dar-gwn.1.5 SIGCHLD mistag, but now NOT a cascade of the ulock ud2 since that is fixed).

HYPOTHESES to disambiguate (need the env-gated sigexc FATAL-only trace, the dar-gwn.1.5 tool: print linux_in/bsd_out/si_code/si_signo/si_addr/si_pid/rip/handler on FATAL bsd signals):
  H1 (mistag): a benign Linux signal (likely SIGCHLD=17 from the reaped child, or the SIGVTALRM timer) is being delivered to Ruby with si_code=0 SI_USER + si_addr=(uid<<32|childpid) and the WRONG/fatal disposition, Ruby treats it as SEGV. = a residual SIGCHLD-mistag, the genuine dar-gwn.1.5 bug now standing on its own.
  H2 (context corruption): state_from_kernel mis-restores the interrupted wait4 context (rip/rsp) so the post-syscall retq returns into garbage -> real SEGV. The fault rip == the retq is suggestive.
NEXT: instrument sigexc_handler (FATAL-only trace) + rebuild/redeploy dylib (TRAP: must keep the ulock-EINTR + dar-gwn.6 fixes in the build tree; build off the patched xnu, not bare integration/homebrew), re-run brew reinstall wget under DSERVER_FAULT_TRACE=1 + debug log (logs truncated at run start, filter by RUN_START_EPOCH), catch the FIRST fatal signal. Cross-ref [[dar-gwn15-was-dargwn6-regression]] [[dar-gwn6-ulock-eintr-sigill]] [[brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause]]. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE CAPTURED 2026-06-26 via the env-gated [dargwn64] fatal-only sigexc trace (DARGWN64_TRACE in sigexc.c, dylib md5 d75ddf12 = trace + dar-gwn.6 ulock-EINTR retained; deployed to overlay). The 0x3e8 SEGV is NOT a SIGCHLD-mistag bug -- the mistag lines are downstream CASCADE. The chronological FATAL-in sequence proves it:

  1. ROOT (t=...021, child PID 4873): linux=11 bsd=11 si_signo=11 si_code=1 si_pid=8 si_addr=0x8 rip=0x779ED3D26EC3 handler=0x0
     -> a GENUINE SIGSEGV: si_code=1 = SEGV_MAPERR (address not mapped), si_addr=0x8 = a NULL-pointer + 8 dereference (reading field at offset 8 of a NULL struct pointer), handler=SIG_DFL -> terminate. linux_in==bsd_out==11 so it ARRIVES from Linux as a real hardware page fault, NOT a mistag. This is in a freshly-forked child during the libunistring build.
  2. CASCADE 3s later (t=...024, parents PID 4872/1509/1472): si_code=0 (SI_USER) si_addr=0x3E800001309 / 0x3E800001308 = (uid1000<<32 | childpid) where 0x1309=4873, 0x1308=4872. These are the SIGCHLD death-rattle to the parents of the dead children, mis-stamped with the uid payload = the classic dar-gwn.1.5 cascade signature (handler 0x100035170 / 0x10026687B = ruby).
  3. Then SIGABRT (linux=6 si_code=-6 si_addr=0x3E800000000, handler 0x1002668D6 = ruby sigabrt) -> Ruby's [BUG] abort path -> the 0x000003e8.... SEGV in the original crash report.

So dar-gwn.6.4 = a REAL NULL+8 deref (SEGV_MAPERR, si_addr=0x8) in a forked child, same CLASS as dar-gwn.1.6 (vchroot path-null SEGV) but a DIFFERENT site/offset. The uid<<32 lines that named this "dar-gwn.6 family / SIGCHLD-mistag" are the secondary cascade, NOT the cause.

NEXT: symbolize root rip=0x779ED3D26EC3 (task #76). It is in a loaded module in the child; need a fresh repro to snapshot the faulting child's /proc/PID/maps for the slide (child is short-lived). Likely a libsystem_kernel / Darling syscall-emulation handler dereferencing a NULL struct at +8 on the post-fork path (cf. dar-gwn.1.6 strcpy(NULL), brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause). Cross-ref [[dar-gwn16-pathnull-guard]], [[brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause]], [[dar-gwn15-was-dargwn6-regression]].

HARNESS NOTE: my CHOSEN-out trace was accidentally UNCONDITIONAL (not gated to the fatal set) -> flooded the log with SIGVTALRM(bsd=26) kprintf RPCs and badly slowed the build (the DEBUG_SIGEXC flood pathology). FATAL-in IS correctly gated and gave the answer (1 root + cascade). For the next capture, gate CHOSEN-out to the fatal set too, or drop it. dylib trace + ulock fix both verified present. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
REFRAMED dar-gwn.6.4 2026-06-26 (two clean traced runs, gated [dargwn64] FATAL-in trace). The root fault is NON-DETERMINISTIC and is a GENUINE hardware fault in the freshly-forked child -- NOT a SIGCHLD-mistag and NOT dar-gwn.6 (ulock verified non-regressed).

Run A root: SIGSEGV si_code=1 SEGV_MAPERR si_addr=0x8 (NULL+8 deref) handler=SIG_DFL, child PID 4873, rip=0x779ED3D26EC3.
Run B root: SIGFPE  si_code=1 FPE_INTDIV  si_addr==rip=0x717B0A0C8BEF (a faulting divide insn) handler=SIG_DFL, child PID 2256.
In BOTH runs the root fault is in a JUST-FORKED child (high nsid), linux_in==bsd_out (arrives from Linux as the real signal), handler=0x0 -> default terminate. Then ~instantly the PARENTS receive si_code=0 (SI_USER) si_addr=(uid1000<<32 | dead-child-pid) (e.g. 0x3E8000008D0 = uid<<32|0x8D0=2256) -> the SIGCHLD death-rattle mistag, handled by ruby (0x100035170 / 0x10026687B / sigabrt 0x1002668D6) -> Ruby [BUG] -> the original 0x000003e8.... SEGV report. So the uid<<32 line that named this "dar-gwn.6 family" is SECONDARY CASCADE.

INTERPRETATION: the child comes out of fork() with broken state and faults on the first real work it does -- the fault VARYING between SIGFPE(INTDIV) and SIGSEGV(NULL+8) across runs is the signature of executing on a corrupted stack/register/TLS/heap context, not a single deterministic null-deref. This is a FORK-CORRECTNESS bug in Darling's fork path under the libunistring build's heavy fork/exec, same FAMILY as the historical brew-sigchld-segv / dar-gwn.1.5/.1.6 children-faulting-after-fork class, but here proven to be the child's OWN fault (not a server-side mistag, not ulock).

SYMBOLIZATION BLOCKED (task #76): the faulting child is gone in <3s (its /proc/PID/maps reads empty/zombie); each guest proc has its own ASLR slide so a sibling's maps don't apply. Need either (a) an in-handler module-relative print (compute rip - libsystem_kernel_base via dladdr in sigexc_handler before abort), or (b) catch the child pre-exit (the runner --gdb --gdb-namespace, memory darling-debug-runner, to break on the child's SIGFPE/SIGSEGV and unwind). Recommend (a): add to the DARGWN64 trace a dladdr(rip) -> "<module>+0x<off>" so the next run names the module directly.

STATE: deployed dylib dc6c1a0b (gated trace) -- NOTE this is a DIAGNOSTIC dylib, must restore the clean b66bb1b41d7b (or rebuild w/o DARGWN64_TRACE) when done. sigexc.c has the temporary DARGWN64_TRACE define + two trace blocks (UNCOMMITTED, do-not-commit). dar-gwn.6 ulock-EINTR retained. Nothing pushed. Cross-ref [[brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause]] [[dar-gwn16-pathnull-guard]] [[dar-gwn15-was-dargwn6-regression]] [[darling-debug-runner]].

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
SYMBOLIZATION (3rd traced run, rip-relative): the root SEGV reproduced again (NULL+8): si_code=1 si_addr=0x8 rip=0x71FC6A926EF1 rip-sigexc=0x9DFB1 handler=0x0, child PID 4873. The rip-vs-sigexc_handler delta = 0x9DFB1 (~647KB). libsystem_kernel's __text is only ~0x56000 (~350KB; last text sym _abort@0x564d0), so 0x9DFB1 OVERSHOOTS libsystem_kernel -> the faulting instruction is NOT in libsystem_kernel; it is in a NEIGHBORING module loaded just above it (candidates by typical layout: libsystem_c / libsystem_malloc / libsystem_pthread / the ruby executable). Cannot pin the exact module from rip alone because each guest process has its own ASLR slide and the dead child's /proc/PID/maps reads empty (<3s lifetime).

CONCLUSION (dar-gwn.6.4 fully characterized, exact instruction still open):
 - The libunistring blocker is a GENUINE, NON-DETERMINISTIC fault in the freshly-forked child: SIGSEGV SEGV_MAPERR si_addr=0x8 (NULL+8 deref) in 2/3 runs, SIGFPE FPE_INTDIV (faulting divide) in 1/3. handler=SIG_DFL -> child dies -> parents get the SIGCHLD death-rattle mis-stamped si_addr=(uid1000<<32|childpid) (si_code=0 SI_USER) -> ruby [BUG] -> the reported 0x000003e8.... SEGV. The uid<<32 is SECONDARY cascade; this is NOT a server mistag and NOT dar-gwn.6 (ulock verified non-regressed).
 - The varying fault type = the child runs on a CORRUPTED post-fork context (stack/regs/TLS/heap), faulting on its first real instruction. = a fork-correctness bug in Darling's fork path under the libunistring build's heavy fork/exec. Same FAMILY as historical brew-sigchld-segv-rootcause / dar-gwn.1.5/.1.6 children-faulting-after-fork.

NEXT (task #76, the only open item): pin the exact faulting instruction/module with the gdb-namespace runner ([[darling-debug-runner]]: capture --tree --gdb --gdb-namespace) -- break on the child's SIGSEGV/SIGFPE and unwind to name the module+function and what produces NULL/zero. Alternatively snapshot the child via the in-server fault tracer with a backtrace at delivery. THEN fix the fork-state corruption.

STATE RESTORED: deployed dylib back to clean b66bb1b41d7b (0 trace strings, ulock-EINTR fix present); sigexc.c diagnostic REVERTED (git clean); xnu working tree carries ONLY the ulock_wait.c fix (the dar-gwn.6 regression guard). darlingserver still 53e24e16 (integration + pthread-canceled). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE PINNED (server-side tracer, run #3, 5 FATAL captures in a 38ms synchronized window). dar-gwn.6.4 is NOT a new fork-correctness bug and NOT a genuine non-deterministic child fault (the prior reframe was wrong -- it rested on an UNSYMBOLIZED rip). It is the SIGCHLD->fatal-signal MISTAG, the dar-gwn.1.5 family, surfacing as SIGFPE.

EVIDENCE (all 5 faults bsd=8 = SIGFPE):
- 4/5: code=0 (SI_USER => sent via kill/tgkill, NOT a CPU fault). si_addr=0x3e800000e7b / 0x3e800000e7a where the LOW 32 BITS = the child nsid (0xe7b=3707, 0xe7a=3706). rip resolves to TRIVIAL STUBS: libsystem_kernel+0x2b785 = inside _voucher_mach_msg_revert (a 5-byte push/mov/pop/RET stub; rip is on the ). A SIGFPE on a  is impossible as a hardware fault => these are software-delivered SIGCHLD-reap notifications mistagged as fatal SIGFPE to whatever harmless instruction each victim thread was parked on.
- 1/5: code=1 (FPE_INTDIV) in libsystem_malloc+0x14bef, si_addr==rip. BUT that child (pid3296803/nsid3707) was mid execve-STORM (repeated 'replacing process with a new task' + darling_sigexc_self x2 right before), and rip 0x15bef is MID-INSTRUCTION inside _malloc_set_zone_name (a movq $imm,mem at 0x15bee, cannot fault FPE) => stale thread_state read across the execve task-replacement, not a clean hardware divide.

CONFIRMATION: patches.yml dar-gwn.6 publication-blocker ALREADY documents this exact signature: 'a reaped-child SIGCHLD mis-delivered as a fatal signal with SI_USER, observed as SIGFPE handler=SIG_DFL, still ends the same storm'. My capture is the hard proof. So dar-gwn.6.4 == the dar-gwn.1.5/SIGCHLD-mistag family, observed as SIGFPE (signal number varies run-to-run; that was the 'non-determinism').

TOOL: pinned via a SERVER-SIDE tracer in Thread::processSignal (reads faulting rip from guest x86_thread_state64 at threadStateAddress+0x80 via process->readMemory; resolves rip->module+offset against /proc/<pid>/maps). The guest-side env-gated trace was defeated by sandbox-exec env-stripping. Server d652e75338d9 (integration 455eaea + tracer, no drift). Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
PRIMARY FAULT PINNED (capture #4, server-side primary-fault tracer with rbp backtrace, code!=0 genuine faults only). dar-gwn.6.4 == THE SAME gdtoa-OOM conftest fault as dar-gwn.1.10.

The genuine primary: bsd=11 SIGSEGV si_code=1 (SEGV_MAPERR) si_addr=0x8 (NULL+8 deref). Backtrace (server-side rbp unwind, all symbolized vs the real libsystem_c.dylib):
  #0 __s2b_D2A (libsystem_c+0x10ef1)
  #1 __dtoa     (+0xdaf7)
  #2 __gdtoa    (+0xe07d)
  #3 __vfprintf (+0x765b8)
  #4 __vfprintf (+0x75456)
  #5 _addtype   (+0x70fae)
  #6 conftest+0x3df8 (the gnulib CHECK_PRINTF_SAFE probe main)
  #7 libdyld+0xa0c5 (dyld start)
Disasm confirms __s2b_D2A: `callq ___Balloc_D2A; movl ...,0x18(%rax); movl $1,0x14(%rax)` -- Balloc returns NULL (multi-MB Bigint under the conftest setrlimit 5MB starvation) and the result is dereferenced UNCHECKED. This is IDENTICAL to the chain in darling-rpc-loss-watch.md:124-129 and is EXPECTED-by-design (happens on real macOS too; gnulib nocrash conftest).

SO THE "non-determinism" (SEGV NULL+8 vs SIGFPE INTDIV) was just which fault the OOM Bigint deref happens to produce; the 0x3e8 SIGFPE/SI_USER faults are the sigexc kill(0,signum) cascade. NOT a new fork-correctness bug.

dar-gwn.1.10 fix IS present in the build (fatal_exception_delivery bound in duct-tape/thread.c:412) and is WORKING: the faulting pid 3308748 dies CLEANLY ~3.1s after the fault (process dying@337.98 vs fault@334.86), NO wedge -- exactly the dar-gwn.1.10 GREEN signature. But the conftest dies by SEGV (terminated by uncaught signal SEGV -> ruby build.rb RC=1) INSTEAD of being caught by its own in-process nocrash_init EXC_BAD_ACCESS handler + _exit(0).

REMAINING ROOT (the real dar-gwn.6.4, distinct from .1.10): on Darling the conftest fault FALLS THROUGH to default-terminate rather than being DELIVERED to the guest task-level EXC_BAD_ACCESS handler the conftest registered (nocrash_init: mach_exception_thread + task_set_exception_ports). dar-gwn.1.10 deliberately chose terminate-over-deadlock (bounded wait), which unblocked the hang but means the in-process handler never catches it. NEXT: determine whether Darling should/can deliver the fatal EXC_BAD_ACCESS to the conftest task handler (so it _exit(0)s like macOS) vs the bounded-fallback. Server clean-restored 53e24e16; tracer reverted; env clean. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
=========================================================================
HANDOFF (2026-06-26) — dar-gwn.6.4 ROOT CAUSE PINNED. Next agent: read this first.
=========================================================================

ONE-LINE: dar-gwn.6.4 is NOT a new bug. It is the SAME gnulib CHECK_PRINTF_SAFE
gdtoa-OOM conftest SEGV as dar-gwn.1.10. The build fails because that conftest's
OWN in-process EXC_BAD_ACCESS handler (nocrash_init) does NOT catch the fault on
Darling -> the conftest dies by SEGV -> `ruby build.rb` RC=1 -> libunistring fails.

ALL PRIOR "ROOT CAUSE" FRAMINGS OF THIS BEAD WERE WRONG — superseded:
  - WRONG: "0x3e8 SEGV = dar-gwn.6 ulock regression"  (dar-gwn.6 verified PRESENT)
  - WRONG: "genuine non-deterministic forked-child fault / fork-correctness bug,
            SIGSEGV NULL+8 or SIGFPE INTDIV, NOT a mistag"  (that reframe rested on
            an UNRELIABLE guest-side rip — see METHODOLOGY trap below)
  - CORRECT (this session, hard evidence): the expected gdtoa-OOM conftest fault.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
HOW IT WAS PINNED (capture #4, the trustworthy one)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
Server-side tracer in DarlingServer::Thread::processSignal (src/thread.cpp):
gated on env DSERVER_DARGWN64, fires ONLY on genuine CPU faults (si_code != 0;
LINUX_SI_USER==0 = the cascade), reads faulting rip/rsp/rbp from the guest
x86_thread_state64 (threadStateAddress + 16*8 = rip, +7*8 = rsp, +6*8 = rbp) via
process->readMemory, does an rbp frame-pointer unwind, resolves every frame against
/proc/<host_pid>/maps. Logs under category "dargwn64" at .error() (lands at any level).

CAPTURED (genuine, si_code=1 SEGV_MAPERR, si_addr=0x8 = NULL+8):
  #0 __s2b_D2A  (libsystem_c.dylib+0x10ef1)
  #1 __dtoa     (+0xdaf7)
  #2 __gdtoa    (+0xe07d)
  #3 __vfprintf (+0x765b8)
  #4 __vfprintf (+0x75456)
  #5 _addtype   (+0x70fae)
  #6 conftest+0x3df8          <- gnulib CHECK_PRINTF_SAFE probe main()
  #7 libdyld.dylib+0xa0c5     <- dyld start
Disasm of __s2b_D2A: `callq ___Balloc_D2A; movl ...,0x18(%rax); movl $1,0x14(%rax)`
= Balloc() returns NULL (multi-MB Bigint can't fit under the conftest's setrlimit
5MB starvation) and the result is dereferenced UNCHECKED. This is gnulib's probe
"checking whether printf survives out-of-memory conditions" — DESIGNED to crash;
it crashes on real macOS too. (Symbolize vs the REAL file:
darling-prefix/libexec/darling/usr/lib/system/libsystem_c.dylib ; maps off=0x1000 so
vmaddr = 0x1000 + off.)

The "SEGV-NULL+8 vs SIGFPE-INTDIV non-determinism" earlier sessions chased = just
which fault the OOM Bigint deref happens to produce. The 0x3e8<<32|nsid SIGFPE/SI_USER
faults are the CASCADE: dtape_thread_process_signal routes si_code==SI_USER down the
EXC_SOFT_SIGNAL path, and guest sigexc.c "emulating default signal effects" does
kill(0,signum) which re-stamps si_code=0 SI_USER. Those rips land on trivial stubs
(e.g. libsystem_kernel+0x2b785 = the `retq` of _voucher_mach_msg_revert) = useless.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
WHY THE BUILD STILL FAILS (the real remaining root = task #77)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
dar-gwn.1.10's fix (fatal_exception_delivery bound, duct-tape/src/thread.c:412) IS in
the deployed server (53e24e16) and IS WORKING: the faulting conftest pid dies CLEAN
~3.1s after the fault (process dying, no wedge) = the dar-gwn.1.10 GREEN signature.
BUT .1.10 deliberately chose TERMINATE-over-deadlock: the bounded wait falls through
to default SIGSEGV-terminate instead of the conftest's own task-level EXC_BAD_ACCESS
handler (nocrash_init: mach_exception_thread + task_set_exception_ports) catching it
and calling _exit(0) like macOS. So the conftest dies by SEGV -> build.rb RC=1.

To get libunistring GREEN, Darling must DELIVER the fatal EXC_BAD_ACCESS to the guest
task handler (so the conftest catches its own fault and _exit(0)s), rather than the
bounded-fallback terminate. This OVERLAPS/EXTENDS dar-gwn.1.10 — hence the open
DECISION in task #77: same bead (re-open .1.10) or distinct follow-up? Open questions
to answer first:
  (a) does the conftest actually register task_set_exception_ports for EXC_BAD_ACCESS
      in this run (confirm via the guest, or dar-gwn110-repro/oom_printf_nocrash.c)?
  (b) WHY isn't the handler catching: bound times out before handler replies? the
      lock-deadlock .1.10 found still blocks the handler? or the exc kmsg never reaches
      the task port at all under the fork-storm?
  (c) is delivering-to-handler safe given the .1.10 deadlock (handler needs a lock the
      faulting thread holds) — i.e. does fixing delivery REQUIRE also fixing the lock
      identity / macOS task_terminate force-abort semantics?

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
METHODOLOGY TRAPS (cost real time this session — don't repeat)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
1. GUEST-SIDE env-gated traces DIE under sandbox-exec. The Homebrew build child runs
   under /usr/bin/sandbox-exec -f homebrew.sb which SCRUBS the environment, so a
   DSERVER_DARGWN64 gate read from the child's /proc/self/environ is ALWAYS off in the
   dying process. A guest-side sigexc_handler tracer fired ZERO times. USE A SERVER-SIDE
   TRACER (the server keeps its env, has the host pid, can readMemory + read maps).
   This also explains why last session's guest rip was garbage -> the "neighboring
   module / fork-corruption" conclusion was an artifact, not real.
2. DSERVER_LOG_LEVEL=debug FLOODS ~152MB and slows the guest 10-50x so brew TIMES OUT
   (EXIT=124) before reaching the crash. Use DSERVER_LOG_LEVEL=info: kprintf logs at
   Info(2) (enum Debug=1<Info=2<Warning=3<Error=4; cutoff drops type<level), so info is
   enough to capture and the guest runs near full speed (14MB, reaches crash in ~5min).
3. STALE-LOG verdict trap (now FIXED in the harness): dargwn64-capture.sh truncates the
   logs at run start and greps only this-run lines. Reusable.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
TOOLING / ARTIFACTS (ready to reuse)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
- Server-side primary-fault tracer: the diff to src/thread.cpp processSignal (reverted
  to keep the tree clean, but trivially re-appliable — see this session's transcript /
  the dargwn64 code block). Build: cd ~/work/darling-build && ninja
  src/external/darlingserver/darlingserver ; deploy -> darling-prefix/bin/darlingserver.
  Build tree is integration/homebrew @ 455eaea (has ALL sibling fixes — no drift).
- Capture harness: ~/work/dar-gwn18-etimedout-repro/dargwn64-capture.sh
  (DSERVER_DARGWN64=1 DSERVER_FAULT_TRACE=1 DSERVER_LOG_LEVEL=info; truncates logs;
  greps this-run [dargwn64]/FATAL/PRIMARY lines). Evidence: dargwn64-capture.out,
  dserver.log (capture #4 PRIMARY block).
- dar-gwn.1.10 faithful repro: ~/work/dar-gwn110-repro/run-repro*.sh + oom_printf_nocrash.c
  (nocrash_init + OOM model — exactly what configure does). Use for task #77.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
STATE (all clean, NOTHING PUSHED)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
- deployed server: 53e24e16b97b (integration + pthread-canceled + fault tracer); 0 dargwn64
- deployed dylib: clean b66bb1b41d7b (ulock-EINTR retry present; 0 dargwn64)
- darlingserver tree: only src/darlingserver.cpp modified (the intentional dar-fwl
  fault tracer, test-only, uncommitted) — the server-side dargwn64 tracer was REVERTED
- xnu tree: clean (dar-gwn.6 ulock fix is COMMITTED at 4102c49, not a working-tree mod)
- env: srv=0 mldr=0
- Tasks: #76 DONE (primary fault pinned). #77 OPEN (the fix-direction decision above).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------
SCOPE NOTE: this RE-OPENS dar-gwn.1.5/.1.10 territory
-------------------------------------------------------------------------
The memory dar-gwn15-was-dargwn6-regression marked dar-gwn.1.5 "RESOLVED as not-a-distinct-
bug (cascade of dar-gwn.6)". This session shows dar-gwn.6 is healthy yet the SIGCHLD/
SI_USER mistag cascade STILL fires (driven by sigexc.c kill(0,signum)) — but it's a
CASCADE of the gdtoa-OOM primary, not an independent fault. The actionable bug is the
gdtoa-OOM exception-delivery (task #77), which is dar-gwn.1.10's domain. Confirm with
user whether to drive this under dar-gwn.6.4, re-open dar-gwn.1.10, or a new bead.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE PINNED LIVE (repro7 + server-side dargwn64sig tracer, 2026-06-26). The
build-killer is NOT the gdtoa fault, NOT the .1.10 exception-delivery deadlock, and
NOT an uncaught conftest SEGV. All of those are EXPECTED/benign (the conftest is
SUPPOSED to crash). The build dies because the conftest's normal default-SIGSEGV
re-raise BROADCASTS SIGSEGV to its whole process group, killing the parent
bash/configure/ruby processes.

EVIDENCE (DSERVER_DARGWN64SIG=1 sigprocess trace, conftest=nsid28, parents bash 18<-17):
  #1 [P:(28)] sigprocess IN: bsd=11 linux=11 sender_pid=8  code=1 addr=0x8          <- GENUINE gdtoa NULL+8 SEGV (SEGV_MAPERR), the conftest's own fault. OUT new_bsd=11 (takes SIGSEGV, correct).
  #2 [P:(18)] sigprocess IN: bsd=11 linux=11 sender_pid=28 code=0 addr=0x3e80000001c <- SI_USER (code=0) SIGSEGV SENT BY pid 28, 0x3e8<<32|0x1c(=28). parent bash DIES.
  #3 [P:(17)] sigprocess IN: bsd=11 linux=11 sender_pid=28 code=0 addr=0x3e80000001c <- same, grandparent bash DIES.
Both parents then "process dying". build.rb -> "terminated by uncaught signal SEGV" -> RC=1.

THE BUG: sigexc.c:383, in sigexc_handler's "emulating default signal effects" branch:
    // Resend signal to self
    LINUX_SYSCALL(__NR_kill, 0, linux_signum);
kill(0, sig) does NOT mean "self" -- pid 0 = "every process in the CALLER'S PROCESS
GROUP". So when the conftest re-raises its fatal default SIGSEGV, it broadcasts
SIGSEGV to the entire process group (parent bash shells share the pgid) -> they die
with SI_USER SIGSEGV (sender=28, the 0x3e8 signature). On macOS the default-signal
re-raise terminates only the faulting process; the group is not collaterally killed.

This IS the dar-gwn.1.5 "0x3e8 SI_USER SIGSEGV-to-parent mistag" -- now exactly
mechanised: kill(0) group-broadcast, not a SIGCHLD mistag (SIGCHLD is skipped by
darling_sigexc_self). dar-gwn.6 is healthy; this is independent.

FIX: resend to the CALLING THREAD only, raise()-style:
    tgkill(getpid(), gettid(), linux_signum)   (self pid+tid, not the group)

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FIX IMPLEMENTED + MICRO-VALIDATED (RED->GREEN), NOT pushed.

FIX: xnu sigexc.c sigexc_handler "emulating default signal effects" branch.
  - BEFORE: LINUX_SYSCALL(__NR_kill, 0, linux_signum)  // comment lied: "to self"
  - AFTER:  self_pid=getpid(); self_tid=gettid();
            LINUX_SYSCALL(__NR_tgkill, self_pid, self_tid, linux_signum)
  kill(0,sig) targets the caller's whole PROCESS GROUP; tgkill(pid,tid,sig) is
  raise()-to-this-thread, so the fatal default action terminates only this
  process (macOS-faithful), never the parent shells.
  branch fix/sigexc-default-resend-self @362aafc (off integration/homebrew tip
  8886216 -- carries all integration fixes, no drift). Built libsystem_kernel.dylib
  (md5 93955773...), deployed to darling-prefix-homebrew-test/usr/lib/system.

MICRO-VALIDATION (repro7-exitcode.sh, faithful nocrash+OOM conftest, server-side
DSERVER_DARGWN64SIG sigprocess trace):
  BEFORE (RED): 3 sigprocess events -- conftest's genuine SEGV (sender=8,code=1,
    addr=0x8) PLUS two spurious SIGSEGV broadcasts to parent bash 18 & 17
    (sender_pid=28, code=0 SI_USER, addr=0x3e80000001c) -> both parents die ->
    guest session GONE, no GUEST_EXIT_RC.
  AFTER (GREEN): ONLY ONE sigprocess event -- the conftest's own genuine fault.
    The kill(0) broadcast is GONE. Parent bash SURVIVES, reports
    "Segmentation fault: 11" + GUEST_EXIT_RC=139 + GUEST_DONE. = macOS-faithful:
    the gnulib probe crashes (answer "no"), the parent continues.

NEXT: brew reinstall wget end-to-end to confirm libunistring install proceeds
(the conftest crash no longer kills configure/ruby). Then package as west patch +
integrate. dar-gwn.1.5 memory (which marked the mistag "resolved as dar-gwn.6
cascade") needs correcting: the mistag is the kill(0) group-broadcast, independent
of dar-gwn.6.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
CROSS-PLATFORM ORACLE + A/B PROOF (answers "is macOS behavior identical, does the test run on macOS too").

NEW PORTABLE ORACLE: dar-gwn110-repro/grpkill_oracle.c — pure POSIX/Darwin
(fork+setpgid+sigaction+NULL-deref+waitpid), NO Linux/Darling/mach-specific code.
Compiles + runs UNCHANGED on real macOS and on Darling, identical PASS/FAIL logic:
parent becomes pgrp leader, forks a child that crashes by genuine SIGSEGV (NULL+8),
parent installs a SIGSEGV handler ONLY to detect a COLLATERAL hit, then waitpid()s.
  PASS (exit 0) = child died by SIGSEGV ALONE, parent never signalled (parent_hit=0).
  FAIL (exit 1) = parent was collaterally SIGSEGV'd = the kill(0) group-broadcast bug.

A/B RESULTS (same binary):
  - native Linux (no Darling layer):        PASS  (kernel never group-broadcasts a per-process fault)
  - Darling UN-FIXED dylib (b66bb1b4):      FAIL  ("parent was collaterally SIGSEGV'd")  <- RED reproduces
  - Darling FIXED  dylib (93955773):        PASS  (child dies alone, parent survives)     <- GREEN
The fixed Darling verdict == native verdict == the macOS reference.

WHY THIS = macOS behavior (two independent arguments):
  1. DEFINITIONAL: a per-process fatal default signal terminates ONLY that process
     on macOS/Darwin (and any POSIX kernel). kill(0)-style group-broadcast on a
     plain fault is NOT something the macOS kernel does — it was a Darling
     emulation artifact (sigexc.c re-raise). macOS's ux_handler delivers the
     default signal to the faulting THREAD (psignal/threadsignal), which
     tgkill(getpid,gettid,sig) faithfully mirrors.
  2. EMPIRICAL GROUND TRUTH: libunistring builds fine on real macOS, where this
     exact gnulib CHECK_PRINTF_SAFE conftest crashes by design without killing
     configure. The bug only manifested on Darling. The fix restores the macOS
     outcome.

NOTE: I have NO macOS host in this env (Linux build server), so I could not run
grpkill_oracle on a real Mac myself. It is written to be run there as-is to
confirm PASS — the cheapest possible cross-check if a Mac is available.

title: libunistring make-phase stall: make all-am wedges (sh in do_wait, server idle) AFTER dar-gwn.6.4 fix lets configure+early-make pass = lost-reaping/RPC (dar-gwn.1.7/1.8 family) during BUILD
description:
# libunistring make-phase stall (downstream of dar-gwn.6.4 sigexc fix)

## Why this bead exists
The dar-gwn.6.4 fix (sigexc.c kill(0) process-group broadcast -> tgkill self-resend) let `brew reinstall wget` get PAST the gnulib CHECK_PRINTF_SAFE conftest cascade: libunistring configure ran to completion (config.log L7255->29569, reached the configure tail; 0 "uncaught signal") and `make` built ~50 .o files (lib/libunistring_la-*.o). Then `make all-am` WEDGED. This is a NEW, distinct bug unmasked by the fix -- NOT a regression of it.

## Live snapshot (the stall)
- darlingserver: IDLE (wchan ep_poll) -- no pending work.
- The make recursion `/bin/sh -c fail=; if (target_option=k; ...` parked in `do_wait` waiting on a child that never completes/reaps.
- No compile worker advancing for ~13 min (last .o at 13:19:42, still wedged at 13:32).
- Cleaned up; evidence: scratchpad/brew-stall-dserver.log (run bundle from 2026-06-26 ~13:11 brew-validate-fix.sh).

## Hypothesis / class
dar-gwn.1.7 (lost-RPC reply -> guest recvmsg blocks) / dar-gwn.1.8 (lost child-exit reaping -> parent do_wait forever) family, but during the libunistring BUILD phase rather than the conftest/signal phase. Server idle + a process parked in do_wait strongly suggests a child whose exit was never reaped (SIGCHLD/wait4 path) OR a fork RPC that never produced the child.

## What to do
1. Re-run brew reinstall wget on the dar-gwn.6.4-fixed dylib (deployed md5 93955773...), with the invariant-watchdog ([[darling-invariant-watchdog]]) + rpc-loss-watch ([[darling-rpc-loss-watch]]) running alongside to auto-surface lost-reaping vs lost-RPC-reply.
2. When it wedges, snapshot: which guest pid is in do_wait, its children (zombies? missing?), and whether any guest thread is in recvmsg with the server idle (D2 silent-stall) -> names the lost call.
3. Cross-ref dar-gwn.1.7/1.8 fixes (already deployed) -- this may be a new lost-RPC site or a rarer race in that family during make's heavy fork/wait.

## Validation gate
brew reinstall wget reaches "Pouring"/completes libunistring (and proceeds to gettext/libidn2/openssl@3/wget) with no make-phase wedge.

Nothing pushed.
close_reason: Fixed: sticky _forkChildCheckedIn flag backstops the fork-wait semaphore so a child checkin is never lost when a SIGCHLD force-aborts the parent's wait. Validated RED->GREEN on a deterministic SIGCHLD fork-storm (forkstorm2, 400/400) + plain-forkstorm regression GREEN + brew reinstall wget no longer stalls building libunistring (0 fork-checkin timeouts; make advances). Packaged: fix/fork-checkin-sticky-flag @eb7e93b, patch fork-checkin-sticky-flag.patch (sha256 fcd2d4bb), patches.yml (blocked), PR draft, west patch verify=54, integrated (darlingserver e2c7f12). Nothing pushed. A separate downstream clang SIGFPE layer remains (new bead).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE PINNED (live capture cap-run2 + code, 2026-06-26).

CAPTURED LIVE: wedged parent = innermost `make all` (nsid 8568) parked in __skb_wait_for_more_packets = guest side of fork_wait_for_child (call#11), server IDLE in ep_poll, log repeats every 30s. Parent has 6 ZOMBIE mldr children (unreaped). So NOT "fork failed to spawn" — children DID fork+run+exit; the LAST forked child died before its server checkin completed.

MECHANISM (code-confirmed):
- Guest fork (xnu .../bsd/impl/process/fork.c sys_fork): child path does __NR_fork -> __mldr_postfork_child() -> guard_table_postfork_child() -> __dserver_per_thread_socket_refresh() -> THEN dserver_rpc_checkin(true,...). Checkin is what registers the child at the server and is the ONLY thing that ups the parent's fork-wait semaphore.
- Parent path: libsystem/init.c _mach_fork_parent() -> dserver_rpc_fork_wait_for_child() -> server Process::waitForChildAfterFork() blocks on _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore (bounded 30s by dar-q95.13 fork-checkin-bound patch).
- Server checkin handler (process.cpp notifyCheckin -> line 377): the ONLY dtape_semaphore_up(parent->_dtapeForkWaitSemaphore) in the whole codebase.
- Server death handler (process.cpp notifyDead -> kernelAsync @754): destroys the dying process's OWN fork-wait semaphore but NEVER ups its PARENT's. grep confirms no other up() site.

=> THE GAP: if a forked child dies in the window between __NR_fork returning 0 and dserver_rpc_checkin completing (that window = __mldr_postfork_child + guard_table_postfork_child + socket refresh), the server never runs notifyCheckin for it, so the parent's fork-wait semaphore is never upped. The parent then waits the full 30s -> -ETIMEDOUT. Guest _mach_fork_parent (xnu libsyscall/mach/mach_init.c:147) only retries on -EINTR; on -ETIMEDOUT it hits __builtin_unreachable() (UB) -> in practice the bounded wait re-arms and the parent re-issues forever (every-30s log). Dead children are never reaped (parent never returns from fork to reach wait4) -> zombie pile-up = the dar-gwn.1.8 reaping signature, here CAUSED by the lost fork-checkin rather than a lost wait4.

This is a TRUE race/correctness bug, distinct from dar-gwn.6.4 (which is upstream of it). The fork-checkin-bound patch (dar-q95.13) only converts the permanent hang into a 30s-repeating ETIMEDOUT; it is NOT a fix.

FIX DIRECTION (two parts, both needed):
  (1) Server-side: on process death, if the dying process has a registered fork parent whose fork-wait is outstanding, up the parent's _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore (with an error/child-died result) so fork_wait_for_child returns promptly instead of 30s-timing-out.
  (2) Guest-side: _mach_fork_parent must handle a non-EINTR error (esp. -ETIMEDOUT / child-died) instead of __builtin_unreachable() — propagate it as a fork failure (or reap + signal) rather than looping forever.
  STILL OPEN: pin the deeper question of WHY these specific make children die pre-checkin (fault in the postfork window? a fatal signal? fast exit racing checkin processing?). Next: gated server-side trace in notifyCheckin/notifyDead/waitForChildAfterFork (env-gated .error(), like the dar-gwn.6.4 sigprocess trace) to log child<->parent nsid + which case fired.

Evidence: /home/ilyagulya/work/dar-gwn65-forkcheckin/cap-run2/snapshot.txt (3 snapshots, 6 zombies, parent in skb_wait, server ep_poll). Capture harness: dar-gwn65-forkcheckin/capture-forkstall.sh.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE CORRECTED (cap-run3, dargwn65 server trace, 2026-06-26). My first comment was WRONG about "child dies before checkin" — the trace DISPROVES it.

TRACE FACTS (parent = `make all` nsid 8569, the wedged process):
- 136 CHECKINs to parent 8569 — i.e. 136 forked children ALL successfully checked in (each did dtape_semaphore_up on the parent's fork-wait sema). This INCLUDES all 5 surviving zombies (3464455/8721, 3464467/8733, 3464472/8738, 3464473/8739, 3464474/8740 — each has a CHECKIN line, verified).
- 134 of those children then DIED (DEATH ... parent=8569). The death path does NOT touch the parent's sema (as designed).
- Only 2 FORKWAIT-TIMEOUTs total for 8569 (the 30s bound firing).
- The CHECKIN timeline shows children 8570/8571/8572/8574 checking in back-to-back at ~6-7ms intervals with NO death of the prior child in between, plus grandchildren (8721 forks 8724, etc). make's fork->fork_wait_for_child(down) is sequential PER FORK, but each fork+checkin cycle is only ~6ms, so checkins stream in very fast.

So the bug is NOT a lost checkin. It is a SEMAPHORE-ACCOUNTING DESYNC on the SINGLE shared per-process fork-wait semaphore under CONCURRENT forks:

- darlingserver gives each Process ONE _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore (process.cpp:87). The protocol is: each guest fork() -> parent calls dserver_rpc_fork_wait_for_child -> server waitForChildAfterFork() does ONE down(); each child checkin does ONE up() (process.cpp:377, notifyCheckin). It assumes strict 1:1 down/up pairing.
- The up()/down() pair is NOT correlated to a specific child: a down() consumes WHICHEVER up() is in the semaphore, not "its own" child's checkin. With strict lockstep (fork; down; child ups; reap; repeat) this is harmless. But a fast child can complete its checkin (up()) BEFORE the parent's dserver_rpc_fork_wait_for_child RPC even reaches the server's down(). That up() is then BANKED (count goes to 1). The NEXT fork's down() consumes that banked (wrong child's) up() IMMEDIATELY without waiting — shifting the whole chain by one. Every subsequent fork keeps consuming the previous child's banked up(), so the LAST fork's down() finds the semaphore empty (its own child's up() was eaten by the previous waiter) and blocks → the dar-q95.13 30s bound fires (2 FORKWAIT-TIMEOUTs seen) → returns without consuming → guest re-issues → blocks forever (every-30s FORKWAIT-TIMEOUT loop).
- The 5 surviving zombies are children whose up()s were "spent" on the wrong (earlier) waiter; the make thread is stuck on a fork whose child already checked in and died, so it never returns from fork() to reach wait4 → those children stay unreaped.

NET: a single counting semaphore cannot correctly synchronize a stream of fork operations because up()s are FUNGIBLE — there is no binding between a checkin and the specific fork_wait that should consume it. A checkin that races ahead of its matching wait banks a count that desyncs all following pairs. The 30s bound converts the latent desync into a permanent stuck waiter + unreaped zombies (the dar-gwn.1.8 reaping signature here is a CONSEQUENCE, not the cause).

This is distinct from and downstream of dar-gwn.6.4. It is a genuine darlingserver fork-synchronization correctness bug, exposed by parallel make.

FIX DIRECTION (revised): make the fork-wait per-fork-operation, not a single shared counting semaphore. Options:
  (A) Per-child correlation: the child's checkin must wake the SPECIFIC waiter for THAT child (e.g. key the wait on the child nsid/pid, or a per-pending-fork semaphore/condition). The parent learns the Linux child pid from fork() and can pass it in fork_wait_for_child so the server waits for exactly that child.
  (B) Per-fork semaphore: allocate a fresh fork-wait semaphore per outstanding fork (parent thread) rather than one per process.
  (C) At minimum, the death of a checked-in-then-dead child and the timeout path must not leak/steal counts.
Plus the guest-side _mach_fork_parent must stop using __builtin_unreachable() on non-EINTR (it currently UB-loops on -ETIMEDOUT).

RECOMMENDED FIX (per-child correlation): bind each fork_wait to the specific child. The child's checkin should wake ONLY the waiter expecting THAT child (not bank a fungible count). Cleanest: replace the single counting semaphore with a per-pending-fork wait keyed on the child nsid/pid, OR have the parent's fork_wait_for_child record "I am waiting for the child I just forked" and the child's notifyCheckin satisfy exactly that waiter. notifyCheckin already locates the parent via /proc PPid, so the parent<->child binding exists; the wakeup just needs to be 1:1 instead of a shared count. Plus the guest _mach_fork_parent must handle -ETIMEDOUT/non-EINTR instead of __builtin_unreachable().

Evidence: cap-run3/snapshot.txt + dserver.log dargwn65 trace (136 up / 134 death / 2 timeout for nsid 8569; children 8570/71/72/74 stream in at ~6ms with no interleaved death). Trace built into deployed diagnostic server md5 6239eb6d (DSERVER_DARGWN65=1 gated, in process.cpp notifyCheckin/notifyDead/waitForChildAfterFork).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: CONCURRENCY CONFIRMED (the decisive fact for the fix). Children of make-all 8569 check in in this order: 8570,8571,8572,8574,8576 ALL born (CHECKIN) BEFORE the first DEATH (8570). => 5+ direct children of 8569 are ALIVE SIMULTANEOUSLY. So make-all is PARALLEL make (make -j): multiple fork()s outstanding at once, hence multiple concurrent down()s on the ONE shared _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore, and up()s arriving out-of-order. This is exactly the condition under which a single fungible counting semaphore cannot correctly pair checkins to fork-waits. Combined with the dar-q95.13 30s bound (which returns a down WITHOUT consuming on timeout), the count permanently desyncs and one waiter starves forever (the 2 FORKWAIT-TIMEOUTs -> every-30s loop). ROOT CAUSE FULLY PINNED. The fix must give each outstanding fork its OWN wait correlated to its specific child (per-child, not a shared count). Not yet implemented; nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
MICRO-REPRO STATUS (2026-06-26): a clean parallel fork-storm does NOT trip it. forkstorm.c (fork BATCH children alive-at-once then reap; tried plain _exit, busy-spin, and fork+execv /usr/bin/true; BATCH up to 32, ROUNDS 300 = 4800+ fork[/exec] cycles) ran fully GREEN every time on the diagnostic server (6239eb6d) — ALL ROUNDS DONE, zero fork-checkin timeouts. So generic balanced fork/exec concurrency is NOT sufficient to desync the per-process counting semaphore.

REFINED READING of the cap-run3 trace tail: at the moment make-all 8569 wedges, ALL its children (the 5 zombies 8721/8733/8738/8739/8740 and the ~131 others) have ALREADY checked in (up) AND died. 136 ups total, parent still stuck. There were 2 EARLIER FORKWAIT-TIMEOUTs for 8569 before the final permanent one. So the leak is cumulative: each 30s-bounded timeout returns a down WITHOUT consuming, while a child's up was banked → the count drifts by the number of timeouts; after enough drift the last real fork starves permanently. The FIRST timeout's trigger is the missing ingredient my repro doesn't recreate — likely needs (a) a child that is SLOW to check in (so the parent's down genuinely waits ~30s while OTHER children's ups are consumed by it out of order), or (b) server back-pressure delaying the checkin RPC under the real make's heavier per-child load (dyld of real cc/cc1/as, vchroot, sandbox), or (c) a child hitting the exec/pending-replacement path AND a fault. /usr/bin/true exec was too light.

DECISION POINT: the structural bug is already PROVEN from code+trace (single fungible _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore per process for all forks + notifyCheckin is the only up() + notifyDead never ups the parent). A bit-perfect deterministic micro-repro is proving expensive to tune blind. Options going forward: (1) implement the per-child-correlation fix now and validate RED->GREEN against the REAL brew libunistring path (which reproduces 100%), accepting brew as the oracle; (2) keep tuning the micro-repro (make children slow-to-checkin / heavier exec / inject a child fault). Leaning (1) since brew is a reliable RED oracle and the micro-repro is a nicety, not a gate. Awaiting steer.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
ROOT CAUSE — FINAL, PROVEN (cap-inflight run, FORKWAIT-ENTER/EXIT in_flight trace, 2026-06-26). Supersedes the "concurrent fungible semaphore" theory, which the in_flight data DISPROVES.

DECISIVE TRACE FACTS (wedged parent THIS run = make-all nsid 8568 pid 3521324):
- in_flight NEVER exceeds 1. Fork-waits are STRICTLY SEQUENTIAL per process — there are NO concurrent down()s. So the bug is NOT a fungible counting semaphore under concurrent forks.
- EXIT results for 8568: 107 ok, 5 result=intr (KERN_ABORTED / interrupted), 3 result=TIMEOUT.
- The wedge sequence (all in_flight=1):
    ENTER .675673
    CHECKIN child 8711 -> 8568 (up) .676068
    EXIT result=intr .676118            <- down INTERRUPTED right as an up arrived
    ENTER .676591 / EXIT intr .680160
    ENTER .680557 / EXIT intr .684475
    ENTER .684803 / EXIT intr .750715
    ENTER .751536 / EXIT intr .855719   (DEATHs of 8685/8686/8703/8710/8711 interleaved)
    ENTER .857237
    [30s] EXIT result=TIMEOUT .857826   <- final down starves, no up left to consume

MECHANISM (interrupted-wait lost-wakeup):
- fork_wait_for_child is declared ALLOW_INTERRUPTIONS (generate-rpc-wrappers.py:163). The server's down() = dtape_semaphore_down_timeout -> XNU semaphore_timedwait, which returns KERN_ABORTED when the microthread's wait is interrupted by a signal.
- The interrupting signal is SIGCHLD: the make parent is forking children that DIE rapidly, and each child death raises SIGCHLD on the parent, which aborts its in-progress fork_wait_for_child wait. (This is exactly the signal pressure my clean fork-storm micro-repro LACKED — a fork-storm whose children _exit/exec cleanly without flooding the parent with SIGCHLD during the wait did NOT reproduce; the real make case has heavy SIGCHLD because children die while siblings are still being forked/waited.)
- The lost wakeup: when a child checks in (semaphore_signal = up) at almost the same instant the parent's down is aborted by SIGCHLD (KERN_ABORTED), the up is consumed/handed-off by XNU's semaphore to the waiter that is simultaneously aborting -> the waiter returns KERN_ABORTED (intr) WITHOUT delivering the count to the guest, and the up is GONE. The guest sees -EINTR, loops (goto retry in _mach_fork_parent), and re-issues a fresh down — but that child's up has already been eaten by the aborted wait. Each such race permanently loses one up. After the count is depleted, the next genuine fork's down has nothing to consume -> 30s TIMEOUT -> guest re-issues -> TIMEOUT forever (the every-30s loop). The 7 zombies are children whose ups were lost to interruption; the make thread is stuck and never returns from fork() to reap them.

WHY my micro-repro stayed GREEN: it created the concurrency but NOT the signal-vs-up race. The trigger REQUIRES SIGCHLD (or other signals) interrupting the parent's fork-wait at the same moment a child checks in. A correct micro-repro must make children die (SIGCHLD) WHILE the parent is parked in fork_wait_for_child for a DIFFERENT child.

THE BUG, precisely: an interruptible semaphore wait (fork_wait_for_child) loses the checkin up() when the wait is aborted by a signal concurrent with the signal_signal. = a lost-wakeup on KERN_ABORTED. This is in the dar-gwn.1.7/.1.8 lost-RPC/reaping family, mechanised exactly.

FIX OPTIONS (revised, now that we know it is interruption-driven, not concurrency-driven):
  (A) Make the fork-wait NOT lose the up on interruption: on KERN_ABORTED, re-check/retry the down inside the server (loop on interrupt) instead of returning -EINTR to the guest, so a banked up is never abandoned. Simplest, server-only.
  (B) Make fork_wait_for_child NON-interruptible (drop ALLOW_INTERRUPTIONS) — the checkin is a fast internal handshake; it should not be abortable by the guest's own SIGCHLD. But this may interact with legit interruption needs.
  (C) Per-child correlation (heavier) — also fixes it but is overkill given the bug is a lost-wakeup, not a mismatched-pairing.
  Plus: guest _mach_fork_parent must handle -ETIMEDOUT/non-EINTR instead of __builtin_unreachable().
  LEANING (A): on interrupt, the server loops the down (optionally re-checking for a checked-in child) so the up is never lost — preserves interruptibility semantics for the guest while not dropping the count.

NEXT: confirm in code whether KERN_ABORTED can race a semaphore_signal and lose it (read XNU semaphore_wait/_signal interrupt path), then implement (A) and validate RED->GREEN on brew (100% oracle). Build a SIGCHLD-driven micro-repro as the deterministic gate.
Evidence: cap-inflight (dserver.log epoch>1782485020; 8568: 107 ok / 5 intr / 3 timeout; in_flight<=1 always). Diagnostic server md5 0cb4aacf (FORKWAIT-ENTER/EXIT + in_flight, DSERVER_DARGWN65=1).

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
MECHANISM CONFIRMED + DETERMINISTIC RED GATE (2026-06-26). forkstorm2.c (parent installs a SIGCHLD handler WITHOUT SA_RESTART, then fork-storms children that exit at staggered times) reproduced the fork-checkin 30s timeout on ROUND 0 — instant RED. The plain forkstorm.c (no SIGCHLD handler, 4800+ fork+exec) stayed GREEN. => the trigger is exactly SIGCHLD (delivered, non-restarting) interrupting the parent's fork_wait_for_child wait, racing a child checkin up() and losing it. This nails the v3-FINAL root cause empirically and ties it to the dar-gwn.1.5/dar-gwn.6 SA_RESTART/SIGCHLD lineage.

CODE CONFIRMATION: server down() = dtape_semaphore_down_timeout -> semaphore_timedwait, which blocks via waitq_assert_wait64_locked with THREAD_ABORTSAFE (duct-tape sync_sema.c:707) -> a signal aborts it as THREAD_INTERRUPTED -> KERN_ABORTED -> dtape_semaphore_wait_result_interrupted -> ForkWaitForChild replies, guest _mach_fork_parent sees -EINTR and re-issues. The up()/abort race in semaphore_signal_internal (count<0 wakeup-one fails because waiter is aborting -> count reset to 0; PREPOST re-bank is fragile across the abort) loses the checkin. fork_wait_for_child is ALLOW_INTERRUPTIONS (generate-rpc-wrappers.py:163), which is what makes the wait abortable by the guest's own SIGCHLD.

RED GATE: DSERVER_DARGWN65=1 SRC=forkstorm2.c BATCH=12 ROUNDS=400 run-forkstorm.sh -> RED in ~30s (round 0). This is the TDD oracle for the fix.

FIX (chosen): in Process::waitForChildAfterFork, LOOP the down on the interrupted result instead of returning false — re-issue dtape_semaphore_down_timeout on dtape_semaphore_wait_result_interrupted (preserving an overall deadline) so an aborted wait re-checks the semaphore and consumes the banked checkin up() rather than abandoning it. Keep the 30s overall bound for the genuine never-checks-in case. This is server-only, minimal, and does not change the wire protocol or guest. Implementing now; will validate RED->GREEN on forkstorm2 then on brew.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FIX ATTEMPT 1 (in-handler re-arm loop) FAILED — server PANIC. Looping dtape_semaphore_down_timeout inside waitForChildAfterFork on the interrupted result panics the duct-tape: 'semaphore_block' @ sync_sema.c:635 (the default case of semaphore_convert_wait_result = an unexpected wait_result from thread_handoff_deallocate on rapid re-block within the SAME microthread invocation). The existing per-RPC retry (guest re-issues fork_wait_for_child after -EINTR) is safe because the microthread fully unwinds to the event loop between waits; an in-handler tight re-block is NOT safe in this duct-tape. Restored clean server d0ad1ff1.

REVISED FIX PLAN: do not re-block in-handler. Instead eliminate the interruption at the source — the server-internal fork-checkin handshake should not be abortable by the guest's own SIGCHLD. The wait uses THREAD_ABORTSAFE (sync_sema.c:707, shared semaphore_wait_internal). Make the FORK-WAIT specifically uninterruptible without touching the shared psynch/other semaphore waits: add a duct-tape uninterruptible timed-down (THREAD_UNINT variant) and use it ONLY for _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore. This keeps the 30s bound for the genuine never-checkin case while making SIGCHLD unable to abort+lose the checkin up. RED gate unchanged (forkstorm2 round0). Implementing Option B.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
LOST-UP PINNED EXACTLY (count trace, fs-count1, wedged parent nsid 30 sem 0x5855fce4fd40):

  .012046 CHECKIN-UP child 34 -> parent 30   count -1->-1   (up arrives; waiter parked at count=-1; semaphore_signal HANDS OFF to the waiter, count stays -1)
  .012104 FORKWAIT-DOWN parent 30            count_before=0 count_after=-1 result=intr   (SAME waiter returns KERN_ABORTED, discarding the handed-off wakeup; count left at -1)
  .014864 FORKWAIT-DOWN parent 30            count_before=-1 count_after=-1 result=intr   (re-issued down sees a STUCK count=-1 = phantom waiter; intr again)
  .015335 FORKWAIT-DOWN parent 30            count_before=-1 count_after=-1 result=TIMEOUT

EXACT MECHANISM: when a child checkin (semaphore_signal/up) hands the wakeup off (THREAD_HANDOFF / SEMAPHORE_THREAD_HANDOFF) to a fork-wait waiter that is SIMULTANEOUSLY being aborted by a SIGCHLD (THREAD_INTERRUPTED -> KERN_ABORTED), the waiter returns 'interrupted' and THROWS AWAY the wakeup WITHOUT restoring the semaphore count. Net: (1) the checkin up is LOST, and (2) count is left STUCK at -1 (a phantom parked waiter that no longer exists). Every subsequent fork_wait_for_child down on this semaphore now starts from count=-1 and can never reach 0 from a single up -> spurious 30s TIMEOUT -> permanent hang + unreaped zombies. The -1 stuck state also explains why MORE than one up is effectively lost.

This is a genuine handoff-vs-abort race in the duct-tape XNU semaphore (sync_sema.c semaphore_wait_internal uses THREAD_ABORTSAFE @707 and SEMAPHORE_THREAD_HANDOFF @712; the abort path does not undo the handoff/count). 

=> The interruptibility IS the root trigger (confirmed empirically: forkstorm2 with SIGCHLD = RED; without = GREEN; and the count trace shows the abort eating the handoff). FIX = make the fork-wait UNINTERRUPTIBLE so the abort can never race the handoff. Implementing a THREAD_UNINT timed-down used ONLY for the fork-wait semaphore (not the shared psynch waits). Count diag server md5 to be replaced. RED gate: forkstorm2 round0.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FIX ATTEMPT 2 (THREAD_UNINT fork-wait) FAILED — interruption STILL happens. Even with dtape_semaphore_down_timeout_uninterruptible (waitq THREAD_UNINT), the count trace shows result=intr still fires (count_before=0 count_after=-1 result=intr, identical to before). ROOT: the duct-tape SIGNAL-DELIVERY path bulldozes uninterruptible waits. dtape_thread_sigexc_enter (duct-tape/src/thread.c:501) does: state &= ~(TH_UNINT|TH_WAIT); wait_result=THREAD_INTERRUPTED; clear_wait_internal(THREAD_INTERRUPTED). So when SIGCHLD is delivered to the parent microthread, the server FORCE-clears whatever it's waiting on, regardless of THREAD_UNINT. (This is the same sigexc lineage as dar-gwn.1.5/dar-gwn.6.) So 'make the wait uninterruptible' cannot work at the waitq level — the sigexc handler overrides it.

CONCRETE CORRUPTION (confirmed by count trace): after the aborted wait, the fork-wait semaphore is left STUCK at count=-1 (a phantom waiter that no longer exists). The single-int XNU semaphore count cannot represent the post-abort state, and the guest's retry adds yet another waiter on a -1 count -> never converges -> 30s timeout.

EARLIER FIX ATTEMPT 1 (loop-retry in waitForChildAfterFork) panicked ('semaphore_block' / unexpected wait_result on in-microthread re-block). So neither (A) in-handler loop nor (B) THREAD_UNINT works.

REVISED FIX DIRECTION (needs a real mechanism change, not a flag):
  Option D: don't use a counting semaphore for fork-checkin at all. Replace _dtapeForkWaitSemaphore with an explicit per-process boolean 'child_checked_in' flag + a condvar (or re-derive readiness): notifyCheckin sets the flag + signals; waitForChildAfterFork loops until the flag is set OR the 30s deadline, re-waiting across sigexc aborts WITHOUT relying on a fragile counting semaphore. The flag is sticky so a checkin that races an abort is never lost.
  Option E: on interrupt in waitForChildAfterFork, RESET the semaphore to a known state (drain/clear the phantom -1) and re-check via the boolean before re-waiting.
  Both must survive the sigexc clear_wait. Leaning Option D (sticky flag + condvar), since the bug is fundamentally 'a counting semaphore loses an edge across a forced clear_wait'.

This is a larger darlingserver change than a flag flip. Restored clean server d0ad1ff1; nothing pushed. RED gate ready (forkstorm2 round0). Pausing to confirm direction with user before the rewrite.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FIX 3 (STICKY FLAG) = GREEN. Mechanism: notifyCheckin sets _forkChildCheckedIn=true (sticky atomic) BEFORE up()ing the fork-wait semaphore; waitForChildAfterFork checks the flag on entry (fast path, consume+return), and on interrupt/timeout re-checks the flag before failing. The semaphore stays the fast wakeup; the sticky flag is the lost-edge backstop so a checkin whose semaphore wakeup is destroyed by the sigexc-forced clear_wait is never lost (the guest's per-RPC retry observes the flag). No in-handler re-block (avoids the attempt-1 panic); does not rely on uninterruptibility (which attempt-2 proved the sigexc path ignores). Flag reset on exec (notifyCheckin replacement branch) and after each consume.

VALIDATION RED->GREEN on the deterministic gate forkstorm2 (SIGCHLD-no-SA_RESTART fork storm):
  UNFIXED server: RED on round 0 (~30s, fork-checkin timeout).
  STICKY-FLAG server (md5 to record): GREEN — ALL 400 rounds done, 4729 SIGCHLDs delivered, 0 fork-checkin timeouts, no panic.

Files: darlingserver/src/process.cpp (notifyCheckin set flag; waitForChildAfterFork sticky check; exec-branch reset), internal-include/darlingserver/process.hpp (std::atomic<bool> _forkChildCheckedIn). NEXT: regression-check plain forkstorm.c (no SIGCHLD) stays GREEN; strip the now-unused UNINT option + dtape_semaphore_down_timeout_uninterruptible + peek_count diagnostics from sync_sema.c/semaphore.c/duct-tape.h; validate on REAL brew reinstall wget (100% RED oracle); then package as a west patch. Nothing pushed.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text:
FIX VALIDATED ON REAL BREW (clean sticky-flag server fb027685, brew reinstall wget). The dar-gwn.6.5 make-phase fork-checkin stall is GONE:
  - 0 'fork child checkin' timeouts (was the signature; this run epoch>1782519080).
  - 0 Dropping-message, 0 waitq panics, 0 ETIMEDOUT.
  - libunistring make ADVANCED far past the former wedge: compiled amemxfrm.c/c-ctype.c/c-strcasecmp.c/c32isalnum.c/c32isalpha.c... (no fork hang, no zombie pileup).
Combined with the deterministic gate: forkstorm2 RED(round0, unfixed) -> GREEN(400/400, fixed) + plain-forkstorm regression stays GREEN. CONCLUSION: dar-gwn.6.5 FIXED.

NEW DOWNSTREAM LAYER (separate bug, NOT a regression, NOT dar-gwn.6.5): libunistring then died with  -> 'Floating point exception: 8 (core dumped)' building the config.h regeneration step -> make Error. This is a guest-side clang SIGFPE (the dserver-fault.log was stale 12:16, so not a server fault), likely the dar-gwn.6 sigexc-in-malloc SIGILL/FPE family or another conftest-style probe. Needs its own bead/layer under dar-gwn.6.2. dar-gwn.6.5's scope (the fork-checkin lost-up stall) is COMPLETE.

Fix = darlingserver process.cpp + process.hpp only (sticky _forkChildCheckedIn flag; set in notifyCheckin before up(), checked in waitForChildAfterFork fast-path + interrupt + timeout branches, reset on consume and on exec). duct-tape sync_sema.c/semaphore.c/duct-tape.h reverted to pristine (the UNINT/peek_count diagnostics removed). NEXT: package as west patch + patches.yml + PR draft + west patch verify. Nothing pushed.

Patch darlingserver/fork-checkin-sticky-flag.patch (sha256 fcd2d4bb3422950ade6fc244472af789f4b16ec05eceeebfc34bf93c6b2f09cf), patches.yml entry (publication-status blocked, blocker = review the sticky-flag vs a broader duct-tape sigexc-clear_wait/semaphore-handoff fix), PR draft pr-drafts/darlingserver-fork-checkin-sticky-flag.md. west patch verify = 54 (was 53). title: libunistring config.h regen: clang SIGFPE (core dumped) -- downstream layer after dar-gwn.6.5 fork-checkin fix
description:
ROOT-CAUSED to the fault site; mechanism CORRECTED. NOT fixed, NOT pushed.

SYMPTOM: under brew reinstall wget -> libunistring `make -j12` (config.h regen rule, and
ANY parallel build phase), a random short-lived guest process dies:
  'Floating point exception: 8 (core dumped)' -> make config.h Error 1 -> libunistring fails.
PROCESS-AGNOSTIC: observed killing clang, rm, the `echo|sed` subshell, sh ./exported.sh,
and a bare `/usr/bin/true` -- so it is NOT specific to clang or to any one binary.

THE FAULT (firmly pinned by live server trace + disasm): SIGFPE, si_code=1 (FPE_INTDIV,
integer divide-by-zero), at libsystem_malloc.dylib + 0x14bef = the `divb %dl` inside
__malloc_initialize (it computes hyper_shift = logical_ncpus / phys_ncpus from the
commpage CPU-count bytes). __malloc_initialize runs once at startup in EVERY process, so
every freshly-exec'd build tool is exposed. Rate ~1 FPE per 6-24k execs under the
fork/exec + signal storm.

THE REAL MECHANISM: NOT a commpage data bug. It is GUEST THREAD-STATE (GP register)
CORRUPTION on signal resumption -- the dar-gwn.6 sigexc / thread-state save-restore
family. At the fault the guest GP register file is ZEROED (rax=rdx=0) while RIP correctly
points at the divb, so `divb %dl` divides by dl=0. NOT commpage, NOT libmalloc, NOT a
SIGFPE-mistag (signal mapping is 1:1 and si_code=1 kernel-generated, not SI_USER), NOT a
dar-gwn.6/6.4 regression (both fixes verified present in the deployed dylib + xnu tree).
design:
FIX DIRECTION: stop the guest from being resumed with a zeroed GP register file after a
signal under load. The corruption is in the sigexc / thread-state save-restore path
(guest sigexc.c state_to_kernel/state_from_kernel + server Thread::processSignal
load/saveStateFromUser + the S2C/SIGRT interrupt_enter/exit nesting). Pin the exact site
with the instrumentation in NEXT STEPS, then fix so the resumed ucontext always carries
the real GP registers. Validate with the fpestorm A/B RED->GREEN gate (>=24000 execs),
then brew e2e, then package as a west patch. Acceptance: fpestorm GREEN at 2x the rounds
the unfixed baseline needed, AND brew reinstall wget builds past libunistring's config.h.
notes: VALIDATED (NOT pushed). Second independent GREEN: fpestorm fix-sanity = VERDICT=GREEN, FPE=0/othersig=0/12000-of-12000 ok, landfail=0, guest finished cleanly. Combined with the 25224-exec GREEN = 37k+ execs ZERO SIGFPE on fixed mldr 5f01b70f, vs baseline reliably RED at ~6k. Integrated (west patch verify 55). e2e make-loop caveat: interactive 'darling shell -c <loop>' hits shellspawn Broken-pipe + orphan-mldr leak after ~2 heavy iters (harness limit, NOT a guest crash); dserver.log only had STALE pre-fix FPE (last epoch 1782526915, hrs before the fixed run, zero new). fpestorm A/B is the authoritative gate. Nothing pushed.
close_reason: FIXED: mldr commpage_setup mapped the commpage without MAP_FIXED -> under ASLR it intermittently relocated and libmalloc read a zero-filled canonical page -> divb-by-0 SIGFPE. Fix = MAP_FIXED (fix/commpage-map-fixed @2ed457f76, patch commpage-map-fixed.patch sha256 d619aa57, integrated, west patch verify 55). Proven live (COMMPAGE MISLANDED log immediately precedes the FPE, 1:1) and A/B GREEN 0 FPE over 37k+ execs. Deployed mldr 5f01b70f. NOT pushed.

title: WGET-RESIDUAL-FREEZE: canceled-storm all-parked freeze (~1/10 wget reinstalls, pre-existing on 886d13af)
description: Session tasklist #117. ~1 in 10 brew wget reinstalls freeze; deterministic fingerprint: a ~57.4k pthread_canceled storm (57398-57400 across binaries) after an interrupt_enter, then an all-parked guest + a semaphore_timedwait that never times out (timer-loss suspicion) + unreaped zombies; server idle. PRE-EXISTING on baseline 886d13af (measured on BOTH baseline and A0-ARCH stage-1 in an 11-run A/B) -- NOT an A0-ARCH regression. Walker is much stronger now (post stage-2/3 tape + frame decode) so this is far more diagnosable than when first seen. STRONGLY RELATED to dar-rpc-correctness-z27x.7 (flood dispatch-starvation, same 1Hz long-term-timer / semaphore-timedwait-never-fires shape) and .3 (unified deadline scheduler) -- may be the same root or subsumed once .3 lands; confirm with the walker before separately fixing. Detail: memory dar-a0arch-stage1-typed-wake-tokens (WGET-RESIDUAL-FREEZE note).

title: xnu fchownat/lchown stubs return success for NULL paths
description:
Observed while migrating xnu/vchroot-pathnull-guard to guest runtime coverage: real Darling guest calls lchown(NULL, 0, 0) and fchownat(AT_FDCWD, NULL, ...) return 0 with errno=0, not -1/EFAULT. Root cause in current source: sys_fchownat() and sys_lchown() keep the real implementation behind #if 0 and the active #else returns 0 unconditionally, so the NULL path guards added by vchroot-pathnull-guard live in dead code. This means the old compile/root-cause contract overstated runtime coverage for these two disabled stubs and public chown-like APIs have incorrect success semantics.

Repros: (1) tests/vchroot_pathnull_guard_guest.c with lchown(NULL) expectation produced bundle 20260708T164736Z-west-test-vchroot_pathnull_guard_guest: 'lchown: rc=0 errno=0, expected EFAULT'. (2) west test --profile homebrew --patch xnu/vchroot-pathnull-guard.patch --red-only --prove-red failed GREEN with 'sys_fchownat returned 0, expected -EFAULT'.

Acceptance: decide correct disabled-stub policy for fchownat/lchown (invalid NULL path must not report success; likely return EFAULT before any disabled-success fallback), implement behavior with RED guest/compile tests, update vchroot-pathnull coverage so disabled-code guards are not counted as runtime proof, and keep E-UNION template-safety concern documented if ownership mutation remains otherwise disabled.
close_reason: Completed: source/patcheSet fixed with xnu/chown-disabled-null-guard.patch; disabled chown stubs now return ENOTSUP instead of false success and NULL path returns EFAULT; RED->GREEN compile behavior contract proves old false success and new behavior; patch export/checks pass.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: 2026-07-08: fixed as xnu/chown-disabled-null-guard.patch on fix/chown-disabled-null-guard (commit b9e995a). The fix covers the whole disabled ownership-stub class, not just NULL: fchown/chown/fchownat/lchown no longer report success when mutation is disabled; disabled non-NULL operations return ENOTSUP, and NULL path operations return EFAULT before vchroot/Linux fallback. Added tests/chown_disabled_null_guard_contract.sh as a compile behavior RED->GREEN proof; source-base returns 0 for all cases, current tree passes. Patch export --check, west patch check, metadata/prefix cleanup contracts pass. Live guest validation needs a rebuilt/deployed libsystem_kernel, so the runtime suite should pick this up after deploy; the source-level bug and false coverage issue are fixed here.

title: Focused shellspawn diagnostics need narrow deploy path, not full bootchain build
description: While investigating dar-9f8b on 2026-07-08, west darling-build --deploy-launcher --deploy-bootchain tried to build broad bootchain/closure targets and failed before deploy in unrelated objc4 debug macro build path. For shellspawn/launcher diagnostics we need a narrow deploy path that builds/copies only src/startup/darling and src/shellspawn/shellspawn, avoiding unrelated broad-build instability. Add --deploy-shellspawn and keep runtime validation focused.

comments.author: ilyagulya
comments.text: Narrow deploy flag alone was insufficient: even west darling-build --deploy-closure-names --no-deploy-dyld --deploy-launcher --deploy-shellspawn built only 2 requested targets but ninja still pulled objc4/runtime/objc-runtime.mm.o and failed with #error mismatch in debug-ness macros. Need either fix the underlying objc4 debug macro config or add a truly isolated host-tool rebuild/deploy path for startup/darling + shellspawn diagnostics.

title: Extract darling-debug-runner into standalone private project

title: west test: runtime deploy build output can wedge interactive sessions

No darling/timeout/west child process was visible in ps; Ctrl-C returned and cleanup unmounted the prefix proc mount. Immediate mitigation: capture CMake/Ninja output and print only a tail on failure. Follow-up: add a regression/contract for quiet runtime build output and verify interrupted guest-runtime-deploy removes source/build/deploy/cache temp dirs and worktrees.

comments.author: ilyagulya

Added a Python process-group watchdog around guest-command-fixture and narrowed the DCC valid-cache fixture to smoke2-list.txt so the behavioral reader/substitution gate is not coupled to full 40-dylib cache output/latency.

comments.author: ilyagulya

Metadata supports expect.returncode: timeout for intentional hard-fail/abort cases with required output markers.

Added contract coverage in tests/run-west-test-runtime-red-contract.sh for quiet success output and 200-line failure tail, including preserving stdout/stderr separation.
